tags.name: norm:mseccfg_rlb_mmwp_mml_presence
tags.text: The Smepmp extension adds the RLB, MMWP, and the MML fields in mseccfg.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_rlb_mmwp_mml_presence
tags.text: The Smepmp extension adds the RLB, MMWP, and the MML fields in mseccfg.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_rlb_mmwp_mml_presence
tags.text: The Smepmp extension adds the RLB, MMWP, and the MML fields in mseccfg.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_rlb_op_warl
tags.text: When mseccfg.RLB (Rule Locking Bypass) a WARL field that provides a mechanism to temporarily modify Locked PMP rules. When mseccfg.RLB is 1, locked PMP rules may be removed or modified and locked PMP rules may be edited. When mseccfg.RLB is 0 and pmpcfg.L is 1 in any rule or entry (including disabled entries), then mseccfg.RLB remains 0 and any further modifications to mseccfg.RLB are ignored until a PMP reset.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_rlb_op_warl
tags.text: When mseccfg.RLB (Rule Locking Bypass) a WARL field that provides a mechanism to temporarily modify Locked PMP rules. When mseccfg.RLB is 1, locked PMP rules may be removed or modified and locked PMP rules may be edited. When mseccfg.RLB is 0 and pmpcfg.L is 1 in any rule or entry (including disabled entries), then mseccfg.RLB remains 0 and any further modifications to mseccfg.RLB are ignored until a PMP reset.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mmwp_op_warl
tags.text: The mseccfg.MMWP (Machine-Mode Allowlist Policy) is a WARL field. This field changes the default PMP policy for Machine mode when accessing memory regions that don’t have a matching PMP rule. This is a sticky bit, meaning that once set it cannot be unset until a PMP reset. When set it changes the default PMP policy for M-mode when accessing memory regions that don’t have a matching PMP rule, to denied instead of ignored.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mmwp_op_warl
tags.text: The mseccfg.MMWP (Machine-Mode Allowlist Policy) is a WARL field. This field changes the default PMP policy for Machine mode when accessing memory regions that don’t have a matching PMP rule. This is a sticky bit, meaning that once set it cannot be unset until a PMP reset. When set it changes the default PMP policy for M-mode when accessing memory regions that don’t have a matching PMP rule, to denied instead of ignored.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_warl
tags.text: The mseccfg.MML (Machine Mode Lockdown) is a WARL field.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_sticky
tags.text:
This is a sticky bit, meaning that once set it cannot be unset
until a PMP reset.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_set
tags.text: When mseccfg.MML is set the system's behavior changes in the following way:

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_pmpcfg_L_op
tags.text:
The meaning of pmpcfg.L changes:
Instead of marking a rule as locked and
enforced in all modes, it now marks a rule as M-mode-only when set and
S/U-mode-only when unset. The formerly reserved encoding of pmpcfg.RW=01,
and the encoding pmpcfg.LRWX=1111, now encode a Shared-Region.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_set
tags.text: When mseccfg.MML is set the system's behavior changes in the following way:

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_M_rule_op
tags.text:
An M-mode-only rule is enforced on Machine mode and denied in Supervisor
or User mode. It also remains locked so that any further modifications to
its associated configuration or address registers are ignored until a PMP
reset, unless mseccfg.RLB is set.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_set
tags.text: When mseccfg.MML is set the system's behavior changes in the following way:

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_SorU_rule_op
tags.text:
An S/U-mode-only rule is enforced on Supervisor and User modes and
denied on Machine mode.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_set
tags.text: When mseccfg.MML is set the system's behavior changes in the following way:

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_shared_rule_op
tags.text:
A Shared-Region rule is enforced on all modes, with restrictions depending
on the pmpcfg.L and pmpcfg.X bits:

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_shared_L0_op
tags.text:
A Shared-Region rule where pmpcfg.L is not set can be used for sharing
data between M-mode and S/U-mode, so is not executable. M-mode has
read/write access to that region, and S/U-mode has read access if pmpcfg.X
is not set, or read/write access if pmpcfg.X is set.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_shared_L1_op
tags.text:
A Shared-Region rule where pmpcfg.L is set can be used for sharing code
between M-mode and S/U-mode, so is not writable. Both M-mode and S/U-mode
have execute access on the region, and M-mode also has read access if
pmpcfg.X is set. The rule remains locked so that any further
modifications to its associated configuration or address registers are
ignored until a PMP reset, unless mseccfg.RLB is set.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_shared_LRWX_1111_op
tags.text:
The encoding pmpcfg.LRWX=1111 can be used for sharing data between M-mode
and S/U mode, where both modes only have read-only access to the region. The
rule remains locked so that any further modifications to its associated
configuration or address registers are ignored until a PMP reset, unless
mseccfg.RLB is set.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_X_restrict
tags.text:
Adding a rule with executable privileges that either is M-mode-only or a
locked Shared-Region is not possible and such pmpcfg writes are
ignored, leaving pmpcfg unchanged. This restriction can be temporarily
lifted by setting mseccfg.RLB

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_exec_code
tags.text:
Executing code with Machine mode privileges is only possible from memory
regions with a matching M-mode-only rule or a locked Shared-Region rule
with executable privileges. Executing code from a region without a matching
rule or with a matching S/U-mode-only rule is denied.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_pmm_presence_op
tags.text:
If the Smmpm extension is implemented,
the PMM field enables or disables pointer masking (see <<Zpm>>) for M-mode
according to the values in <<norm:mseccfg_pmm_enc>>.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_pmm_presence_op
tags.text:
If the Smmpm extension is implemented,
the PMM field enables or disables pointer masking (see <<Zpm>>) for M-mode
according to the values in <<norm:mseccfg_pmm_enc>>.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_pmm_rdonly0
tags.text:
If Smmpm is not implemented, PMM is read-only zero.
The PMM field is read-only zero for RV32.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_pmm_enc
tags.text:
Value|Description
===
00|Pointer masking is disabled (PMLEN = 0)¶01|Reserved¶10|Pointer masking is enabled with PMLEN = XLEN - 57 (PMLEN = 7 on RV64)¶11|Pointer masking is enabled with PMLEN = XLEN - 48 (PMLEN = 16 on RV64)
===

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_pmm_enc
tags.text:
Value|Description
===
00|Pointer masking is disabled (PMLEN = 0)¶01|Reserved¶10|Pointer masking is enabled with PMLEN = XLEN - 57 (PMLEN = 7 on RV64)¶11|Pointer masking is enabled with PMLEN = XLEN - 48 (PMLEN = 16 on RV64)
===

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mlpe_presence
tags.text: The Zicfilp extension adds the MLPE field in mseccfg.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mlpe_set_op
tags.text: When MLPE field is 1, Zicfilp extension is enabled in M-mode.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mlpe_clr_op_lead-in
tags.text:
When the MLPE field is 0, the Zicfilp extension is not enabled
in M-mode and the following rules apply to M-mode.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mlpe_clr_op_list
tags.text:
The hart does not update the ELP state; it remains as NO_LP_EXPECTED.
The LPAD instruction operates as a no-op.

tags.name: norm:mseccfgh_sz_acc_op
tags.text:
When XLEN=32 only, mseccfgh is a 32-bit read/write register that
aliases bits 63:32 of mseccfg.

tags.name: norm:mseccfgh_sz_acc_op
tags.text:
When XLEN=32 only, mseccfgh is a 32-bit read/write register that
aliases bits 63:32 of mseccfg.

tags.name: norm:mseccfgh_sz_acc_op
tags.text:
When XLEN=32 only, mseccfgh is a 32-bit read/write register that
aliases bits 63:32 of mseccfg.

tags.name: norm:mseccfgh_presence
tags.text:
Register mseccfgh exists when XLEN=32 and mseccfg is implemented;
it does not exist when XLEN=64.

tags.name: norm:mtime_acc
tags.text:
Platforms provide a real-time counter, exposed as a memory-mapped
machine-mode read-write register, mtime.

tags.name: norm:mtime_op
tags.text: mtime must increment at constant frequency

tags.name: norm:mtime_tick_period
tags.text:
the platform must provide a mechanism for
determining the period of an mtime tick.

tags.name: norm:mtime_wrap
tags.text: The mtime register will wrap around if the count overflows.

tags.name: norm:mtime_sz
tags.text: The mtime register has a 64-bit precision on all RV32 and RV64 systems.

tags.name: norm:mtime_intr_pending
tags.text:
A machine timer interrupt becomes pending
whenever mtime contains a value greater than or equal to mtimecmp,
treating the values as unsigned integers.
The interrupt remains posted until mtimecmp becomes greater than mtime

tags.name: norm:mtime_intr_taken
tags.text:
The interrupt will only be taken if interrupts are
enabled and the MTIE bit is set in the mie register.

tags.name: norm:mtime_intr_mtip_visibility
tags.text:
If the result of the comparison between mtime and mtimecmp
changes, it is guaranteed to be reflected in MTIP eventually, but not necessarily immediately.

tags.name: norm:mtime_mtimecmp_rv64_wr
tags.text:
For RV64, naturally aligned 64-bit memory accesses to the mtime and
mtimecmp registers are additionally supported and are atomic.

tags.name: norm:mtimecmp_sz
tags.text:
Platforms provide a 64-bit memory-mapped machine-mode timer
compare register (mtimecmp).

tags.name: norm:mtimecmp_rv32_wr
tags.text:
In RV32, memory-mapped writes to mtimecmp modify only one 32-bit part
of the register.

tags.name: norm:mtime_mtimecmp_rv64_wr
tags.text:
For RV64, naturally aligned 64-bit memory accesses to the mtime and
mtimecmp registers are additionally supported and are atomic.

tags.name: norm:ecall_ebreak_epc_value
tags.text:
ECALL and EBREAK cause the receiving privilege mode’s epc register to
be set to the address of the ECALL or EBREAK instruction itself, not
the address of the following instruction.

tags.name: norm:ecall_ebreak_epc_value
tags.text:
ECALL and EBREAK cause the receiving privilege mode’s epc register to
be set to the address of the ECALL or EBREAK instruction itself, not
the address of the following instruction.

tags.name: norm:ecall_ebreak_no_minstret_inc
tags.text:
As ECALL and EBREAK cause
synchronous exceptions, they are not considered to retire, and should
not increment the minstret CSR.

tags.name: norm:ecall_ebreak_no_minstret_inc
tags.text:
As ECALL and EBREAK cause
synchronous exceptions, they are not considered to retire, and should
not increment the minstret CSR.

tags.name: norm:mret_presence
tags.text: MRET is always provided.

tags.name: norm:sret_presence
tags.text:
SRET must be provided if supervisor mode is supported, and
should raise an illegal-instruction exception otherwise.

tags.name: norm:sret_ill_inst_exc_rst
tags.text:
SRET should also raise an illegal-instruction exception when TSR=1
in mstatus, as described in <<virt-control>>.

tags.name: norm:xret_in_higher_mode
tags.text:
An xRET instruction can be executed in privilege mode x or higher,
where executing a lower-privilege xRET instruction will pop the relevant lower-privilege
interrupt enable and privilege mode stack.

tags.name: norm:xret_in_lower_mode
tags.text:
Attempting to execute an xRET
instruction in a mode less privileged than x will raise an
illegal-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:xret_in_lower_mode
tags.text:
Attempting to execute an xRET
instruction in a mode less privileged than x will raise an
illegal-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:xret_in_lower_mode
tags.text:
Attempting to execute an xRET
instruction in a mode less privileged than x will raise an
illegal-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:xret_op
tags.text:
In addition to manipulating the privilege stack as described in <<privstack>>,
xRET sets the pc to the value stored in the xepc register.

tags.name: norm:xret_op
tags.text:
In addition to manipulating the privilege stack as described in <<privstack>>,
xRET sets the pc to the value stored in the xepc register.

tags.name: norm:xret_clr_lr_resv
tags.text:
If the A extension is supported, the xRET instruction is allowed to
clear any outstanding LR address reservation but is not required to.

tags.name: norm:xret_clr_lr_resv
tags.text:
If the A extension is supported, the xRET instruction is allowed to
clear any outstanding LR address reservation but is not required to.

tags.name: norm:wfi_op
tags.text:
The Wait for Interrupt instruction (WFI) informs the
implementation that the current hart can be stalled until an interrupt
might need servicing. Execution of the WFI instruction can also be used
to inform the hardware platform that suitable interrupts should
preferentially be routed to this hart.

tags.name: norm:wfi_all_privileged_modes
tags.text: WFI is available in all privileged modes

tags.name: norm:wfi_opt_U_mode
tags.text: optionally available to U-mode.

tags.name: norm:wfi_ill_exc
tags.text:
This instruction may raise an illegal-instruction exception when TW=1 in mstatus, as
described in <<virt-control>>.

tags.name: norm:wfi_mepc_val
tags.text:
If an enabled interrupt is present or later becomes present while the
hart is stalled, the interrupt trap will be taken on the following
instruction, i.e., execution resumes in the trap handler and mepc = pc + 4.

tags.name: norm:wfi_resume_reason
tags.text:
Implementations are permitted to resume execution for any reason, even if an
enabled interrupt has not become pending.
Hence, a legal implementation is to simply implement the WFI instruction as a NOP.

tags.name: norm:wfi_intr_dis
tags.text: The WFI instruction can also be executed when interrupts are disabled.

tags.name: norm:wfi_unaffected_conditions
tags.text:
The operation of WFI must be unaffected by the global interrupt bits in
mstatus (MIE and SIE) and the delegation register mideleg (i.e.,
the hart must resume if a locally enabled interrupt becomes pending,
even if it has been delegated to a less-privileged mode), but should
honor the individual interrupt enables (e.g, MTIE) (i.e.,
implementations should avoid resuming the hart if the interrupt is
pending but not individually enabled). WFI is also required to resume
execution for locally enabled interrupts pending at any privilege level,
regardless of the global interrupt enable at each privilege level.

tags.name: norm:wfi_no_intr_pc
tags.text:
If the event that causes the hart to resume execution
does not cause an interrupt to be taken, execution will resume at pc + 4

tags.name: norm:M-mode_at_rst1
tags.text:
M-mode is used for low-level access to a hardware platform and
is the first mode entered at reset.

tags.name: norm:M-mode_at_rst2
tags.text: Upon reset, a hart’s privilege mode is set to M.

tags.name: norm:ld_rsv_rst
tags.text:
For implementations with the "A" standard extension, there is no valid
load reservation.

tags.name: norm:pc_rst
tags.text: The pc is set to an implementation-defined reset vector.

tags.name: norm:mcause_rst1_val
tags.text: The mcause register is set to a value indicating the cause of the reset.

tags.name: norm:mcause_rst2_val
tags.text:
The mcause values after reset have implementation-specific
interpretation

tags.name: norm:mcause_rst_zero
tags.text:
the value 0 should be returned on implementations
that do not distinguish different reset conditions. Implementations that
distinguish different reset conditions should only use 0 to indicate the
most complete reset.

tags.name: norm:pmp_A_L_rst
tags.text:
Writable PMP registers’ A and L fields are set to 0, unless
the platform mandates a different reset value for some PMP registers’ A
and L fields.

tags.name: norm:hgatp_vsatp_mode_rst
tags.text:
If the hypervisor extension is implemented, the
hgatp.MODE and vsatp.MODE fields are reset to 0.

tags.name: norm:mnstatus_nmie_rst
tags.text: If the Smrnmi extension is implemented, the mnstatus.NMIE field is reset to 0.

tags.name: norm:warl_rst
tags.text: No WARL field contains an illegal value.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mlpe_rst
tags.text: If the Zicfilp extension is implemented, the mseccfg.MLPE field is reset to 0.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_mml_mmwp_rlb_rst
tags.text:
The MML, MMWP, and RLB fields of the mseccfg register
are set to 0, unless the platform mandates a different reset value.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_useed_sseed_rst
tags.text:
The USEED and SSEED fields of the mseccfg CSR
must have defined reset values. The system must not allow them to be in an undefined state
after reset.

tags.name: norm:mcause_rst_alias_ok
tags.text:
mcause reset values may alias mcause values
following synchronous exceptions

tags.name: norm:pma_indep_exec_context
tags.text:
PMAs do not vary by execution
context.

tags.name: norm:pma_rtcfg_mmr
tags.text:
Where the attributes are run-time configurable,
platform-specific memory-mapped control
registers can be provided

tags.name: norm:pma_chk_paddr
tags.text:
PMAs are checked for any access to physical memory, including accesses
that have undergone virtual to physical memory translation.

tags.name: norm:pma_precise_recom
tags.text:
we strongly recommend that, where possible, RISC-V
processors precisely trap physical memory accesses that fail PMA checks.
Precisely trapped PMA violations manifest as instruction, load, or store
access-fault exceptions, distinct from virtual-memory page-fault exceptions.

tags.name: norm:pma_imprecise_ok
tags.text:
Precise PMA traps might not always be possible, for example,
when probing a legacy bus architecture that uses access failures as part
of the discovery mechanism. In this case, error responses from
peripheral devices will be reported as imprecise bus-error interrupts.

tags.name: norm:pma_mm_vs_io_def
tags.text: The most important characterization of a given memory address range is whether it holds regular main memory or I/O devices. Regular main memory is required to have a number of properties, specified below, whereas I/O devices can have a much broader range of attributes. Memory regions that do not fit into regular main memory, for example, device scratchpad RAMs, are categorized as I/O regions.

tags.name: norm:pma_mm_rw
tags.text:
Main memory regions always support read and write of all access widths
required by the attached devices

tags.name: norm:pma_mm_rw
tags.text:
Main memory regions always support read and write of all access widths
required by the attached devices

tags.name: norm:pma_mm_ifetch
tags.text: can specify whether instruction fetch is supported.

tags.name: norm:pma_mm_mandatory_sz
tags.text:
the design of a processor or device accessing main memory
might support other widths, but must be able to function with the types
supported by the main memory.

tags.name: norm:pma_io_rwx_per_sz
tags.text:
I/O regions can specify which combinations of read, write, or execute
accesses to which data widths are supported.

tags.name: norm:pma_vm_hw_tbl_acc
tags.text:
For systems with page-based virtual memory, I/O and memory regions can
specify which combinations of hardware page-table reads and hardware
page-table writes are supported.

tags.name: norm:pma_cache_mm_all_atomics
tags.text:
Some platforms might mandate that all of cacheable main memory support
all atomic operations

tags.name: norm:pma_amo_levels
tags.text:
Within AMOs, there are four levels of support: AMONone, AMOSwap,
AMOLogical, and AMOArithmetic. AMONone indicates that no AMO
operations are supported. AMOSwap indicates that only amoswap
instructions are supported in this address range. AMOLogical indicates
that swap instructions plus all the logical AMOs (amoand, amoor,
amoxor) are supported. AMOArithmetic indicates that all RISC-V AMOs defined by
the A extension are supported.

tags.name: norm:pma_amo_sz_support
tags.text:
For each level of support, naturally aligned AMOs of a given width
are supported if the underlying memory region supports reads and writes of that width.

tags.name: norm:pma_amo_far_subset_proc
tags.text: Main memory and I/O regions may only support a subset or none of the processor-supported atomic operations.

tags.name: norm:pma_amo_zacas_levels1
tags.text:
The Zacas extension defines three additional levels of support: AMOCASW,
AMOCASD, and AMOCASQ.

tags.name: norm:pma_amo_zacas_levels2
tags.text:
AMOCASW indicates that in addition to instructions indicated by AMOArithmetic
level support, the AMOCAS.W instruction is supported. AMOCASD indicates that
in addition to instructions indicated by AMOCASW level support, the AMOCAS.D
instruction is supported. AMOCASQ indicates that in addition to instructions
indicated by AMOCASD level support, the AMOCAS.Q instruction is supported.

tags.name: norm:pma_amo_zacas_req_arith
tags.text:
AMOCASW/D/Q require AMOArithmetic level support as the AMOCAS.W/D/Q
instructions require ability to perform an arithmetic comparison and a swap operation.

tags.name: norm:pma_amo_zabha_req
tags.text:
The AMOs specified by the Zabha extension require the same level of support as
the corresponding instructions in the A standard extension or the Zacas extension.

tags.name: norm:pma_rsrv_levels
tags.text:
For LR/SC, there are three levels of support indicating combinations
of the reservability and eventuality properties: RsrvNone,
RsrvNonEventual, and RsrvEventual. RsrvNone indicates that no LR/SC
operations are supported (the location is non-reservable).
RsrvNonEventual indicates that the operations are supported (the
location is reservable), but without the eventual success guarantee
described in the unprivileged ISA specification. RsrvEventual indicates
that the operations are supported and provide the eventual success guarantee.

tags.name: norm:pma_mag_def
tags.text: The misaligned atomicity granule PMA provides constrained support for misaligned AMOs. This PMA, if present, specifies the size of a misaligned atomicity granule, a naturally aligned power-of-two number of bytes. Specific supported values for this PMA are represented by MAGNN, e.g., MAG16 indicates the misaligned atomicity granule is at least 16 bytes.

tags.name: norm:pma_mag_insts
tags.text:
The misaligned atomicity granule PMA applies only to AMOs, loads and stores
defined in the base ISAs, and loads and stores of no more than XLEN bits
defined in the F, D, and Q extensions, and compressed encodings thereof.

tags.name: norm:pma_mag_op_within
tags.text:
if all accessed bytes lie within the same
misaligned atomicity granule, the instruction will not raise an exception for
reasons of address alignment, and the instruction will give rise to only one
memory operation for the purposes of RVWMO--i.e., it will execute atomically.

tags.name: norm:pma_mag_op_amo
tags.text:
If a misaligned AMO accesses a region that does not specify a misaligned
atomicity granule PMA, or if not all accessed bytes lie within the same
misaligned atomicity granule, then an exception is raised.

tags.name: norm:pma_mag_op_amo
tags.text:
If a misaligned AMO accesses a region that does not specify a misaligned
atomicity granule PMA, or if not all accessed bytes lie within the same
misaligned atomicity granule, then an exception is raised.

tags.name: norm:pma_mag_op_ldst
tags.text:
For regular loads and stores that access such a region or for which not all
accessed bytes lie within the same atomicity granule, then either an exception
is raised, or the access proceeds but is not guaranteed to be atomic.

tags.name: norm:pma_mag_exc
tags.text:
Implementations may raise access-fault exceptions instead of
address-misaligned exceptions for some misaligned accesses, indicating the
instruction should not be emulated by a trap handler.

tags.name: norm:pma_mag_op_rsrv
tags.text:
LR/SC instructions are unaffected by this PMA and so always raise an
exception when misaligned.

tags.name: norm:pma_mag_op_vec
tags.text:
Vector memory accesses are also unaffected, so might execute non-atomically even
when contained within a misaligned atomicity granule.

tags.name: norm:pma_mag_op_implicit
tags.text: Implicit accesses are similarly unaffected by this PMA.

tags.name: norm:pma_mo_io_chan_def
tags.text:
Each strongly ordered I/O region specifies a numbered ordering channel,
which is a mechanism by which ordering guarantees can be provided
between different I/O regions.

tags.name: norm:pma_mo_io_chan_0
tags.text:
Channel 0 is used to indicate
point-to-point strong ordering only, where only accesses by the hart to
the single associated I/O region are strongly ordered.

tags.name: norm:pma_mo_io_chan_def
tags.text:
Each strongly ordered I/O region specifies a numbered ordering channel,
which is a mechanism by which ordering guarantees can be provided
between different I/O regions.

tags.name: norm:pma_mo_io_chan_1
tags.text: Channel 1 is used to provide global strong ordering across all I/O regions. Any accesses by a hart to any I/O region associated with channel 1 can only be observed to have occurred in program order by all other harts and I/O devices, including relative to accesses made by that hart to relaxed I/O regions or strongly ordered I/O regions with different channel numbers. In other words, any access to a region in channel 1 is equivalent to executing a fence io,io instruction before and after the instruction.

tags.name: norm:pma_mo_io_chan_def
tags.text:
Each strongly ordered I/O region specifies a numbered ordering channel,
which is a mechanism by which ordering guarantees can be provided
between different I/O regions.

tags.name: norm:pma_mo_io_chan_other
tags.text: Other larger channel numbers provide program ordering to accesses by that hart across any regions with the same channel number

tags.name: norm:pma_mo_dyn
tags.text: Systems might support dynamic configuration of ordering properties on each memory region.

tags.name: norm:pma_non_cacheable
tags.text:
If a PMA indicates non-cacheability, then accesses to that region must
be satisfied by the memory itself, not by any caches.

tags.name: norm:pma_idp_mm
tags.text: Main memory regions are assumed to be idempotent.

tags.name: norm:pma_idp_no_spec
tags.text:
hardware should always be designed to avoid speculative or
redundant accesses to memory regions marked as non-idempotent

tags.name: norm:pma_idp_misaligned_exc
tags.text:
Non-idempotent regions might not support misaligned accesses. Misaligned
accesses to such regions should raise access-fault exceptions rather
than address-misaligned exceptions

tags.name: norm:pma_idp_implicit_ok
tags.text:
For non-idempotent regions, implicit reads and writes must not be
performed early or speculatively, with the following exceptions. When a
non-speculative implicit read is performed, an implementation is
permitted to additionally read any of the bytes within a naturally
aligned power-of-2 region containing the address of the non-speculative
implicit read. Furthermore, when a non-speculative instruction fetch is
performed, an implementation is permitted to additionally read any of
the bytes within the next naturally aligned power-of-2 region of the
same size (with the address of the region taken modulo 2^XLEN^).

tags.name: norm:pma_idp_implicit_ok
tags.text:
For non-idempotent regions, implicit reads and writes must not be
performed early or speculatively, with the following exceptions. When a
non-speculative implicit read is performed, an implementation is
permitted to additionally read any of the bytes within a naturally
aligned power-of-2 region containing the address of the non-speculative
implicit read. Furthermore, when a non-speculative instruction fetch is
performed, an implementation is permitted to additionally read any of
the bytes within the next naturally aligned power-of-2 region of the
same size (with the address of the region taken modulo 2^XLEN^).

tags.name: norm:pma_idp_implicit_sz
tags.text:
The size of these naturally aligned power-of-2 regions is
implementation-defined, but, for systems with page-based virtual memory,
must not exceed the smallest supported page size.

tags.name: norm:trap_exp
tags.text: When a trap is to be taken into M-mode, if the MDT bit is currently 0, it is then set to 1, and the trap is delivered as expected.

tags.name: norm:trap_exp
tags.text: When a trap is to be taken into M-mode, if the MDT bit is currently 0, it is then set to 1, and the trap is delivered as expected.

tags.name: norm:trap_unexp_mdt_1
tags.text: However, if MDT is already set to 1, then this is an unexpected trap.

tags.name: norm:trap_unexp_rnmi
tags.text:
When the Smrnmi extension
is implemented, a trap caused by an RNMI is not considered an unexpected trap
irrespective of the state of the MDT bit.

tags.name: norm:trap_unexp_mnstatus_nmie_0
tags.text:
a trap that occurs when executing in M-mode with
mnstatus.NMIE set to 0 is an unexpected trap.

tags.name: norm:trap_unexp_hndl_lead-in
tags.text: In the event of a unexpected trap, the handling is as follows:

tags.name: norm:trap_unexp_hndl_rnmi
tags.text:
When the Smrnmi extension is implemented and mnstatus.NMIE is 1, the hart
traps to the RNMI handler. To deliver this trap, the mnepc and mncause
registers are written with the values that the unexpected trap would have
written to the mepc and mcause registers respectively. The privilege
mode information fields in the mnstatus register are written to indicate
M-mode and its NMIE field is set to 0.

tags.name: norm:trap_unexp_hndl_lead-in
tags.text: In the event of a unexpected trap, the handling is as follows:

tags.name: norm:trap_unexp_hndl_no_rnmi
tags.text:
When the Smrnmi extension is not implemented, or if the Smrnmi extension is
implemented and mnstatus.NMIE is 0, the hart enters a critical-error state
without updating any architectural state, including the pc. This state
involves ceasing execution, disabling all interrupts (including NMIs), and
asserting a critical-error signal to the platform.

tags.name: norm:critical_error
tags.text:
The actions performed by the platform when a hart asserts a critical-error signal
are platform-specific. The range of possible actions include restarting
the affected hart or restarting the entire platform, among others.

tags.name: norm:trap_def_M_mode
tags.text:
By default, all traps at any privilege level are handled in machine
mode

tags.name: norm:trap_del_S-mode
tags.text: When a trap is delegated to S-mode

tags.name: norm:trap_del_S_mode_op
tags.text:
the scause register is written
with the trap cause; the sepc register is written with the virtual
address of the instruction that took the trap; the stval register is
written with an exception-specific datum; the SPP field of mstatus is
written with the active privilege mode at the time of the trap; the SPIE
field of mstatus is written with the value of the SIE field at the
time of the trap; and the SIE field of mstatus is cleared.

tags.name: norm:trap_del_S-mode
tags.text: When a trap is delegated to S-mode

tags.name: norm:trap_del_S_mode_no_M_mode
tags.text:
The mcause, mepc, and mtval registers and the MPP and MPIE fields of
mstatus are not written.

tags.name: norm:trap_never_trans_lower
tags.text:
Traps never transition from a more-privileged mode to a less-privileged mode.
For example, if M-mode has delegated illegal-instruction
exceptions to S-mode, and M-mode software later executes an illegal
instruction, the trap is taken in M-mode, rather than being delegated to S-mode.

tags.name: norm:trap_horiz
tags.text:
traps may be taken horizontally. Using the same
example, if M-mode has delegated illegal-instruction exceptions to
S-mode, and S-mode software later executes an illegal instruction, the
trap is taken in S-mode.

tags.name: norm:trap_del_intr_priv_lvl
tags.text:
Delegated interrupts result in the interrupt being masked at the
delegator privilege level. For example, if the supervisor timer
interrupt (STI) is delegated to S-mode by setting mideleg[5], STIs
will not be taken when executing in M-mode. By contrast, if mideleg[5]
is clear, STIs can be taken in any mode and regardless of current mode
will transfer control to M-mode.

tags.text:

tags.name: norm:intr_mip_mie_op
tags.text:
An interrupt i will trap to M-mode (causing the privilege mode to
change to M-mode) if all of the following are true: (a) either the
current privilege mode is M and the MIE bit in the mstatus register is
set, or the current privilege mode has less privilege than M-mode;
(b) bit i is set in both mip and mie; and (c) if register
mideleg exists, bit i is not set in mideleg.

tags.name: norm:intr_mip_mie_op
tags.text:
An interrupt i will trap to M-mode (causing the privilege mode to
change to M-mode) if all of the following are true: (a) either the
current privilege mode is M and the MIE bit in the mstatus register is
set, or the current privilege mode has less privilege than M-mode;
(b) bit i is set in both mip and mie; and (c) if register
mideleg exists, bit i is not set in mideleg.

tags.name: norm:intr_mip_mie_bounded_time
tags.text:
These conditions for an interrupt trap to occur must be evaluated in a
bounded amount of time from when an interrupt becomes, or ceases to be,
pending in mip,

tags.name: norm:intr_mip_mie_op
tags.text:
An interrupt i will trap to M-mode (causing the privilege mode to
change to M-mode) if all of the following are true: (a) either the
current privilege mode is M and the MIE bit in the mstatus register is
set, or the current privilege mode has less privilege than M-mode;
(b) bit i is set in both mip and mie; and (c) if register
mideleg exists, bit i is not set in mideleg.

tags.name: norm:intr_mip_mie_xret_csrwr
tags.text:
be evaluated immediately following the
execution of an xRET instruction or an explicit write to a CSR on
which these interrupt trap conditions expressly depend (including mip,
mie, mstatus, and mideleg).

tags.name: norm:intr_M_mode_highest_pri
tags.text: Interrupts to M-mode take priority over any interrupts to lower privilege modes.

tags.name: norm:intr_sei_op
tags.text:
Supervisor-level external interrupts are made pending based on the logical-OR of the
software-writable SEIP bit and the signal from the external interrupt controller.

tags.name: norm:intr_M_mode_pri
tags.text:
Multiple simultaneous interrupts destined for M-mode are handled in the
following decreasing priority order: MEI, MSI, MTI, SEI, SSI, STI, LCOFI.

tags.name: norm:nmi_op
tags.text:
cause an immediate jump to an implementation-defined NMI
vector running in M-mode regardless of the state of a hart’s interrupt
enable bits. The mepc register is written with the virtual address of
the instruction that was interrupted,

tags.name: norm:nmi_mcause_val1
tags.text:
mcause is set to a value
indicating the source of the NMI.

tags.name: norm:nmi_mcause_val2
tags.text: The values written to mcause on an NMI are implementation-defined.

tags.name: norm:nmi_mcause_restrictions
tags.text:
The high Interrupt bit of mcause should be set to indicate
that this was an interrupt. An Exception Code of 0 is reserved to mean "unknown
cause" and implementations that do not distinguish sources of NMIs via
the mcause register should return 0 in the Exception Code.

tags.name: norm:nmi_no_rst
tags.text: NMIs do not reset processor state

tags.name: norm:sxlen_uxlen
tags.text: The effective XLEN in S-mode and U-mode are termed SXLEN and UXLEN, respectively.

tags.name: norm:mstatus_sxl_uxl_sxlen_uxlen_mxlen32
tags.text: When MXLEN=32, the SXL and UXL fields do not exist, and SXLEN=32 and UXLEN=32.

tags.name: norm:sxlen_uxlen
tags.text: The effective XLEN in S-mode and U-mode are termed SXLEN and UXLEN, respectively.

tags.name: norm:mstatus_sxl_uxl_sxlen_uxlen_mxlen32
tags.text: When MXLEN=32, the SXL and UXL fields do not exist, and SXLEN=32 and UXLEN=32.

tags.name: norm:xlen_reduction_op
tags.text: Whenever XLEN in any mode is set to a value less than the widest supported XLEN, all operations must ignore source operand register bits above the configured XLEN, and must sign-extend results to fill the entire widest supported XLEN in the destination register. Similarly, pc bits above XLEN are ignored, and when the pc is written, it is sign-extended to fill the widest supported XLEN.

tags.name: norm:xlen_reduction_hint_op1
tags.text: Some HINT instructions are encoded as integer computational instructions that overwrite their destination register with its current value, e.g., c.addi x8, 0. When such a HINT is executed with XLEN < MXLEN and bits MXLEN..XLEN of the destination register not all equal to bit XLEN-1, it is implementation-defined whether bits MXLEN..XLEN of the destination register are unchanged or are overwritten with copies of bit XLEN-1.

tags.name: norm:xlen_reduction_hint_op2
tags.text:
This definition allows implementations to elide register write-back for
some HINTs, while allowing them to execute other HINTs in the same manner as
other integer computational instructions.
The implementation choice is observable only by privilege modes with an XLEN
setting greater than the current XLEN; it is invisible to the current
privilege mode.

tags.name: norm:endianness_inst_fetch_little
tags.text: Instruction fetches are always little-endian.

tags.name: norm:rst_and_nmi_addr
tags.text: Reset and NMI vector locations are given in a platform specification.

tags.name: norm:rst_and_nmi_addr
tags.text: Reset and NMI vector locations are given in a platform specification.

tags.name: norm:uncached_ignores_cached
tags.text:
For implementations with a cacheability-control mechanism, the situation
may arise that a program uncacheably accesses a memory location that is
currently cache-resident. In this situation, the cached copy must be
ignored. This constraint is necessary to prevent more-privileged modes’
speculative cache refills from affecting the behavior of less-privileged
modes’ uncacheable accesses.

normative_rules.name: PMP_GRANULARITY
normative_rules.summary: Minimum PMP region granularity supported by the platform

tags.name: norm:pmp_granularity
tags.text:
The granularity of PMP access control settings are platform-specific,
but the standard PMP encoding supports regions as small as four bytes.

tags.name: norm:pmp_mmode_only_regions
tags.text:
Certain regions’ privileges can be hardwired—for example, some regions
might only ever be visible in machine mode but in no lower-privilege
layers.

tags.name: norm:pmp_check_priv_modes
tags.text:
PMP checks are applied to all accesses whose effective privilege mode is
S or U, including instruction fetches and data accesses in S and U mode,
and data accesses in M-mode when the MPRV bit in mstatus is set and
the MPP field in mstatus contains S or U.

tags.name: norm:pmp_check_pagetable_access
tags.text:
PMP checks are also applied
to page-table accesses for virtual-address translation, for which the
effective privilege mode is S.

tags.name: norm:pmp_mmode_locking
tags.text:
Optionally, PMP checks may additionally
apply to M-mode accesses, in which case the PMP registers themselves are
locked, so that even M-mode software cannot change them until the hart
is reset.

tags.name: norm:pmp_violation_precise_trap
tags.text: PMP violations are always trapped precisely at the processor.

tags.name: norm:pmp_entry_structure
tags.text:
PMP entries are described by an 8-bit configuration register and one
MXLEN-bit address register. Some PMP settings additionally use the
address register associated with the preceding PMP entry.

tags.name: norm:pmp_csr_mode
tags.text:
PMP CSRs are only
accessible to M-mode.

normative_rules.name: pmp_entry_count
normative_rules.summary: PMP supports up to 64 entries with allowed implementation counts and ordering rules

tags.name: norm:pmp_entry_count
tags.text:
Up to 64 PMP
entries are supported. Implementations may implement zero, 16, or 64 PMP
entries; the lowest-numbered PMP entries must be implemented first.

normative_rules.name: PMP_CSRS_WARL_ACCESS
normative_rules.summary: PMP CSRs are WARL, may read as zero, and are accessible only in M-mode

tags.name: norm:pmp_csrs_warl_access
tags.text:
All
PMP CSR fields are WARL and may be read-only zero.

normative_rules.name: pmp_cfg_rv32_layout
normative_rules.summary: RV32 PMP configuration CSRs layout and mapping

tags.name: norm:pmp_cfg_rv32_layout
tags.text:
For RV32, sixteen CSRs, pmpcfg0–pmpcfg15, hold
the configurations pmp0cfg–pmp63cfg for the 64 PMP entries, as shown
in <<pmpcfg-rv32>>.

normative_rules.name: pmp_cfg_rv64_layout
normative_rules.summary: RV64 PMP configuration CSRs layout using even-numbered CSRs

tags.name: norm:pmp_cfg_rv64_layout
tags.text:
For RV64, eight
even-numbered CSRs, pmpcfg0, pmpcfg2, …, pmpcfg14, hold the
configurations for the 64 PMP entries, as shown in
<<pmpcfg-rv64>>.

normative_rules.name: pmp_cfg_rv64_illegal
normative_rules.summary: Odd-numbered PMP configuration CSRs are illegal in RV64

tags.name: norm:pmp_cfg_rv64_illegal
tags.text:
For RV64, the odd-numbered
configuration registers, pmpcfg1, pmpcfg3, …, pmpcfg15, are
illegal.

normative_rules.name: pmp_addr_registers
normative_rules.summary: PMP address registers are named pmpaddr0 through pmpaddr63

tags.name: norm:pmp_addr_registers
tags.text: The PMP address registers are CSRs named pmpaddr0-pmpaddr63.

normative_rules.name: pmp_addr_encoding
normative_rules.summary: PMP address registers encode physical address bits depending on XLEN

tags.name: norm:pmp_addr_encoding
tags.text:
Each
PMP address register encodes bits 33-2 of a 34-bit physical address for
RV32, as shown in <<pmpaddr-rv32>>. For RV64,
each PMP address register encodes bits 55-2 of a 56-bit physical
address, as shown in <<pmpaddr-rv64>>.

normative_rules.name: PMP_ADDR_WARL
normative_rules.summary: PMP address registers are WARL due to optional physical address bits

tags.name: norm:pmp_addr_warl
tags.text:
Not all
physical address bits may be implemented, and so the pmpaddr registers
are WARL.

normative_rules.name: pmp_cfg_permissions
normative_rules.summary: PMP R, W, and X bits control read, write, and execute permissions

tags.name: norm:pmp_cfg_permissions
tags.text:
<<pmpcfg>> shows the layout of a PMP configuration
register. The R, W, and X bits, when set, indicate that the PMP entry
permits read, write, and instruction execution, respectively. When one
of these bits is clear, the corresponding access type is denied.

tags.name: norm:pmp_rwx_warl
tags.text:
The R,
W, and X fields form a collective WARL field for which the combinations with R=0 and W=1 are reserved.

tags.name: norm:pmp_exec_fault
tags.text:
Attempting to fetch an instruction from a PMP region that does not have
execute permissions raises an instruction access-fault exception.

tags.name: norm:pmp_load_fault
tags.text:
Attempting to execute a load, load-reserved, or cache-block management instruction which accesses
a physical address within a PMP region without read permissions raises a
load access-fault exception.

tags.name: norm:pmp_store_fault
tags.text:
Attempting to execute a store,
store-conditional, AMO, or cache-block zero instruction which accesses a physical address
within a PMP region without write permissions raises a store
access-fault exception.

tags.name: norm:pmp_a_field_encoding
tags.text:
The A field in a PMP entry's configuration register encodes the
address-matching mode of the associated PMP address register. The
encoding of this field is shown in <<pmpcfg-a>>.

tags.name: norm:pmp_a_field_off
tags.text: When A=0, this PMP entry is disabled and matches no addresses.

tags.name: norm:pmp_a_field_napot_na4
norm:pmp_a_field_tor
tags.text:
If TOR is selected, the associated address register forms the top of the
address range, and the preceding PMP address register forms the bottom
of the address range. If PMP entry i's A field is set to
TOR, the entry matches any address y such that pmpaddr~i-1~{le}y<pmpaddr~i~ (irrespective of the value of pmpcfg~i-1~). If PMP entry 0's A field is set to TOR, zero is used for the lower bound, and so it matches
any address y<pmpaddr~0~.

tags.name: norm:pmp_napot_encoding_low_bits
tags.text:
NAPOT ranges make use of the low-order bits of the associated address
register to encode the size of the range, as shown in
<<pmpcfg-napot>>.

tags.name: norm:pmp_region_granularity_g
tags.text:
Although the PMP mechanism supports regions as small as four bytes,
platforms may specify coarser PMP regions. In general, the PMP grain is
2^G+2^ bytes and must be the same across all PMP regions.

tags.name: norm:pmp_na4_select_restriction
tags.text: When G {ge} 1, the NA4 mode is not selectable.

tags.name: norm:pmp_napot_addr_read_mask
tags.text:
When
G {ge} 2 and pmpcfg~i~.A[1] is set,
i.e. the mode is NAPOT, then bits pmpaddr~i~[G-2:0]
read as all ones.

tags.name: norm:pmp_tor_off_addr_read_mask
tags.text:
When G {ge} 1 and
pmpcfg~i~.A[1] is clear, i.e. the mode is OFF or TOR,
then bits pmpaddr~i~[G-1:0] read as all zeros. Bits
pmpaddr~i~[G-1:0] do not affect the TOR address-matching logic.

tags.name: norm:pmp_addr_retention_on_mode_change
tags.text:
Although changing pmpcfg~i~.A[1] affects the value
read from pmpaddr~i~, it does not affect the
underlying value stored in that register—in particular,
pmpaddr~i~[G-1] retains its original value when
pmpcfg~i~.A is changed from NAPOT to TOR/OFF then
back to NAPOT.

tags.name: norm:pmp_l_bit_function
tags.text:
The L bit indicates that the PMP entry is locked, i.e., writes to the
configuration register and associated address registers are ignored.

tags.name: norm:pmp_l_bit_write_protection
tags.text:
If PMP entry
i is locked, writes to pmpicfg and pmpaddri are ignored. Additionally, if PMP
entry i is locked and pmpicfg.A is set
to TOR, writes to pmpaddri-1 are ignored.

tags.name: norm:pmp_l_bit_m_mode_enforcement
tags.text:
In addition to locking the PMP entry, the L bit indicates whether the
R/W/X permissions are additionally enforced on M-mode accesses.
When the L bit is
set, these permissions are enforced for all privilege modes. When the L
bit is clear, any M-mode access matching the PMP entry will succeed; the
R/W/X permissions apply only to S and U modes.

tags.name: norm:pmp_misaligned_access_behavior
tags.text:
On some implementations, misaligned loads, stores, and instruction
fetches may be decomposed into multiple memory operations, some of which may
succeed before an access-fault exception occurs, as described in the RVWMO
specification. PMP checking is performed on each memory operation independently.

tags.name: norm:pmp_entry_priority
tags.text:
PMP entries are statically prioritized. The lowest-numbered PMP entry
that matches any byte of a memory operation determines whether that operation
succeeds or fails.

tags.name: norm:pmp_full_match_required
tags.text:
The matching PMP entry must match all bytes of a memory
operation, or the operation fails, irrespective of the L, R, W, and X bits.

tags.name: norm:pmp_rwx_check
tags.text:
If a PMP entry matches all bytes of a memory operation, then the L, R, W, and X
bits determine whether the operation succeeds or fails. If the L bit is
clear and the privilege mode of the access is M, the operation succeeds.
Otherwise, if the L bit is set or the privilege mode of the access is S
or U, then the operation succeeds only if the R, W, or X bit corresponding
to the access type is set.

tags.name: norm:pmp_no_entry_match
tags.text:
If no PMP entry matches an M-mode memory operation, the operation succeeds.
If no PMP entry matches an S-mode or U-mode memory operation, but at least
one PMP entry is implemented, the operation fails.

tags.name: norm:pmp_access_fault_exception
tags.text:
Failed memory operations generate an instruction, load, or store access-fault
exception.

tags.name: norm:pmp_with_paging
tags.text:
When paging is enabled,
instructions that access virtual memory may result in multiple
physical-memory accesses, including implicit references to the page
tables. The PMP checks apply to all of these accesses.

tags.name: norm:pmp_speculative_translations
tags.text:
Implementations with virtual memory are permitted to perform address
translations speculatively and earlier than required by an explicit
memory access, and are permitted to cache them in address translation
cache structures—including possibly caching the identity mappings from
effective address to physical address used in Bare translation modes and
M-mode. The PMP settings for the resulting physical address may be
checked (and possibly cached) at any point between the address
translation and the explicit memory access.

tags.name: norm:pmp_sfence_required
tags.text:
when the PMP settings
are modified, M-mode software must synchronize the PMP settings with the
virtual memory system and any PMP or address-translation caches. This is
accomplished by executing an SFENCE.VMA instruction with rs1=x0 and
rs2=x0, after the PMP CSRs are written.

tags.name: norm:Zicfilp_forward_traps
tags.text:
A trap may need to be delivered to the same or to a higher privilege mode upon
completion of JALR/C.JALR/C.JR, but before the instruction at the target
of indirect call/jump was decoded

tags.name: norm:Zicfilp_forward_trap_async_interrupt
tags.text: Asynchronous interrupts.

tags.name: norm:Zicfilp_forward_trap_async_exception
tags.text:
Synchronous exceptions with priority higher than that of a software-check
exception with xtval set to "landing pad fault (code=2)" (See
<<norm:exc_priority>> of Privileged Specification).

tags.name: norm:Zicfilp_exception_priority
tags.text: The software-check exception caused by Zicfilp has higher priority than an illegal-instruction exception but lower priority than instruction access-fault.

tags.name: norm:lpad_sw_exception
tags.text: The software-check exception due to the instruction not being an LPAD instruction when ELP is LP_EXPECTED or a software-check exception caused by the LPAD instruction itself leads to a trap being delivered to the same or to a higher privilege mode.

tags.name: norm:cfi_mstatus_spelp_op
tags.text:
To store the previous ELP state on trap
delivery to S/HS-mode, an SPELP bit is provided in the mstatus CSR.

tags.name: norm:cfi_sstatus_spelp_op
tags.text:
The
SPELP bit in mstatus can be accessed through the sstatus CSR.

tags.name: norm:vsstatus_spelp_op2
tags.text:
To store
the previous ELP state on traps to VS-mode, a SPELP bit is defined in the
vsstatus (VS-modes version of sstatus).

tags.name: norm:dcsr_pelp_op
tags.text:
To store the previous ELP state on
transition to Debug Mode, a pelp bit is defined in the dcsr register.

tags.name: norm:Zicfilp_pelp_debug_mode
tags.text: Upon entry into Debug Mode, the pelp bit in dcsr is updated with the ELP at the privilege level the hart was previously in, and the ELP is set to NO_LP_EXPECTED. When a hart resumes from Debug Mode, if the new privilege mode is y, then ELP is set to the value of pelp if yLPE (see ) is 1; otherwise, it is set to NO_LP_EXPECTED.

tags.name: norm:Zicfilp_pelp_trap
tags.text: When a trap is taken into privilege mode x, the xPELP is set to ELP and ELP is set to NO_LP_EXPECTED.

tags.name: norm:Zicfilp_pelp_trap_return
tags.text: An MRET or SRET instruction is used to return from a trap in M-mode or S-mode, respectively.  When executing an xRET instruction, if the new privilege mode is y, then ELP is set to the value of xPELP if yLPE (see ) is 1; otherwise, it is set to NO_LP_EXPECTED; xPELP is set to NO_LP_EXPECTED.

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_m_menvcfg_sse
tags.text:
If the privilege mode is less than M and menvcfg.SSE is 0, an
illegal-instruction exception is raised.

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_ssp_csr
tags.text: Attempts to access the ssp CSR may result in either an illegal-instruction exception or a virtual-instruction  exception, contingent upon the state of the x`envcfg.SSE` fields. The conditions are specified as follows:

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_u_senvcfg_sse
tags.text:
Otherwise, if in U-mode and senvcfg.SSE is 0, an illegal-instruction
exception is raised.

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_ssp_csr
tags.text: Attempts to access the ssp CSR may result in either an illegal-instruction exception or a virtual-instruction  exception, contingent upon the state of the x`envcfg.SSE` fields. The conditions are specified as follows:

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_vs_henvcfg_sse
tags.text:
Otherwise, if in VS-mode and henvcfg.SSE is 0, a virtual-instruction
exception is raised.

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_ssp_csr
tags.text: Attempts to access the ssp CSR may result in either an illegal-instruction exception or a virtual-instruction  exception, contingent upon the state of the x`envcfg.SSE` fields. The conditions are specified as follows:

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_vu_henvcfg_senvcfg_sse
tags.text:
Otherwise, if in VU-mode and either henvcfg.SSE or senvcfg.SSE is 0,
a virtual-instruction  exception is raised.

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_ssp_csr
tags.text: Attempts to access the ssp CSR may result in either an illegal-instruction exception or a virtual-instruction  exception, contingent upon the state of the x`envcfg.SSE` fields. The conditions are specified as follows:

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_sse_access
tags.text: Otherwise, the access is allowed.

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_ssp_csr
tags.text: Attempts to access the ssp CSR may result in either an illegal-instruction exception or a virtual-instruction  exception, contingent upon the state of the x`envcfg.SSE` fields. The conditions are specified as follows:

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_smode_xsse
tags.text: When S-mode is not implemented, then xSSE is 0 at both M and U privilege modes.

tags.name: norm:ss_page_enc
tags.text:
The
encoding R=0, W=1, and X=0, is defined to represent an SS page.

tags.name: norm:ssmp_menvcfg_sse
tags.text:
When
menvcfg.SSE=0, this encoding remains reserved.

tags.name: norm:ssmp_henvcfg_sse
tags.text:
Similarly, when V=1 and
henvcfg.SSE=0, this encoding remains reserved at VS and VU levels.

tags.name: norm:satp_mode_bare
tags.text:
If satp.MODE (or vsatp.MODE when V=1) is set to Bare and the effective
privilege mode is less than M, shadow stack instructions raise a store/AMO access-fault exception.

tags.name: norm:ssmp_ssamoswap
tags.text:
When the effective privilege mode is M, memory access
by an SSAMOSWAP.W/D instruction results in a store/AMO access-fault exception.

tags.name: norm:ssmp_ss_page_access_fault
tags.text:
Memory mapped as an SS page cannot be written to by instructions other than
SSAMOSWAP.W/D, SSPUSH, and C.SSPUSH. Attempts will raise a store/AMO
access-fault exception.

tags.name: norm:ssmp_ss_cache_block_access_fault
tags.text:
Access to a SS page using cache-block operation
(CBO.*) instructions is not permitted. Such accesses will raise a store/AMO
access-fault exception.

tags.name: norm:ssmp_ss_implicit_access_fault
tags.text:
Implicit accesses, including instruction fetches to an
SS page, are not permitted. Such accesses will raise an access-fault exception
appropriate to the access type.

tags.name: norm:ssmp_ss_load
tags.text:
However, the shadow stack is readable by all
instructions that only load from memory.

tags.name: norm:ss_fault_exception_code
tags.text:
If a shadow stack (SS) instruction raises an access-fault, page-fault, or
guest-page-fault exception that is supposed to indicate the original instruction
type (load or store/AMO), then the reported exception cause is respectively a
store/AMO access fault (code 7), a store/AMO page fault (code 15), or a
store/AMO guest-page fault (code 23).

tags.name: norm:ssmp_ss_page_illegeal_access
tags.text:
Should a shadow stack instruction access a page that is
not designated as a shadow stack page and is not marked as read-only
(pte.xwr=001), a store/AMO access-fault exception will be invoked.

tags.name: norm:ssmp_ss_read_only_page
tags.text:
Conversely,
if the page being accessed by a shadow stack instruction is a read-only page, a
store/AMO page-fault exception will be triggered.

tags.name: norm:ssp_xlen_aligned
tags.text: If the virtual address in ssp is not XLEN aligned, then the SSPUSH/ C.SSPUSH/SSPOPCHK/C.SSPOPCHK instructions cause a store/AMO access-fault exception.

tags.name: norm:ssmp_ss_idempotent_memory
tags.text:
If the memory referenced by
SSPUSH/C.SSPUSH/SSPOPCHK/C.SSPOPCHK/SSAMOSWAP.W/D instructions is not
idempotent, then the instructions cause a store/AMO access-fault exception.

tags.name: norm:active_g_stage_pte
tags.text:
When G-stage page tables are active, the shadow stack instructions that access memory
require the G-stage page table to have read-write permission for the accessed
memory; else a store/AMO guest-page-fault exception is raised.

tags.name: norm:Zicsr_higher_priv
tags.text:
although CSRs and instructions are associated with one privilege
level, they are also accessible at all higher privilege levels.

tags.name: norm:Zicsr_rw
tags.text: The top two bits (csr[11:10]) indicate whether the register is read/write (00,01, or 10) or read-only (11).

tags.name: norm:Zicsr_access
tags.text: The next two bits (csr[9:8]) encode the lowest privilege level that can access the CSR, with the pattern 10 representing hypervisor CSRs.

tags.name: norm:Zicsr_illegal_mode
tags.text:
Attempts to access a CSR without appropriate privilege level
raise illegal-instruction exceptions or, as described in
<<sec:hcauses>>, virtual-instruction exceptions.

tags.name: norm:Zicsr_illegal_acc
tags.text: Attempts to write a read-only register raise illegal-instruction exceptions.

tags.name: norm:Zicwr_write_ro
tags.text:
A read/write register might also contain some bits that are
read-only, in which case writes to the read-only bits are ignored.

tags.name: norm:Zicsr_debug_illegal
tags.text:
0x7B0-0x7BF are only visible to debug mode.
Implementations should raise illegal-instruction exceptions on
machine-mode access to the latter set of registers.

tags.name: norm:Zicsr_wpri_roz
tags.text:
For forward compatibility, implementations that do not
furnish these fields must make them read-only zero. These fields are
labeled WPRI in the register descriptions.

tags.name: norm:Zicsr_wlrl
tags.text:
Software
should not write anything other than legal values to such a field, and
should not assume a read will return a legal value unless the last write
was of a legal value, or the register has not been written since another
operation (e.g., reset) set the register to a legal value. These fields
are labeled WLRL in the register descriptions.

tags.name: norm:Zicsr_wlrl_exception
tags.text:
Implementations are permitted but not required to raise an
illegal-instruction exception if an instruction attempts to write a
non-supported value to a WLRL field.

tags.name: norm:Zicsr_warl
tags.text:
Implementations will not raise an exception on writes of unsupported
values to a WARL field.

tags.name: norm:always_priv_level
tags.text:
At any time, a RISC-V hardware thread (hart) is running at some
privilege level encoded as a mode in one or more CSRs (control and
status registers).

tags.name: norm:priv_levels-txt
tags.text: Three RISC-V privilege levels are currently defined

tags.name: norm:priv_levels_tbl
tags.text:
Level|Encoding|Name|Abbreviation
===
0
1
2
3|00
01
10
11|User/Application
Supervisor
Reserved
Machine|U
S
 
M
===

tags.name: norm:non_priv_exc
tags.text:
attempts to perform operations not
permitted by the current privilege mode will cause an exception to be
raised.

tags.name: norm:m-level-high-priv-only-mandatory
tags.text:
The machine level has the highest privileges and
is the only mandatory privilege level for a RISC-V hardware platform.

tags.name: norm:m-level-high-priv-only-mandatory
tags.text:
The machine level has the highest privileges and
is the only mandatory privilege level for a RISC-V hardware platform.

tags.name: norm:m_mode_mandatory
tags.text: All hardware implementations must provide M-mode

tags.name: norm:priv_combs_txt
tags.text: Implementations might provide anywhere from 1 to 3 privilege modes

tags.name: norm:priv_combs_tbl
tags.text:
Number of levels|Supported Modes|Intended Usage
===
1
2
3|M
M, U
M, S, U|Simple embedded systems
Secure embedded systems
Systems running Unix-like operating systems
===

tags.name: norm:flen
tags.text:
The F extension adds 32 floating-point registers, f0-f31, each 32
bits wide

tags.name: norm:Q_flen_128
tags.text:
The floating-point
registers are now extended to hold either a single, double, or
quad-precision floating-point value (FLEN=128).

tags.name: norm:fsq_flq_op
tags.text: New 128-bit variants of LOAD-FP and STORE-FP instructions are added, encoded with a new value for the funct3 width field.

tags.name: norm:fsq_flq_op
tags.text: New 128-bit variants of LOAD-FP and STORE-FP instructions are added, encoded with a new value for the funct3 width field.

tags.name: norm:fsq_flq_atomic_align
tags.text: FLQ and FSQ are only guaranteed to execute atomically if the effective address is naturally aligned and XLEN=128.

tags.name: norm:fsq_flq_atomic_align
tags.text: FLQ and FSQ are only guaranteed to execute atomically if the effective address is naturally aligned and XLEN=128.

tags.name: norm:fsq_flq_bits_maintained
tags.text: FLQ and FSQ do not modify the bits being transferred; in particular, the payloads of non-canonical NaNs are preserved.

tags.name: norm:fsq_flq_bits_maintained
tags.text: FLQ and FSQ do not modify the bits being transferred; in particular, the payloads of non-canonical NaNs are preserved.

tags.name: norm:fadd-s_fmul-s_op
tags.text:
FADD.S and FMUL.S
perform single-precision floating-point addition and multiplication
respectively, between rs1 and rs2

tags.name: norm:Q_computational_instrs
tags.text: The quad-precision floating-point computational instructions are defined analogously to their double-precision counterparts, but operate on quad-precision operands and produce quad-precision results.

tags.name: norm:FP_nontransfer_instrs_improper_nan-boxed_input
tags.text: Apart from transfer operations described in the previous paragraph, all other floating-point operations on narrower n-bit operations, n<FLEN, check if the input operands are correctly NaN-boxed, i.e., all upper FLEN-n bits are 1. If so, the n least-significant bits of the input are used as the input value, otherwise the input value is treated as an n-bit canonical NaN.

tags.name: norm:ieee_std_subnormal
tags.text: Operations on subnormal numbers are handled in accordance with the IEEE 754-2008 standard.

tags.name: norm:ieee_std_tininess
tags.text: In the parlance of the IEEE standard, tininess is detected after rounding.

tags.name: norm:dyn_round_enc
tags.text:
Rounding Mode|Mnemonic|Meaning
===
000|RNE|Round to Nearest, ties to Even¶001|RTZ|Round towards Zero¶010|RDN|Round Down (towards −∞)¶011|RUP|Round Up (towards +∞)¶100|RMM|Round to Nearest, ties to Max Magnitude¶101||Reserved for future use.¶110||Reserved for future use.¶111|DYN|In instruction’s rm field, selects dynamic rounding mode; In Rounding Mode register, reserved.
===

tags.name: norm:roundingmode_dynamic
tags.text:
Floating-point operations use either a static rounding mode encoded in
the instruction, or a dynamic rounding mode held in frm. Rounding
modes are encoded as shown in <<norm:dyn_round_enc>>. A value of 111 in the
instruction's rm field selects the dynamic rounding mode held in
frm

tags.text: FMADD.S multiplies the values in rs1 and rs2, adds the value in rs3, and writes the final result to rd. FMADD.S computes (rs1×rs2)+rs3.

tags.name: norm:fcvt-w-q_fcvt-l-q_op
tags.text:
FCVT.W.Q or FCVT.L.Q converts a quad-precision
floating-point number to a signed 32-bit or 64-bit integer,
respectively

tags.name: norm:fcvt_round
tags.text: All floating-point to integer and integer to floating-point conversion instructions round according to the rm field. A floating-point register can be initialized to floating-point positive zero using FCVT.S.W rd, x0, which will never set any exception flags.

tags.name: norm:fcvt_int_double_valid_input
tags.text:
The range of valid inputs for FCVT.int.D and the behavior for invalid
inputs are the same as for FCVT.int.S

tags.name: norm:fcvt_nx
tags.text: All floating-point conversion instructions set the Inexact exception flag if the rounded result differs from the operand value and the Invalid exception flag is not set.

tags.name: norm:fcvt_unrepresentable_nv
tags.text:
If the rounded result is not representable in the
destination format, it is clipped to the nearest value and the invalid
flag is set

tags.name: norm:ieee_std_subnormal
tags.text: Operations on subnormal numbers are handled in accordance with the IEEE 754-2008 standard.

tags.name: norm:fcvt-q-w_fcvt-q-l_op
tags.text:
FCVT.Q.W or FCVT.Q.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed
integer, respectively, into a quad-precision floating-point number

tags.name: norm:fcvt_round
tags.text: All floating-point to integer and integer to floating-point conversion instructions round according to the rm field. A floating-point register can be initialized to floating-point positive zero using FCVT.S.W rd, x0, which will never set any exception flags.

tags.name: norm:fcvt_long_quad_rv64_only
tags.text:

tags.name: norm:fcvt_nx

tags.name: norm:fcvt_unrepresentable_nv

destination format, it is clipped to the nearest value and the invalid

tags.name: norm:ieee_std_subnormal

tags.name: norm:ieee_std_tininess
tags.text: In the parlance of the IEEE standard, tininess is detected after rounding.

tags.name: norm:fcvt-s-q_fcvt-q-s_op
tags.text:
FCVT.S.Q or
FCVT.Q.S converts a quad-precision floating-point number to a
single-precision floating-point number, or vice-versa, respectively

tags.name: norm:fcvt_float_rounding
tags.text:
FCVT.S.D rounds according to the RM field; FCVT.D.S will
never round

tags.name: norm:fcvt-s-q_fcvt-q-s_op
tags.text:
FCVT.S.Q or
FCVT.Q.S converts a quad-precision floating-point number to a
single-precision floating-point number, or vice-versa, respectively

tags.name: norm:fcvt_float_rounding
tags.text:
FCVT.S.D rounds according to the RM field; FCVT.D.S will
never round

tags.name: norm:fcvt-d-q_fcvt-q-d_op
tags.text:

a double-precision floating-point number, or vice-versa, respectively

tags.name: norm:fcvt_float_rounding
tags.text:
FCVT.S.D rounds according to the RM field; FCVT.D.S will
never round

tags.name: norm:fcvt-d-q_fcvt-q-d_op
tags.text:
