tags.name: norm:seed_ro_illegal
tags.text:
Attempts to access the seed CSR using a read-only CSR-access instruction
(CSRRS/CSRRC with rs1=x0 or CSRRSI/CSRRCI with uimm=0) raise an
illegal-instruction exception; any other CSR-access instruction may be used
to access seed.

tags.name: norm:seed_write_ignore
tags.text: The write value (in rs1 or uimm) must be ignored by implementations.

tags.name: norm:seed_csr_unpriv
tags.text: seed is an unprivileged CSR located at address 0x015.

tags.name: norm:seed_exec_mode_control
tags.text:
The seed CSR is also access controlled by execution mode, and attempted
read or write access will raise an illegal-instruction exception outside M mode
unless access is explicitly granted. See <<crypto_scalar_es_access>> for
more details.

tags.name: norm:seed_entropy_unique
tags.text:
Each returned seed[15:0] = entropy value represents unique randomness
when OPST=ES16 (seed[31:30] = 10), even if its numerical value is
the same as that of a previously polled entropy value.

tags.name: norm:seed_entropy_zero_non_es16
tags.text: When OPST is not ES16, entropy must be set to 0.

tags.name: norm:seed_wipe_on_read
tags.text:
For security reasons, the interface guarantees that secret entropy
words are not made available multiple times. Hence polling (reading) must
also have the side effect of clearing (wipe-on-read) the entropy contents and
changing the state to WAIT (unless there is entropy
immediately available for ES16). Other states (BIST, WAIT, and DEAD)
may be unaffected by polling.

tags.name: norm:seed_bist_latch
tags.text:
Such a BIST alarm must be latched until polled at least once to enable
software to record its occurrence.

tags.name: norm:seed_min_security_256
tags.text:
Any implementation of the seed CSR that limits the security
strength shall not reduce it to less than 256 bits.

tags.name: norm:seed_disable_if_weak
tags.text:
If the security
level is under 256 bits, then the interface must not be available.

tags.name: norm:zkt_optional_instr
tags.text:
Vendors do not have to implement all of the list's instructions to be Zkt
compliant;

tags.name: norm:zkt_impl_must_constant_latency
tags.text: if they claim to have Zkt and implement any of the listed instructions, it must have data-independent latency.

tags.name: norm:smcdeleg_ssccfg_tandem
tags.text: For a RISC-V hardware platform, Smcdeleg and Ssccfg must always be implemented in tandem.

tags.name: norm:smcdeleg_cde_en
tags.text: When the Smcdeleg/Ssccfg extensions are enabled (menvcfg.CDE=1), it further allows M-mode to delegate select counters to S-mode.

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_illegal_sireg_cde0
tags.text: * attempts to access any sireg* when menvcfg.CDE = 0;

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_illegal_sireg3_6
tags.text: * attempts to access sireg3 or sireg6;

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_illegal_sireg4_5_xlen64
tags.text: * attempts to access sireg4 or sireg5 when XLEN = 64;

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_illegal_sireg_not_delegated
tags.text:
* attempts to access sireg* when siselect = 0x41, or when the counter
selected by siselect is not delegated to S-mode (the corresponding bit
in mcounteren = 0).

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_missing_extension_illegal
tags.text: If any extension upon which the underlying state depends is not implemented, an attempt from M or S mode to access the given state through sireg* raises an illegal-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_hyp_vs_or_vu_access_vsireg_illegal
tags.text: If the hypervisor (H) extension is also implemented, then as specified by extensions Smcsrind/Sscsrind, a virtual-instruction  exception is raised for attempts from VS-mode or VU-mode to directly access vsiselect or vsireg*, or attempts from VU-mode to access siselect or sireg*. Furthermore, while vsiselect holds a value in the range 0x40-0x5F:

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_hyp_m_s_vsireg_illegal
tags.text: * An attempt to access any vsireg* from M or S mode raises an illegal-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_hyp_vs_access_sireg_conditional
tags.text: * An attempt from VS-mode to access any sireg* (really vsireg*) raises an illegal-instruction exception if menvcfg.CDE = 0, or a virtual-instruction exception if menvcfg.CDE = 1.

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_scountinhibit_exists
tags.text: Smcdeleg/Ssccfg defines a new scountinhibit register, a masked alias of mcountinhibit.

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_scountinhibit_delegated_rw
tags.text: For counters delegated to S-mode, the associated mcountinhibit bits can be accessed via scountinhibit

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_scountinhibit_nondelegated_ro
tags.text: For counters not delegated to S-mode, the associated bits in scountinhibit are read-only zero.

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_illegal_scountinhibit_cde0
tags.text: When menvcfg.CDE=0, attempts to access scountinhibit raise an illegal-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_illegal_scountinhibit_vs_vu
tags.text:
When Supervisor Counter Delegation
is enabled, attempts to access scountinhibit from VS-mode or VU-mode
raise a virtual-instruction  exception.

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_virtual_scountovf_vs_vu
tags.text: For implementations that support Smcdeleg/Ssccfg, Sscofpmf, and the H extension, when menvcfg.CDE=1, attempts to read scountovf from VS-mode or VU-mode raise a virtual-instruction  exception.

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_lcofi_mvip_mvien
tags.text: For implementations that support Smcdeleg, Sscofpmf, and Smaia, the local-counter-overflow interrupt (LCOFI) bit (bit 13) in each of CSRs mvip and mvien is implemented and writable.

tags.name: norm:ssccfg_lcofi_hvip_hvien
tags.text: For implementations that support Smcdeleg/Ssccfg, Sscofpmf, Smaia/Ssaia, and the H extension, the LCOFI bit (bit 13) in each of hvip and hvien is implemented and writable.

normative_rules.name: mcyclecfg_sz
normative_rules.chapter_name: Smcntrpmf Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering

tags.name: norm:mcyclecfg_sz
tags.text: mcyclecfg and minstretcfg are 64-bit registers

normative_rules.name: mcyclecfg_op
normative_rules.chapter_name: Smcntrpmf Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering

tags.name: norm:mcyclecfg_op
tags.text: configure privilege mode filtering for the cycle and instret counters, respectively.

normative_rules.name: all_xinh_zero
normative_rules.chapter_name: Smcntrpmf Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering

tags.name: norm:all_xinh_zero
tags.text: When all xINH bits are zero, event counting is enabled in all modes.

normative_rules.name: unimplemented_mode_bits
normative_rules.chapter_name: Smcntrpmf Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering

tags.name: norm:unimplemented_mode_bits
tags.text: For each bit in 61:58, if the associated privilege mode is not implemented, the bit is read-only zero.

normative_rules.name: rv32_high_access
normative_rules.chapter_name: Smcntrpmf Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering

tags.name: norm:rv32_high_access
tags.text: For RV32, bits 63:32 of mcyclecfg can be accessed via the mcyclecfgh CSR, and bits 63:32 of minstretcfg can be accessed via the minstretcfgh CSR.

normative_rules.name: csr_supervisor_access
normative_rules.chapter_name: Smcntrpmf Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering

tags.name: norm:csr_supervisor_access
tags.text: The content of these registers may be accessible from Supervisor level if the Smcdeleg/Ssccfg extensions are implemented.

normative_rules.name: counter_inhibited_behavior
normative_rules.chapter_name: Smcntrpmf Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering

tags.name: norm:counter_inhibited_behavior
tags.text: The fundamental behavior of cycle and instret is modified in that counting does not occur while executing in an inhibited privilege mode.

normative_rules.name: transition_counting_defined
normative_rules.chapter_name: Smcntrpmf Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering

tags.name: norm:transition_counting_defined
tags.text: Further, the following defines how transitions between a non-inhibited privilege mode and an inhibited privilege mode are counted.

normative_rules.name: cycle_counting
normative_rules.chapter_name: Smcntrpmf Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering

tags.name: norm:cycle_counting
tags.text: The cycle counter will simply count CPU cycles while the CPU is in a non-inhibited privilege mode.

normative_rules.name: instret_non_inhibited
normative_rules.chapter_name: Smcntrpmf Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering

tags.name: norm:instret_non_inhibited
tags.text: instructions that retire in a non-inhibited mode increment instret, and instructions that retire in an inhibited mode do not.

normative_rules.name: instret_exception
normative_rules.chapter_name: Smcntrpmf Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering

tags.name: norm:instret_exception
tags.text: The former are not considered to retire, and hence do not increment instret.

normative_rules.name: instret_xret
normative_rules.chapter_name: Smcntrpmf Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering

tags.name: norm:instret_xret
tags.text: The latter do retire, and should increment instret only if the originating privilege mode is not inhibited.

normative_rules.name: Smctr_Ssctr_dependencies
normative_rules.summary: Smctr and Ssctr depend on both the implementation of S-mode and the Sscsrind extension.

tags.name: norm:Smctr_Ssctr_depend
tags.text: Smctr and Ssctr depend on both the implementation of S-mode and the Sscsrind extension.

normative_rules.name: CTR_DEPTH
normative_rules.summary: CTR buffer depth depends on implementation maximum and software-selected configuration.

tags.name: norm:ctr_depth
tags.text: The number of records that can be held in the buffer depends upon both the implementation (the maximum supported depth) and the CTR configuration (the software selected depth).

tags.name: norm:sctrdepth_depth
tags.text: It is implementation-specific which DEPTH value(s) are supported.

normative_rules.name: Smctr_recording_criteria
normative_rules.summary: Only transfers meeting filtering criteria (privilege mode, transfer type) are recorded.

tags.name: norm:Smctr_recording_criteria
tags.text: Only qualified transfers are recorded.  Qualified transfers are those that meet the filtering criteria, which include the privilege mode and the transfer type.

normative_rules.name: Smctr_transfer_steps
normative_rules.summary: Recorded transfers are inserted at write pointer; overwrites on full buffer; optional RAS mode.

tags.name: norm:Smctr_transfer_steps
tags.text: Recorded transfers are inserted at the write pointer, which is then incremented, while older recorded transfers may be overwritten once the buffer is full. Or the user can enable RAS (Return Address Stack) emulation mode, where only function calls are recorded, and function returns pop the last call record.  The source PC, target PC, and some optional metadata (transfer type, elapsed cycles) are stored for each recorded transfer.

normative_rules.name: Smctr_CTR_CSR_interface
normative_rules.summary: CTR buffer accessed via indirect CSR; logical entry 0 = youngest transfer.

tags.name: norm:Smctr_CTR_CSR_interface
tags.text: The CTR buffer is accessible through an indirect CSR interface, such that software can specify which logical entry in the buffer it wishes to read or write.  Logical entry 0 always corresponds to the youngest recorded transfer, followed by entry 1 as the next youngest, and so on.

normative_rules.name: Smctr_scope
normative_rules.summary: Smctr covers machine-level CSRs, instructions, and behavior; Ssctr excludes machine-level only.

tags.name: norm:Smctr_scope
tags.text: The machine-level extension, Smctr, encompasses all newly added Control Status Registers (CSRs), instructions, and behavior modifications for a hart across all privilege levels.

tags.name: norm:Ssctr_transfer_steps
tags.text: The corresponding supervisor-level extension, Ssctr, is essentially identical to Smctr, except that it excludes machine-level CSRs and behaviors not intended to be directly accessible at the supervisor level.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl
normative_rules.summary: 64-bit R/W register to enable and configure CTR.

tags.name: norm:Ssctr_mctrctl_sz_acc_op
tags.text: The mctrctl register is a 64-bit read/write register that enables and configures the CTR capability.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_mode_en
normative_rules.summary: Enable transfer recording in selected privilege mode(s).

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_mode_op
tags.text: Enable transfer recording in the selected privileged mode(s).

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_rasemu
normative_rules.summary: Enables RAS (Return Address Stack) emulation mode.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_rasemu_op
tags.text: Enables RAS (Return Address Stack) Emulation Mode.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_mte
normative_rules.summary: Enables recording of traps to M-mode when M=0.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_mte_op
tags.text: Enables recording of traps to M-mode when M=0.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_ste
normative_rules.summary: Enables recording of traps to S-mode when S=0.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_ste_op
tags.text: Enables recording of traps to S-mode when S=0.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_bpfrz
normative_rules.summary: Sets sctrstatus.FROZEN on breakpoint trap to M or S mode.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_bpfrz_op
tags.text: Set sctrstatus.FROZEN on a breakpoint exception that traps to M-mode or S-mode.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_lcofifrz
normative_rules.summary: Sets sctrstatus.FROZEN on local-counter-overflow trap to M or S.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_lcofifrz_op
tags.text: Set sctrstatus.FROZEN on local-counter-overflow interrupt (LCOFI) that traps to M-mode or S-mode.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_excinh
normative_rules.summary: Inhibit recording of exceptions.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_excinh_op
tags.text: Inhibit recording of exceptions.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_intrinh
normative_rules.summary: Inhibit recording of interrupts.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_intrinh_op
tags.text: Inhibit recording of interrupts.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_tretinh
normative_rules.summary: Inhibit recording of trap returns.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_tretinh_op
tags.text: Inhibit recording of trap returns.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_ntbren
normative_rules.summary: Enable recording of not-taken branches.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_ntbren_op
tags.text: Enable recording of not-taken branches.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_tkbrinh
normative_rules.summary: Inhibit recording of taken branches.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_tkbrinh_op
tags.text: Inhibit recording of taken branches.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_indcallinh
normative_rules.summary: Inhibit recording of indirect calls.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_indcallinh_op
tags.text: Inhibit recording of indirect calls.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_dircallinh
normative_rules.summary: Inhibit recording of direct calls.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_dircallinh_op
tags.text: Inhibit recording of direct calls.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_indjmpinh
normative_rules.summary: Inhibit recording of indirect jumps (no linkage).

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_indjmpinh_op
tags.text: Inhibit recording of indirect jumps (without linkage).

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_dirjmpinh
normative_rules.summary: Inhibit recording of direct jumps (no linkage).

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_dirjmpinh_op
tags.text: Inhibit recording of direct jumps (without linkage).

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_corswapinh
normative_rules.summary: Inhibit recording of co-routine swaps.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_corswapinh_op
tags.text: Inhibit recording of co-routine swaps.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_retinh
normative_rules.summary: Inhibit recording of function returns.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_retinh_op
tags.text: Inhibit recording of function returns.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_indljmpinh
normative_rules.summary: Inhibit recording of other indirect jumps (with linkage).

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_indljmpinh_op
tags.text: Inhibit recording of other indirect jumps (with linkage).

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_dirljmpinh
normative_rules.summary: Inhibit recording of other direct jumps (with linkage).

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_dirljmpinh_op
tags.text: Inhibit recording of other direct jumps (with linkage).

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_custom
normative_rules.summary: WARL custom bits; value 0 = standard behavior.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_custom_op
tags.text: WARL bits designated for custom use.

normative_rules.name: mctrctl_impl
normative_rules.summary: All fields optional except M, S, U, BPFRZ; unimplemented = read-only 0.

tags.name: norm:mctrctl_impl
tags.text: All fields are optional except for M, S, U, and BPFRZ.  All unimplemented fields are read-only 0, while all implemented fields are writable.  If the Sscofpmf extension is implemented, LCOFIFRZ must be writable.

normative_rules.name: sctrctl
normative_rules.summary: Supervisor access subset of mctrctl; M and MTE excluded.

tags.name: norm:Ssctr_sctrctl_op
tags.text: The sctrctl register provides supervisor mode access to a subset of mctrctl.

tags.name: norm:Ssctr_sctrctl_acc
tags.text: Bits 2 and 9 in sctrctl are read-only 0. As a result, the M and MTE fields in mctrctl are not accessible through sctrctl.  All other mctrctl fields are accessible through sctrctl.

normative_rules.name: vsctrctl
normative_rules.summary: VS-mode’s version of sctrctl; used when V=1.

tags.name: norm:Ssctr_vsctrctl_sz_acc_op
tags.text: If the H extension is implemented, the vsctrctl register is a 64-bit read/write register that is VS-mode's version of supervisor register sctrctl.  When V=1, vsctrctl substitutes for the usual sctrctl, so instructions that normally read or modify sctrctl actually access vsctrctl instead.

normative_rules.name: vsctr-s
normative_rules.summary: Enable transfer recording in VS-mode.

tags.name: norm:vsctr-s_op
tags.text: Enable transfer recording in VS-mode.

normative_rules.name: vsctrctl-u
normative_rules.summary: Enable transfer recording in VU-mode.

tags.name: norm:vsctrctl-u_op
tags.text: Enable transfer recording in VU-mode.

normative_rules.name: vsctrctl-ste
normative_rules.summary: Enables recording of traps to VS-mode when S=0.

tags.name: norm:vsctrctl-ste_op
tags.text: Enables recording of traps to VS-mode when S=0.

normative_rules.name: vsctrctl-bpfrz
normative_rules.summary: Sets FROZEN on breakpoint trap to VS-mode.

tags.name: norm:vsctrctl-bpfrz_op
tags.text: Set sctrstatus.FROZEN on a breakpoint exception that traps to VS-mode.

normative_rules.name: vsctrctl-lcofifrz
normative_rules.summary: Sets FROZEN on local-counter-overflow trap to VS-mode.

tags.name: norm:vsctrctl-lcofifrz_op
tags.text: Set sctrstatus.FROZEN on local-counter-overflow interrupt (LCOFI) that traps to VS-mode.

normative_rules.name: sctrdepth
normative_rules.summary: 32-bit register selecting CTR buffer depth.

tags.name: norm:sctrdepth
tags.text: The 32-bit sctrdepth register specifies the depth of the CTR buffer.

tags.name: norm:sctrdepth_depth
tags.text: It is implementation-specific which DEPTH value(s) are supported.

normative_rules.name: SCTRDEPTH_DEPTH
normative_rules.summary: WARL field encoding CTR buffer depth (16–256, reserved encodings).

tags.name: norm:sctrdepth_depth_op0
tags.text: WARL field that selects the depth of the CTR buffer.

tags.name: norm:sctrdepth_depth_op1
tags.text: The depth of the CTR buffer dictates the number of entries to which the hardware records transfers. For a depth of N, the hardware records transfers to entries 0..N-1. All <<_entry_registers, Entry Registers>> read as '0' and are read-only when the selected entry is in the range N to 255.  When the depth is increased, the newly accessible entries contain unspecified but legal values.

tags.name: norm:sctrdepth_depth
tags.text: It is implementation-specific which DEPTH value(s) are supported.

tags.name: norm:sctrdepth_depth_op0
tags.text: WARL field that selects the depth of the CTR buffer.

normative_rules.name: sctrdepth_mode
normative_rules.summary: VS/VU-mode access to sctrdepth raises virtual-instruction exception unless enabled.

tags.name: norm:sctrdepth_mode
tags.text: Attempts to access sctrdepth from VS-mode or VU-mode raise a virtual-instruction exception, unless CTR state enable access restrictions apply.

normative_rules.name: sctrstatus
normative_rules.summary: 32-bit register with CTR status; active when privilege mode enabled and not frozen.

tags.name: norm:sctrstatus
tags.text: The 32-bit sctrstatus register grants access to CTR status information and is updated by the hardware whenever CTR is active.  CTR is active when the current privilege mode is enabled for recording and CTR is not frozen.

normative_rules.name: sctrstatus_wrptr
normative_rules.summary: WARL field pointing to next CTR buffer entry; wraps on depth.

tags.name: norm:sctrstatus_wrptr
tags.text: WARL field that indicates the physical CTR buffer entry to be written next.  It is incremented after new transfers are recorded (see <<Behavior>>), though there are exceptions when xctrctl.RASEMU=1, see <<RAS (Return Address Stack) Emulation Mode>>.  For a given CTR depth (where depth = 2^(DEPTH+4)^), WRPTR wraps to 0 on an increment when the value matches depth-1, and to depth-1 on a decrement when the value is 0.  Bits above those needed to represent depth-1 (e.g., bits 7:4 for a depth of 16) are read-only 0. On depth changes, WRPTR holds an unspecified but legal value.

tags.name: norm:sctrstatus_wrptr
tags.text: WARL field that indicates the physical CTR buffer entry to be written next.  It is incremented after new transfers are recorded (see <<Behavior>>), though there are exceptions when xctrctl.RASEMU=1, see <<RAS (Return Address Stack) Emulation Mode>>.  For a given CTR depth (where depth = 2^(DEPTH+4)^), WRPTR wraps to 0 on an increment when the value matches depth-1, and to depth-1 on a decrement when the value is 0.  Bits above those needed to represent depth-1 (e.g., bits 7:4 for a depth of 16) are read-only 0. On depth changes, WRPTR holds an unspecified but legal value.

normative_rules.name: sctrstatus_frozen_op
normative_rules.summary: FROZEN bit inhibits transfer recording.

tags.name: norm:sctrstatus_frozen_op
tags.text: Inhibit transfer recording.

normative_rules.name: sctrstatus_acc
normative_rules.summary: Undefined bits WPRI, read-only 0 unless custom extension.

tags.name: norm:Ssctr_sctrstatus_acc
tags.text:
Undefined bits in sctrstatus are WPRI. Status fields may be added by future extensions,
and software should ignore but preserve any fields that it does not recognize.  Undefined  bits must be implemented as read-only 0, unless a custom extension is implemented and enabled

normative_rules.name: siselect_acc_op
normative_rules.summary: Indirect CSR index range 0x200..0x2FF reserved for CTR entries.

tags.name: norm:siselect_acc_op
tags.text: The siselect index range 0x200 through 0x2FF is reserved for CTR logical entries 0 through 255. When siselect holds a value in this range, sireg provides access to ctrsource, sireg2 provides access to ctrtarget, and sireg3 provides access to ctrdata.  sireg4, sireg5, and sireg6 are read-only 0.

normative_rules.name: vsiselect_op
normative_rules.summary: VS-mode indirect entry registers mirror S-mode.

tags.name: norm:vsiselect_op
tags.text: When vsiselect holds a value in 0x200..0x2FF, the vsireg* registers provide access to the same CTR entry register state as the analogous sireg* registers.  There is not a separate set of entry registers for V=1.

normative_rules.name: ctrsource_op
normative_rules.summary: ctrsource holds source PC or epc of trap.

tags.name: norm:ctrsource_op
tags.text: The ctrsource register contains the source program counter, which is the pc of the recorded control transfer instruction, or the epc of the recorded trap.

normative_rules.name: ctrsource_validbit
normative_rules.summary: V bit indicates valid data in ctrsource/ctrtarget/ctrdata.

tags.name: norm:ctrsource_ctrtartget_ctrdata_Vbit
tags.text: The valid (V) bit is set by the hardware when a transfer is recorded in the selected CTR buffer entry, and implies that data in ctrsource, ctrtarget, and ctrdata is valid for this entry.

normative_rules.name: ctrsource_sz_acc
normative_rules.summary: MXLEN WARL register; must hold valid PC addresses; zero-extended if XLEN<MXLEN.

tags.name: norm:Ssctr_ctrsource_sz_acc_op
tags.text: ctrsource is an MXLEN-bit WARL register that must be able to hold all valid virtual or physical addresses that can serve as a pc. It need not be able to hold any invalid addresses; implementations may convert an invalid address into a valid address that the register is capable of holding.  When XLEN < MXLEN, both explicit writes (by software) and implicit writes (for recorded transfers) will be zero-extended.

tags.name: norm:Ssctr_ctrsource_sz_acc_op
tags.text: ctrsource is an MXLEN-bit WARL register that must be able to hold all valid virtual or physical addresses that can serve as a pc. It need not be able to hold any invalid addresses; implementations may convert an invalid address into a valid address that the register is capable of holding.  When XLEN < MXLEN, both explicit writes (by software) and implicit writes (for recorded transfers) will be zero-extended.

normative_rules.name: ctrtarget_op
normative_rules.summary: ctrtarget holds destination PC of recorded transfer.

tags.name: norm:ctrtarget_op
tags.text:
The ctrtarget register contains the target (destination) program counter
of the recorded transfer.

tags.name: norm:ctrtarget_pc_next_br
tags.text: For a not-taken branch, ctrtarget holds the PC of the next sequential instruction following the branch.

normative_rules.name: ctrtarget_misp
normative_rules.summary: Optional MISP bit shows branch misprediction; read-only 0 if unimplemented.

tags.name: norm:ctrtarget_misp
tags.text:
The optional MISP bit is set by the hardware
when the recorded transfer is an instruction whose target or
taken/not-taken direction was mispredicted by the branch predictor. MISP
is read-only 0 when not implemented.

normative_rules.name: ctrtarget_sz_acc
normative_rules.summary: MXLEN WARL register; holds valid PC addresses; zero-extended if XLEN<MXLEN.

tags.name: norm:ctrtarget_sz_acc
tags.text: ctrtarget is an MXLEN-bit WARL register that must be able to hold all valid virtual or physical addresses that can serve as a pc. It need not be able to hold any invalid addresses; implementations may convert an invalid address into a valid address that the register is capable of holding.  When XLEN < MXLEN, both explicit writes (by software) and implicit writes (by recorded transfers) will be zero-extended.

tags.name: norm:ctrtarget_sz_acc
tags.text: ctrtarget is an MXLEN-bit WARL register that must be able to hold all valid virtual or physical addresses that can serve as a pc. It need not be able to hold any invalid addresses; implementations may convert an invalid address into a valid address that the register is capable of holding.  When XLEN < MXLEN, both explicit writes (by software) and implicit writes (by recorded transfers) will be zero-extended.

normative_rules.name: ctrdata_sz_acc
normative_rules.summary: ctrdata holds metadata; mandatory register, fields optional, 64-bit.

tags.name: norm:ctrdata_sz_acc
tags.text:
The ctrdata register contains metadata for the recorded transfer. This
register must be implemented, though all fields within it are optional.
Unimplemented fields are read-only 0.  ctrdata is a 64-bit register.

normative_rules.name: CTRDATA_CC_SUPPORTED
normative_rules.summary: ctrdata register may optionally include a count of CPU cycles

tags.name: norm:ctrdata_cc_supported
tags.text: The ctrdata register may optionally include a count of CPU cycles elapsed since the prior CTR record.  The elapsed cycle count value is represented by the CC field, which has a 12-bit mantissa component (Cycle Count Mantissa, or CCM) and a 4-bit exponent component (Cycle Count Exponent, or CCE).

normative_rules.name: ctrdata_type
normative_rules.summary: TYPE encodes control transfer type; 0 if not supported.

tags.name: norm:ctrdata_type
tags.text: Identifies the type of the control flow transfer recorded in the entry, using the encodings listed in xref:transfer-type-defs[xrefstyle=short].  Implementations that do not support this field will report 0.

normative_rules.name: ctrdata_ccv
normative_rules.summary: Cycle Count Valid bit.

tags.name: norm:ctrdata_ccv
tags.text: Cycle Count Valid.

normative_rules.name: ctrdata_cc
normative_rules.summary: Cycle Count value (mantissa + exponent).

tags.name: norm:ctrdata_cc
tags.text:
Cycle Count, composed of the Cycle Count Exponent (CCE, in
CC[15:12]) and Cycle Count Mantissa (CCM, in CC[11:0]).

normative_rules.name: ctrdata_undef
normative_rules.summary: Undefined ctrdata bits WPRI, read-only 0 unless custom extension.

tags.name: norm:ctrdata_undef
tags.text: Undefined bits in ctrdata are WPRI. Undefined bits must be implemented as read-only 0, unless a <<_custom_extensions, custom extension>> is implemented and enabled.

normative_rules.name: sctrclr
normative_rules.summary: Instruction clears entry registers and cycle counter.

tags.name: norm:sctrclr_op1
tags.text: Zeroes all  CTR <<_entry_registers, Entry Registers>>, for all DEPTH values

tags.name: norm:sctrclr_op2
tags.text: Zeroes the CTR cycle counter and CCV

tags.name: norm:sctrclr_acc
tags.text: Any read of ctrsource, ctrtarget, or ctrdata that follows SCTRCLR, such that it precedes the next qualified control transfer, will return the value 0.  Further, the first recorded transfer following SCTRCLR will have ctrdata.CCV=0.

tags.name: norm:sctrclr_exceptions
tags.text: SCTRCLR raises an illegal-instruction exception in U-mode, and a virtual-instruction exception in VU-mode, unless CTR state enable access restrictions apply.

normative_rules.name: mstateen_ctr
normative_rules.summary: mstateen0.CTR controls CTR access for lower modes.

tags.name: norm:mstateen_ctr1
tags.text: When mstateen0.CTR=1, accesses to CTR register state behave as described in <<CSRs>> and <<Entry Registers>> above, while SCTRCLR behaves as described in <<Supervisor CTR Clear Instruction>>.

tags.name: norm:mstateen_ctr0
tags.text: When mstateen0.CTR=0 and the privilege mode is less privileged than M-mode, the following operations raise an illegal-instruction exception:

tags.name: norm:mstateen_ctr0_execpt1
tags.text: Attempts to access sctrctl, vsctrctl, sctrdepth, or sctrstatus

tags.name: norm:mstateen_ctr0_execpt2
tags.text: Attempts to access sireg* when siselect is in 0x200..0x2FF, or vsireg* when vsiselect is in 0x200..0x2FF

tags.name: norm:mstateen_ctr0_execpt3
tags.text: Execution of the SCTRCLR instruction

tags.name: norm:mstateen_ctr0_qualified_transfer
tags.text: When mstateen0.CTR=0, qualified control transfers executed in privilege modes less privileged than M-mode will continue to implicitly update entry registers and sctrstatus.

normative_rules.name: hstateen_ctr
normative_rules.summary: hstateen0.CTR controls VS-mode access to CTR; read-only 0 if mstateen0.CTR=0.

tags.name: norm:hstateen_ctr
tags.text: If the H extension is implemented and mstateen0.CTR=1, the hstateen0.CTR bit controls access to supervisor CTR state when V=1.  This state includes sctrctl (really vsctrctl), sctrstatus, and sireg* (really vsireg*) when siselect (really vsiselect) is in 0x200..0x2FF.  hstateen0.CTR is read-only 0 when mstateen0.CTR=0.

tags.name: norm:hstateen_vs
tags.text: When mstateen0.CTR=1 and hstateen0.CTR=1, VS-mode accesses to supervisor CTR state behave as described in <<CSRs>> and <<Entry Registers>> above, while SCTRCLR behaves as described in <<Supervisor CTR Clear Instruction>>.  When mstateen0.CTR=1 and hstateen0.CTR=0, both VS-mode accesses to supervisor CTR state and VS-mode execution of SCTRCLR raise a virtual-instruction exception.

normative_rules.name: hstateen0_CTR0-V1_update_sctrstatus
normative_rules.summary: hstateen0.CTR=0 and V=1 updates sctrstatus implicitly

tags.name: norm:hstateen0_CTR0-V1_op
tags.text: When hstateen0.CTR=0, qualified control transfers executed while V=1 will continue to implicitly update entry registers and sctrstatus.

normative_rules.name: ctr_behavior
normative_rules.summary: CTR records transfers if mode enabled, type not inhibited, not frozen, and retired.

tags.name: norm:ctr_behavior
tags.text: CTR records qualified control transfers.  Control transfers are qualified if they meet the following criteria:

tags.name: norm:ctr_behavior_criteria0
tags.text: The current privilege mode is enabled

tags.name: norm:ctr_behavior_criteri1
tags.text: The transfer type is not inhibited

tags.name: norm:ctr_behavior_criteria2
tags.text: sctrstatus.FROZEN is not set

tags.name: norm:ctr_behavior_criteria3
tags.text: The transfer completes/retires

normative_rules.name: ctr_stack
normative_rules.summary: CTR buffer operates as circular stack; overwrites oldest entries when full.

tags.name: norm:ctr_stack
tags.text: Such qualified transfers update the <<_entry_registers, Entry Registers>> at logical entry 0.  As a result, older entries are pushed down the stack; the record previously in logical entry 0 moves to logical entry 1, the record in logical entry 1 moves to logical entry 2, and so on.  If the CTR buffer is full, the oldest recorded entry (previously at entry depth-1) is lost.

normative_rules.name: ctr_validbit
normative_rules.summary: Recorded transfers set ctrsource.V=1 and update fields.

tags.name: norm:ctr_validbit
tags.text: Recorded transfers will set the ctrsource.V bit to 1, and will update all implemented record fields.

normative_rules.name: trap_rules
normative_rules.summary: Defines when traps/trap returns are recorded depending on enabled/disabled modes.

tags.name: norm:trap_enabled
tags.text: Traps between enabled privilege modes are recorded as normal.

tags.name: norm:trap_disabled_src
tags.text: Traps from a disabled privilege mode to an enabled privilege mode are partially recorded, such that the ctrsource.PC is 0.

tags.name: norm:trap_disabled_tgt
tags.text: Traps from an enabled mode to a disabled mode, known as external traps, are not recorded by default.

tags.name: norm:trapret_enabled
tags.text: Trap returns between enabled privilege modes are recorded as normal.

tags.name: norm:trapret_to_disabled
tags.text: Trap returns from an enabled mode back to a disabled mode are partially recorded, such that ctrtarget.PC is 0.

tags.name: norm:trapret_from_disabled
tags.text: Trap returns from a disabled mode to an enabled mode are not recorded.

tags.name: norm:trap_ee
tags.text: Recorded

tags.name: norm:trap_ed
tags.text: External trap.  Not recorded by default,

tags.name: norm:trap_de
tags.text: Recorded, ctrsource.PC is 0.

tags.name: norm:trap_dd
tags.text: Not recorded.

tags.name: norm:trapret_ee
tags.text: Recorded.

tags.name: norm:trapret_ed
tags.text: Recorded, ctrtarget.PC is 0.

tags.name: norm:trapret_de
tags.text: Not recorded.

tags.name: norm:trapret_dd
tags.text: Not recorded.

normative_rules.name: debug_recording_inhibited
normative_rules.summary: Debug mode inhibits recording

tags.name: norm:debug_recording_inhibited
tags.text: Recording in Debug Mode is always inhibited. Transfers into and out of Debug Mode are never recorded.

normative_rules.name: exttrap
normative_rules.summary: External traps not recorded by default; can opt-in with TE bits.

tags.name: norm:exttrap_def
tags.text: External traps are traps from a privilege mode enabled for CTR recording to a privilege mode that is not enabled for CTR recording.  By default external traps are not recorded, but privileged software running in the target mode of the trap can opt-in to allowing CTR to record external traps into that mode. The xctrctl.xTE bits allow M-mode, S-mode, and VS-mode to opt-in separately.

tags.name: norm:exttrap_requirements
tags.text: External trap recording depends not only on the target mode, but on any intervening modes, which are modes that are more privileged than the source mode but less privileged than the target mode.  Not only must the external trap enable bit for the target mode be set, but the external trap enable bit(s) for any intervening modes must also be set.

tags.name: norm:exttrap_us
tags.text: sctrctl.STE

tags.name: norm:exttrap_um
tags.text: mctrctl.MTE, sctrctl.STE

tags.name: norm:exttrap_sm
tags.text: mctrctl.MTE

tags.name: norm:exttrap_vuvs
tags.text: vsctrctl.STE

tags.name: norm:exttrap_vuhs
tags.text: sctrctl.STE, vsctrctl.STE

tags.name: norm:exttrap_vum
tags.text: mctrctl.MTE, sctrctl.STE, vsctrctl.STE

tags.name: norm:exttrap_vshs
tags.text: sctrctl.STE

tags.name: norm:exttrap_vsm
tags.text: mctrctl.MTE, sctrctl.STE

tags.name: norm:exttrap_ctrtarget0
tags.text: In records for external traps, the ctrtarget.PC is 0.

tags.name: norm:exttrap_implreq
tags.text: If external trap recording is implemented, mctrctl.MTE and sctrctl.STE must be implemented, while vsctrctl.STE must be implemented if the H extension is implemented.

normative_rules.name: ttf_default
normative_rules.summary: Default is to record all transfers unless filtered by type bits.

tags.name: norm:ttf_default
tags.text: Default CTR behavior, when all transfer type filter bits (xctrctl[47:32]) are unimplemented or 0, is to record all control transfers within enabled privileged modes. By setting transfer type filter bits, software can opt out of recording select transfer types, or opt into recording non-default operations.  All transfer type filter bits are optional.

normative_rules.name: ttype
normative_rules.summary: Transfer type encodings 0–15 with specific meanings.

tags.name: norm:ttype0
tags.text: Not used by CTR

tags.name: norm:ttype1
tags.text: Exception

tags.name: norm:ttype2
tags.text: Interrupt

tags.name: norm:ttype3
tags.text: Trap return

tags.name: norm:ttype4
tags.text: Not-taken branch

tags.name: norm:ttype5
tags.text: Taken branch

tags.name: norm:ttype8
tags.text: Indirect call

tags.name: norm:ttype9
tags.text: Direct call

tags.name: norm:ttype10
tags.text: Indirect jump (without linkage)

tags.name: norm:ttype11
tags.text: Direct jump (without linkage)

tags.name: norm:ttype12
tags.text: Co-routine swap

tags.name: norm:ttype13
tags.text: Function return

tags.name: norm:ttype14
tags.text: Other indirect jump (with linkage)

tags.name: norm:ttype15
tags.text: Other direct jump (with linkage)

normative_rules.name: varios_jump_enc
normative_rules.summary: encodings for various indirect jumps with linkage.

tags.name: norm:various_jump_enc
tags.text: Encodings 8 through 15 refer to various encodings of jump instructions.  The types are distinguished as described below.

normative_rules.name: ccounter
normative_rules.summary: Cycle counter increments with mcycle; reset on ctrctl writes and SCTRCLR; CCV/CC fields define elapsed cycles.

tags.name: norm:ccounter_inc
tags.text: The elapsed cycle counter (CtrCycleCounter) increments at the same rate as the mcycle counter.  Only cycles while CTR is active are counted, where active implies that the current privilege mode is enabled for recording and CTR is not frozen.  The CC field is encoded such that CCE holds 0 if the CtrCycleCounter value is less than 4096, otherwise it holds the index of the most significant one bit in the CtrCycleCounter value, minus 11.  CCM holds CtrCycleCounter bits CCE+10:CCE-1.

tags.name: norm:ccounter_reset
tags.text: The CtrCycleCounter is reset on writes to xctrctl, and on execution of SCTRCLR, to ensure that any accumulated cycle counts do not persist across a context switch.

tags.name: norm:ccounter_impl
tags.text:
An implementation that supports cycle counting must implement CCV and all
CCM bits, but may implement 0..4 exponent bits in CCE. Unimplemented CCE
bits are read-only 0. For implementations that support transfer type
filtering, it is recommended to implement at least 3 exponent bits. This
allows capturing the full latency of most functions, when recording only
calls and returns.

tags.name: norm:ccounter_sat
tags.text: The CC value saturates when all implemented bits in CCM and CCE are 1.

tags.name: norm:ccounter_ccv
tags.text: The CC value is valid only when the Cycle Count Valid (CCV) bit is set.  If CCV=0, the CC value might not hold the correct count of elapsed active cycles since the last recorded transfer.  The next record will have CCV=0 after a write to xctrctl, or execution of SCTRCLR, since CtrCycleCounter is reset.  CCV should additionally be cleared after any other implementation-specific scenarios where active cycles might not be counted in CtrCycleCounter.

normative_rules.name: ctrctl_rasemu
normative_rules.summary: RASEMU bit enables RAS emulation mode with altered recording behavior.

tags.name: norm:ctrctl_rasemu_op
tags.text: When the optional xctrctl.RASEMU bit is implemented and set to 1, transfer recording behavior is altered to emulate the behavior of a return-address stack (RAS).

normative_rules.name: freeze
normative_rules.summary: FROZEN bit inhibits CTR recording; set on LCOFI or breakpoint traps.

tags.name: norm:sctrstatus_frozen_set
tags.text:
When sctrctl.LCOFIFRZ=1 and a local-counter-overflow interrupt
(LCOFI) traps (as a result of an HPM counter overflow) to M-mode or to S-mode, sctrstatus.FROZEN is set by hardware. This inhibits CTR recording until software clears FROZEN. The LCOFI trap itself is not recorded.

tags.name: norm:freeze_bp
tags.text: Similarly, on a breakpoint exception that traps to M-mode or S-mode with sctrctl.BPFRZ=1, FROZEN is set by hardware. The breakpoint exception itself is not recorded.

tags.name: norm:freeze_vs
tags.text: If the H extension is implemented, freeze behavior for LCOFIs and breakpoint exceptions that trap to VS-mode is determined by the LCOFIFRZ and BPFRZ values, respectively, in vsctrctl.  This includes virtual LCOFIs pended by a hypervisor.

normative_rules.name: custom_bits
normative_rules.summary: Non-zero custom bits in ctrctl enable custom extensions; must revert to standard when zero.

tags.name: norm:custom_bits
tags.text: Any custom CTR extension must be associated with a non-zero value within the designated custom bits in xctrctl.  When the custom bits hold a non-zero value that enables a custom extension, the extension may alter standard CTR behavior, and may define new custom status fields within sctrstatus or the CTR <<_entry_registers, Entry Registers>>.  All custom status fields, and standard status fields whose behavior is altered by the custom extension, must revert to standard behavior when the custom bits hold zero.  This includes read-only 0 behavior for any bits undefined by any implemented standard extensions.

normative_rules.name: Smdbltrp_with_Smrnmi_op
normative_rules.summary: When Smrnmi is implemented, a double trap in M-mode invokes the RNMI handler to handle the critical error

tags.name: norm:Smdbltrp_with_Smrnmi_op
tags.text:
When the Smrnmi extension (<<rnmi>>) is implemented, it enables
invocation of the RNMI handler on a double trap in M-mode to handle the
critical error.

tags.name: norm:smepmp_no_mml_limit
tags.text: Without the Smepmp extension, it is not possible for a PMP rule to be enforced only on non-Machine modes and denied on Machine mode, in order to allow access to a memory region solely by less-privileged modes.

tags.name: norm:smepmp_no_mml_behavior
tags.text: It is only possible to have a locked rule that will be enforced on all modes, or a rule that will be enforced on non-Machine modes and be ignored by Machine mode.

tags.name: norm:smepmp_machine_unlimited
tags.text: So for any physical memory region which is not protected with a Locked rule, Machine mode has unlimited access, including the ability to execute it.

tags.name: norm:smepmp_attack_surface
tags.text: Without being able to protect less-privileged modes from Machine mode, it is not possible to prevent the mentioned attack vector.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_fields_exist
tags.text:
this
extension introduces the RLB, MMWP, and MML fields in the mseccfg CSR
and their associated rules.

tags.name: norm:mml_truth_table
tags.text: The physical memory protection rules when mseccfg.MML is set to 1 are summarized in the truth table below.

tags.name: norm:mseccfg_locking
tags.text: Since all fields defined in mseccfg as part of this extension are locked when set (MMWP/MML) or locked when cleared (RLB), software can't poll them for determining the presence of Smepmp.

tags.name: norm:bootrom_discovery
tags.text: It is expected that BootROM will set mseccfg.MMWP and/or mseccfg.MML during early boot, before jumping to the firmware, so that the firmware will be able to determine the presence of Smepmp by reading mseccfg and checking the state of mseccfg.MMWP and mseccfg.MML.

tags.name: norm:smstateen_mmode_rv64_csrs
tags.text: For RV64 harts, this extension adds four new 64-bit CSRs at machine level: mstateen0 (Machine State Enable 0), mstateen1, mstateen2, and mstateen3.

tags.name: norm:smstateen_smode_rv64_csrs
tags.text: If supervisor mode is implemented, another four CSRs are defined at supervisor level: sstateen0, sstateen1, sstateen2, and sstateen3.

tags.name: norm:smstateen_hypervisor_rv64_csrs
tags.text: And if the hypervisor extension is implemented, another set of CSRs is added: hstateen0, hstateen1, hstateen2, and hstateen3.

tags.name: norm:smstateen_rv32_upper_bits_csrs
tags.text: For RV32, there are CSR addresses for accessing the upper 32 bits of corresponding machine-level and hypervisor CSRs: mstateen0h, mstateen1h, mstateen2h, mstateen3h, hstateen0h, hstateen1h, hstateen2h, and hstateen3h.

tags.name: norm:stateen_op
tags.text:
The stateen registers at each level control access to state at all
less-privileged levels, but not at its own level.

tags.name: norm:smstateen_illegal_state_access
tags.text:
Just
as with the counteren CSRs, when a stateen CSR prevents access to state by
less-privileged levels, an attempt in one of those privilege modes to execute
an instruction that would read or write the protected state raises an illegal-instruction
exception, or, if executing in VS or VU mode and the circumstances
for a virtual-instruction  exception apply, raises a virtual-instruction
exception instead of an illegal-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:smstateen_implicit_state_update
tags.text: When a stateen CSR prevents access to state for a privilege mode, attempting to execute in that privilege mode an instruction that implicitly updates the state without reading it may or may not raise an illegal-instruction or virtual-instruction exception. Such cases must be disambiguated by being explicitly specified one way or the other.

tags.name: norm:sstateen_user_access_control
tags.text:
Each bit of a supervisor-level sstateen CSR controls user-level access (from
U-mode or VU-mode) to an extension's state.

tags.name: norm:sstateen_bit_allocation
tags.text:
The intention is to allocate the
bits of sstateen CSRs starting at the least-significant end, bit 0, through to
bit 31, and then on to the next-higher-numbered sstateen CSR.

tags.name: norm:sstateen_bit_correspondence
tags.text:
For every bit with a defined purpose in an sstateen CSR, the same bit is
defined in the matching mstateen CSR to control access below machine level to
the same state.

tags.name: norm:mstateen_bit_allocation
tags.text:
The intention is to allocate bits
for this purpose starting at the most-significant end, bit 63, through to bit
32, and then on to the next-higher mstateen CSR.

tags.name: norm:mstateen_bit_encroachment
tags.text:
If the rate that bits are
being allocated from the least-significant end for sstateen CSRs is
sufficiently low, allocation from the most-significant end of mstateen CSRs may
be allowed to encroach on the lower 32 bits before jumping to the next-higher
mstateen CSR.

tags.name: norm:sstateen_encroachment_bits_roz
tags.text:
In that case, the bit positions of "encroaching" bits will remain
forever read-only zeros in the matching sstateen CSRs.

tags.name: norm:hstateen_encoding
tags.text: With the hypervisor extension, the hstateen CSRs have identical encodings to the mstateen CSRs, except controlling accesses for a virtual machine (from VS and VU modes).

tags.name: norm:stateen_warl_access
tags.text: Each standard-defined bit of a stateen CSR is WARL and may be read-only zero or one, subject to the following conditions.

tags.name: norm:stateen_unimplemented_state_roz
tags.text:
Bits in any stateen CSR that are defined to control state that a hart doesn't
implement are read-only zeros for that hart.

tags.name: norm:stateen_reserved_roz
tags.text:
Likewise, all reserved bits not
yet given a defined meaning are also read-only zeros.

tags.name: norm:sstateen_vsmode_access_roz
tags.text:
For every
bit in an hstateen CSR that is zero (whether read-only zero or set to zero),
the same bit appears as read-only zero in sstateen when accessed in VS-mode.

tags.name: norm:sstateen_ro1_bits
tags.text:
A bit in a supervisor-level sstateen CSR cannot be read-only one unless the
same bit is read-only one in the matching mstateen CSR and, if it exists, in
the matching hstateen CSR.

tags.name: norm:hstateen_ro1_bits
tags.text:
A bit in an hstateen CSR cannot be read-only one
unless the same bit is read-only one in the matching mstateen CSR.

tags.name: norm:mstateen_zero_initialization
tags.text: On reset, all writable mstateen bits are initialized by the hardware to zeros.

tags.name: norm:hstateen_sstateen_zero_initialization
tags.text:
If machine-level software changes these values, it is responsible for
initializing the corresponding writable bits of the hstateen and sstateen CSRs
to zeros too.

tags.name: norm:mstateen_bit_63_op
tags.text:
For each mstateen CSR, bit 63 is defined to control access to the
matching sstateen and hstateen CSRs.

tags.name: norm:hstateen_bit_63_op
tags.text:
Likewise, bit 63 of each hstateen
correspondingly controls access to the matching sstateen CSR.

tags.name: norm:mstateen_bit_63_roz
tags.text:
Bit 63 of each mstateen CSR may be read-only zero only if the hypervisor
extension is not implemented and the matching supervisor-level sstateen CSR is
all read-only zeros.

tags.name: norm:hstateen_bit_63_writable
tags.text:
Bit 63 of each hstateen CSR is always writable (not
read-only).

tags.name: norm:stateen0_c_op
tags.text: The C bit controls access to any and all custom state.

tags.name: norm:stateen0_fcsr_op
tags.text:
The FCSR bit controls access to fcsr for the case when floating-point
instructions operate on x registers instead of f registers as specified by
the Zfinx and related extensions (Zdinx, etc.).

tags.name: norm:stateen0_fcsr0_misa_f0_illegal_fpu_instr
tags.text:
For convenience, when the stateen CSRs are implemented and
misa.F = 0, then if the FCSR bit of a controlling stateen0 CSR is zero, all
floating-point instructions cause an illegal-instruction exception (or virtual-instruction
exception, if relevant), as though they all access fcsr, regardless of
whether they really do.

tags.name: norm:stateen0_jvt_op
tags.text: The JVT bit controls access to the jvt CSR provided by the Zcmt extension.

tags.name: norm:mstateen0_se0_op
tags.text:
The SE0 bit in mstateen0 controls access to the hstateen0, hstateen0h,
and the sstateen0 CSRs.

tags.name: norm:hstateen0_SE0_op
tags.text:
The SE0 bit in hstateen0 controls access to the
sstateen0 CSR.

tags.name: norm:mstateen0_envcfg_op
tags.text:
The ENVCFG bit in mstateen0 controls access to the henvcfg, henvcfgh,
and the senvcfg CSRs.

tags.name: norm:hstateen0_envcfg_op
tags.text:
The ENVCFG bit in hstateen0 controls access to the
senvcfg CSRs.

tags.name: norm:mstateen0_csrind_op
tags.text:
The CSRIND bit in mstateen0 controls access to the siselect, sireg*,
vsiselect, and the vsireg* CSRs provided by the Sscsrind extensions.

tags.name: norm:hstateen0_csrind_op
tags.text:
The CSRIND bit in hstateen0 controls access to the siselect and the
sireg*, (really vsiselect and vsireg*) CSRs provided by the Sscsrind
extensions.

tags.name: norm:mstateen0_imsic_op
tags.text:
The IMSIC bit in mstateen0 controls access to the IMSIC state, including
CSRs stopei and vstopei, provided by the Ssaia extension.

tags.name: norm:hstateen0_imsic_op
tags.text:
The IMSIC bit in
hstateen0 controls access to the guest IMSIC state, including CSRs stopei
(really vstopei), provided by the Ssaia extension.

tags.name: norm:mstateen0_aia_op
tags.text:
The AIA bit in mstateen0 controls access to all state introduced by the
Ssaia extension and not controlled by either the CSRIND or the IMSIC
bits.

tags.name: norm:hstateen0_aia_op
tags.text:
The AIA bit in hstateen0 controls access to all state introduced by the
Ssaia extension and not controlled by either the CSRIND or the IMSIC
bits of hstateen0.

tags.name: norm:mstateen0_context_op
tags.text:
The CONTEXT bit in mstateen0 controls access to the scontext and
hcontext CSRs provided by the Sdtrig extension.

tags.name: norm:hstateen0_context_op
tags.text:
The CONTEXT bit in
hstateen0 controls access to the scontext CSR provided by the Sdtrig
extension.

tags.name: norm:mstateen0_p1p13_op
tags.text: The P1P13 bit in mstateen0 controls access to the hedelegh introduced by Privileged Specification Version 1.13.

tags.name: norm:mstateen0_srmcfg_op
tags.text: The SRMCFG bit in mstateen0 controls access to the srmcfg CSR introduced by the Ssqosid  extension.

tags.name: norm:mhpmevent_inh_op
tags.text: Each of the five xINH bits, when set, inhibit counting of events while in privilege mode x. All-zeroes for these bits results in counting of events in all modes.

tags.name: norm:mhpmevent_of_op
tags.text:
The OF bit is set when the corresponding hpmcounter overflows, and remains set
until written by software.

tags.name: norm:hpmcounter_overflow
tags.text:
Since hpmcounter values are unsigned values,
overflow is defined as unsigned overflow of the implemented counter bits.

tags.name: norm:count_overflow_interrupt
tags.text: If an hpmcounter overflows while the associated OF bit is zero, then a "count overflow interrupt request" is generated. If the OF bit is one, then no interrupt request is generated. Consequently the OF bit also functions as a count overflow interrupt disable for the associated hpmcounter.

tags.name: norm:count_overflow_trigger
tags.text: Count overflow never results from writes to the mhpmcountern or mhpmeventn registers, only from hardware increments of counter registers.

tags.name: norm:mhpmevent_of_bit_set
tags.text:
Generation of a count-overflow-interrupt request by an hpmcounter sets the
associated OF bit.

tags.name: norm:LCOFIP_op
tags.text:
When an OF bit is set, it eventually, but not necessarily immediately, sets
the LCOFIP bit in the mip/sip registers.

tags.name: norm:scountovf_op
tags.text: This extension adds the scountovf CSR, a 32-bit read-only register that contains shadow copies of the OF bits in the 29 mhpmevent CSRs (mhpmevent3 - mhpmevent31) - where scountovf bit X corresponds to mhpmeventX.

tags.name: norm:scountovf_smode_read_access_control
tags.text:
Read access to bit X is subject to the same mcounteren (or mcounteren and
hcounteren) CSRs that mediate access to the hpmcounter CSRs by S-mode (or
VS-mode).

tags.name: norm:scountovf_mmode_read_access
tags.text: In M-mode, scountovf bit X is always readable.

tags.name: norm:scountovf_smode_read_access
tags.text:
In S/HS-mode, scountovf bit X is readable when mcounteren bit
X is set, and otherwise reads as zero.

tags.name: norm:scountovf_vsmode_read_access
tags.text:
Similarly, in VS mode, scountovf bit
X is readable when mcounteren bit X and hcounteren bit X are both set,
and otherwise reads as zero.

normative_rules.name: HS_mode_invoke_error
normative_rules.summary: HS-mode must invoke a critical error handler in a virtual machine on a double trap in VS-mode

tags.name: norm:HS_mode_invoke_error
tags.text:
It enables HS-mode to invoke a critical error
handler in a virtual machine on a double trap in VS-mode.

normative_rules.name: M_mode_invoke_error
normative_rules.summary: M-mode must invoke a critical error handler in the OS/Hypervisor on a double trap in S/HS-mode

tags.name: norm:M_mode_invoke_error
tags.text:
It also allows M-mode
to invoke a critical error handler in the OS/Hypervisor on a double trap in
S/HS-mode.

normative_rules.name: menvcfg_DTE
normative_rules.summary: The Ssdbltrp extension adds the menvcfg.DTE field

tags.name: norm:menvcfg_DTE
tags.text: The Ssdbltrp extension adds the menvcfg.DTE (See <<sec:menvcfg>>)

normative_rules.name: sstatus_SDT
normative_rules.summary: The Ssdbltrp extension adds the sstatus.SDT field

tags.name: norm:sstatus_SDT
tags.text:
and the
sstatus.SDT fields (See <<sstatus>>).

normative_rules.name: henvcfg_DTE
normative_rules.summary: If the hypervisor extension is implemented, Ssdbltrp adds the henvcfg.DTE field

normative_rules.name: sstc_purpose
normative_rules.chapter_name: Sstc Extension for Supervisor-mode Timer Interrupts

tags.name: norm:sstc_purpose
tags.text:
This extension serves to provide supervisor mode with its own CSR-based timer
interrupt facility that it can directly manage to provide its own timer service
(in the form of having its own stimecmp register) - thus eliminating the large
overheads for emulating S/HS-mode timers and timer interrupt generation up in
M-mode.

normative_rules.name: sstc_vs_facility
normative_rules.chapter_name: Sstc Extension for Supervisor-mode Timer Interrupts

tags.name: norm:sstc_vs_facility
tags.text:
Further, this extension adds a similar facility to the Hypervisor
extension for VS-mode.

tags.name: norm:sstatus
tags.text: The sstatus register is an SXLEN-bit read/write register formatted as shown in  when SXLEN=32 and  when SXLEN=64. The sstatus register keeps track of the processor’s current operating state.

tags.name: norm:sstatus

tags.name: norm:sstatus_spp
tags.text: The SPP bit indicates the privilege level at which a hart was executing before entering supervisor mode. When a trap is taken, SPP is set to 0 if the trap originated from user mode, or 1 otherwise. When an SRET instruction (see ) is executed to return from the trap handler, the privilege level is set to user mode if the SPP bit is 0, or supervisor mode if the SPP bit is 1; SPP is then set to 0.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_spp
tags.text: The SPP bit indicates the privilege level at which a hart was executing before entering supervisor mode. When a trap is taken, SPP is set to 0 if the trap originated from user mode, or 1 otherwise. When an SRET instruction (see ) is executed to return from the trap handler, the privilege level is set to user mode if the SPP bit is 0, or supervisor mode if the SPP bit is 1; SPP is then set to 0.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_spp
tags.text: The SPP bit indicates the privilege level at which a hart was executing before entering supervisor mode. When a trap is taken, SPP is set to 0 if the trap originated from user mode, or 1 otherwise. When an SRET instruction (see ) is executed to return from the trap handler, the privilege level is set to user mode if the SPP bit is 0, or supervisor mode if the SPP bit is 1; SPP is then set to 0.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_sie
tags.text: The SIE bit enables or disables all interrupts in supervisor mode. When SIE is clear, interrupts are not taken while in supervisor mode. When the hart is running in user-mode, the value in SIE is ignored, and supervisor-level interrupts are enabled. The supervisor can disable individual interrupt sources using the sie CSR.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_sie
tags.text: The SIE bit enables or disables all interrupts in supervisor mode. When SIE is clear, interrupts are not taken while in supervisor mode. When the hart is running in user-mode, the value in SIE is ignored, and supervisor-level interrupts are enabled. The supervisor can disable individual interrupt sources using the sie CSR.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_sie
tags.text: The SIE bit enables or disables all interrupts in supervisor mode. When SIE is clear, interrupts are not taken while in supervisor mode. When the hart is running in user-mode, the value in SIE is ignored, and supervisor-level interrupts are enabled. The supervisor can disable individual interrupt sources using the sie CSR.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_spie
tags.text: The SPIE bit indicates whether supervisor interrupts were enabled prior to trapping into supervisor mode. When a trap is taken into supervisor mode, SPIE is set to SIE, and SIE is set to 0. When an SRET instruction is executed, SIE is set to SPIE, then SPIE is set to 1.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_spie
tags.text: The SPIE bit indicates whether supervisor interrupts were enabled prior to trapping into supervisor mode. When a trap is taken into supervisor mode, SPIE is set to SIE, and SIE is set to 0. When an SRET instruction is executed, SIE is set to SPIE, then SPIE is set to 1.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_spie

tags.name: norm:sstatus_uxl
tags.text: The UXL field controls the value of XLEN for U-mode, termed UXLEN, which may differ from the value of XLEN for S-mode, termed SXLEN. The encoding of UXL is the same as that of the MXL field of misa, shown in .

tags.name: norm:sstatus_uxl
tags.text: The UXL field controls the value of XLEN for U-mode, termed UXLEN, which may differ from the value of XLEN for S-mode, termed SXLEN. The encoding of UXL is the same as that of the MXL field of misa, shown in .

tags.name: norm:sstatus_uxl_sz
tags.text: When SXLEN=32, the UXL field does not exist, and UXLEN=32. When SXLEN=64, it is a WARL field that encodes the current value of UXLEN. In particular, an implementation may make UXL be a read-only field whose value always ensures that UXLEN=SXLEN.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_uxl_sz
tags.text: When SXLEN=32, the UXL field does not exist, and UXLEN=32. When SXLEN=64, it is a WARL field that encodes the current value of UXLEN. In particular, an implementation may make UXL be a read-only field whose value always ensures that UXLEN=SXLEN.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_uxl_behavior
tags.text: If UXLEN≠SXLEN, instructions executed in the narrower mode must ignore source register operand bits above the configured XLEN, and must sign-extend results to fill the widest supported XLEN in the destination register.

tags.name: norm:hint_sxlen
tags.text: Some HINT instructions are encoded as integer computational instructions that overwrite their destination register with its current value, e.g., c.addi x8, 0. When such a HINT is executed with XLEN < SXLEN and bits SXLEN..XLEN of the destination register not all equal to bit XLEN-1, it is implementation-defined whether bits SXLEN..XLEN of the destination register are unchanged or are overwritten with copies of bit XLEN-1.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_mxr
tags.text: The MXR (Make eXecutable Readable) bit modifies the privilege with which loads access virtual memory. When MXR=0, only loads from pages marked readable (R=1 in ) will succeed. When MXR=1, loads from pages marked either readable or executable (R=1 or X=1) will succeed. MXR has no effect when page-based virtual memory is not in effect.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_mxr
tags.text: The MXR (Make eXecutable Readable) bit modifies the privilege with which loads access virtual memory. When MXR=0, only loads from pages marked readable (R=1 in ) will succeed. When MXR=1, loads from pages marked either readable or executable (R=1 or X=1) will succeed. MXR has no effect when page-based virtual memory is not in effect.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_mxr
tags.text: The MXR (Make eXecutable Readable) bit modifies the privilege with which loads access virtual memory. When MXR=0, only loads from pages marked readable (R=1 in ) will succeed. When MXR=1, loads from pages marked either readable or executable (R=1 or X=1) will succeed. MXR has no effect when page-based virtual memory is not in effect.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_sum
tags.text: The SUM (permit Supervisor User Memory access) bit modifies the privilege with which S-mode loads and stores access virtual memory. When SUM=0, S-mode memory accesses to pages that are accessible by U-mode (U=1 in ) will fault. When SUM=1, these accesses are permitted. SUM has no effect when page-based virtual memory is not in effect, nor when executing in U-mode. Note that S-mode can never execute instructions from user pages, regardless of the state of SUM.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_sum
tags.text: The SUM (permit Supervisor User Memory access) bit modifies the privilege with which S-mode loads and stores access virtual memory. When SUM=0, S-mode memory accesses to pages that are accessible by U-mode (U=1 in ) will fault. When SUM=1, these accesses are permitted. SUM has no effect when page-based virtual memory is not in effect, nor when executing in U-mode. Note that S-mode can never execute instructions from user pages, regardless of the state of SUM.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_sum
tags.text: The SUM (permit Supervisor User Memory access) bit modifies the privilege with which S-mode loads and stores access virtual memory. When SUM=0, S-mode memory accesses to pages that are accessible by U-mode (U=1 in ) will fault. When SUM=1, these accesses are permitted. SUM has no effect when page-based virtual memory is not in effect, nor when executing in U-mode. Note that S-mode can never execute instructions from user pages, regardless of the state of SUM.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_sum_satp_mode
tags.text: SUM is read-only 0 if satp.MODE is read-only 0.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_ube
tags.text: The UBE bit is a WARL field that controls the endianness of explicit memory accesses made from U-mode, which may differ from the endianness of memory accesses in S-mode. An implementation may make UBE be a read-only field that always specifies the same endianness as for S-mode.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_ube
tags.text: The UBE bit is a WARL field that controls the endianness of explicit memory accesses made from U-mode, which may differ from the endianness of memory accesses in S-mode. An implementation may make UBE be a read-only field that always specifies the same endianness as for S-mode.

tags.text: The UBE bit is a WARL field that controls the endianness of explicit memory accesses made from U-mode, which may differ from the endianness of memory accesses in S-mode. An implementation may make UBE be a read-only field that always specifies the same endianness as for S-mode.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_ube_implicit
tags.text: UBE has no effect on instruction fetches, which are implicit memory accesses that are always little-endian.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_sdt
tags.text: The S-mode-disable-trap (SDT) bit is a WARL field introduced by the Ssdbltrp extension to address double trap (See ) at privilege modes lower than M.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_sdt
tags.text: The S-mode-disable-trap (SDT) bit is a WARL field introduced by the Ssdbltrp extension to address double trap (See ) at privilege modes lower than M.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_sdt
tags.text: The S-mode-disable-trap (SDT) bit is a WARL field introduced by the Ssdbltrp extension to address double trap (See ) at privilege modes lower than M.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_sdt
tags.text: The S-mode-disable-trap (SDT) bit is a WARL field introduced by the Ssdbltrp extension to address double trap (See ) at privilege modes lower than M.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_sdt_sstatus_sie_overwrite
tags.text: When the SDT bit is set to 1 by an explicit CSR write, the SIE (Supervisor Interrupt Enable) bit is cleared to 0. This clearing occurs regardless of the value written, if any, to the SIE bit by the same write. The SIE bit can only be set to 1 by an explicit CSR write if the SDT bit is being set to 0 by the same write or is already 0.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_sdt_trap
tags.text: When a trap is to be taken into S-mode, if the SDT bit is currently 0, it is then set to 1, and the trap is delivered as expected. However, if SDT is already set to 1, then this is an unexpected trap. In the event of an unexpected trap, a double-trap exception trap is delivered into M-mode. To deliver this trap, the hart writes registers, except mcause and mtval2, with the same information that the unexpected trap would have written if it was taken into M-mode. The mtval2 register is then set to what would be otherwise written into the mcause register by the unexpected trap. The mcause register is set to 16, the double-trap exception code.

tags.name: norm:sstatus_sdt_sret
tags.text: An SRET instruction sets the SDT bit to 0.

tags.name: norm:stvec
tags.text: The stvec register is an SXLEN-bit read/write register that holds trap vector configuration, consisting of a vector base address (BASE) and a vector mode (MODE).

tags.name: norm:stvec
tags.text: The stvec register is an SXLEN-bit read/write register that holds trap vector configuration, consisting of a vector base address (BASE) and a vector mode (MODE).

tags.name: norm:stvec
tags.text: The stvec register is an SXLEN-bit read/write register that holds trap vector configuration, consisting of a vector base address (BASE) and a vector mode (MODE).

tags.name: norm:stvec_op
tags.text: The BASE field in stvec is a  field that can hold any valid virtual or physical address, subject to the following alignment constraints: the address must be 4-byte aligned, and MODE settings other than Direct might impose additional alignment constraints on the value in the BASE field.

tags.name: norm:stvec_sz_base
tags.text: Note that the CSR contains only bits XLEN-1 through 2 of the address BASE. When used as an address, the lower two bits are filled with zeroes to obtain an XLEN-bit address that is always aligned on a 4-byte boundary.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie
tags.text: The sip register is an SXLEN-bit read/write register containing information on pending interrupts, while sie is the corresponding SXLEN-bit read/write register containing interrupt enable bits. Interrupt cause number i (as reported in CSR scause, ) corresponds with bit i in both sip and sie. Bits 15:0 are allocated to standard interrupt causes only, while bits 16 and above are designated for platform use.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie
tags.text: The sip register is an SXLEN-bit read/write register containing information on pending interrupts, while sie is the corresponding SXLEN-bit read/write register containing interrupt enable bits. Interrupt cause number i (as reported in CSR scause, ) corresponds with bit i in both sip and sie. Bits 15:0 are allocated to standard interrupt causes only, while bits 16 and above are designated for platform use.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie
tags.text: The sip register is an SXLEN-bit read/write register containing information on pending interrupts, while sie is the corresponding SXLEN-bit read/write register containing interrupt enable bits. Interrupt cause number i (as reported in CSR scause, ) corresponds with bit i in both sip and sie. Bits 15:0 are allocated to standard interrupt causes only, while bits 16 and above are designated for platform use.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie
tags.text: The sip register is an SXLEN-bit read/write register containing information on pending interrupts, while sie is the corresponding SXLEN-bit read/write register containing interrupt enable bits. Interrupt cause number i (as reported in CSR scause, ) corresponds with bit i in both sip and sie. Bits 15:0 are allocated to standard interrupt causes only, while bits 16 and above are designated for platform use.

tags.name: norm:sie_sip_supervisor_strap
tags.text: An interrupt i will trap to S-mode if both of the following are true: (a) either the current privilege mode is S and the SIE bit in the sstatus register is set, or the current privilege mode has less privilege than S-mode; and (b) bit i is set in both sip and sie.

tags.name: norm:sie_sip_strap_time_constraint
tags.text: These conditions for an interrupt trap to occur must be evaluated in a bounded amount of time from when an interrupt becomes, or ceases to be, pending in sip, and must also be evaluated immediately following the execution of an SRET instruction or an explicit write to a CSR on which these interrupt trap conditions expressly depend (including sip, sie and sstatus).

tags.name: norm:s_interrupt_priority
tags.text: Interrupts to S-mode take priority over any interrupts to lower privilege modes.

tags.name: norm:sip_acc
tags.text:
Each individual bit in register sip may be writable or may be
read-only.

tags.name: norm:sip_op
tags.text:
When bit i in sip is writable, a pending interrupt i
can be cleared by writing 0 to this bit. If interrupt i can become
pending but bit i in sip is read-only, the implementation must
provide some other mechanism for clearing the pending interrupt (which
may involve a call to the execution environment).

tags.name: norm:sie_acc
tags.text:
A bit in sie must be writable if the corresponding interrupt can ever
become pending. Bits of sie that are not writable are read-only zero.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie_bits_sz
tags.text: The standard portions (bits 15:0) of registers sip and sie are formatted as shown in Figures  and  respectively.

tags.name: norm:sip_seip_sie_seie
tags.text: Bits sip.SEIP and sie.SEIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level external interrupts. If implemented, SEIP is read-only in sip, and is set and cleared by the execution environment, typically through a platform-specific interrupt controller.

tags.name: norm:sip_seip_sie_seie
tags.text: Bits sip.SEIP and sie.SEIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level external interrupts. If implemented, SEIP is read-only in sip, and is set and cleared by the execution environment, typically through a platform-specific interrupt controller.

tags.name: norm:sip_seip_sie_seie
tags.text: Bits sip.SEIP and sie.SEIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level external interrupts. If implemented, SEIP is read-only in sip, and is set and cleared by the execution environment, typically through a platform-specific interrupt controller.

tags.name: norm:sip_seip_sie_seie
tags.text: Bits sip.SEIP and sie.SEIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level external interrupts. If implemented, SEIP is read-only in sip, and is set and cleared by the execution environment, typically through a platform-specific interrupt controller.

tags.name: norm:sip_stip_sie_stie
tags.text: Bits sip.STIP and sie.STIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level timer interrupts. If implemented, STIP is read-only in sip. When the Sstc extension is not implemented, STIP is set and cleared by the execution environment. When the Sstc extension is implemented, STIP reflects the timer interrupt signal resulting from stimecmp. The sip.STIP bit, in response to timer interrupts generated by stimecmp, is set by writing stimecmp with a value that is less than or equal to time, and is cleared by writing stimecmp with a value greater than time.

tags.name: norm:mip_sip_stip_op
tags.text: A supervisor timer interrupt becomes pending, as reflected in the STIP bit in the mip and sip registers whenever time contains a value greater than or equal to stimecmp, treating the values as unsigned integers. If the result of this comparison changes, it is guaranteed to be reflected in STIP eventually, but not necessarily immediately. The interrupt remains posted until stimecmp becomes greater than time, typically as a result of writing stimecmp. The interrupt will be taken based on the standard interrupt enable and delegation rules.

tags.name: norm:sip_stip_sie_stie
tags.text: Bits sip.STIP and sie.STIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level timer interrupts. If implemented, STIP is read-only in sip. When the Sstc extension is not implemented, STIP is set and cleared by the execution environment. When the Sstc extension is implemented, STIP reflects the timer interrupt signal resulting from stimecmp. The sip.STIP bit, in response to timer interrupts generated by stimecmp, is set by writing stimecmp with a value that is less than or equal to time, and is cleared by writing stimecmp with a value greater than time.

tags.name: norm:mip_sip_stip_op
tags.text: A supervisor timer interrupt becomes pending, as reflected in the STIP bit in the mip and sip registers whenever time contains a value greater than or equal to stimecmp, treating the values as unsigned integers. If the result of this comparison changes, it is guaranteed to be reflected in STIP eventually, but not necessarily immediately. The interrupt remains posted until stimecmp becomes greater than time, typically as a result of writing stimecmp. The interrupt will be taken based on the standard interrupt enable and delegation rules.

tags.name: norm:sip_ssip_sie_ssie
tags.text: Bits sip.SSIP and sie.SSIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level software interrupts. If implemented, SSIP is writable in sip and may also be set to 1 by a platform-specific interrupt controller.

tags.name: norm:sip_ssip_sie_ssie
tags.text: Bits sip.SSIP and sie.SSIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level software interrupts. If implemented, SSIP is writable in sip and may also be set to 1 by a platform-specific interrupt controller.

tags.name: norm:sip_ssip_sie_ssie
tags.text: Bits sip.SSIP and sie.SSIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level software interrupts. If implemented, SSIP is writable in sip and may also be set to 1 by a platform-specific interrupt controller.

tags.name: norm:sip_ssip_sie_ssie
tags.text: Bits sip.SSIP and sie.SSIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level software interrupts. If implemented, SSIP is writable in sip and may also be set to 1 by a platform-specific interrupt controller.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie_Sscofpmf
tags.text: If the Sscofpmf extension is implemented, bits sip.LCOFIP and sie.LCOFIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for local-counter-overflow interrupts. LCOFIP is read-write in sip and reflects the occurrence of a local counter-overflow overflow interrupt request resulting from any of the mhpmeventn.OF bits being set. If the Sscofpmf extension is not implemented, sip.LCOFIP and sie.LCOFIE are read-only zeros.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie_Sscofpmf
tags.text: If the Sscofpmf extension is implemented, bits sip.LCOFIP and sie.LCOFIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for local-counter-overflow interrupts. LCOFIP is read-write in sip and reflects the occurrence of a local counter-overflow overflow interrupt request resulting from any of the mhpmeventn.OF bits being set. If the Sscofpmf extension is not implemented, sip.LCOFIP and sie.LCOFIE are read-only zeros.

If the Sscofpmf extension is not implemented, sip.LCOFIP and sie.LCOFIE are read-only zeros.

tags.text: If the Sscofpmf extension is implemented, bits sip.LCOFIP and sie.LCOFIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for local-counter-overflow interrupts. If the Sscofpmf extension is not implemented, sip.LCOFIP and sie.LCOFIE are read-only zeros.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie_unimpl
tags.text:
Each standard interrupt type (SEI, STI, SSI, or LCOFI) may not be implemented,
in which case the corresponding interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable
bits are read-only zeros.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie_unimpl
tags.text:
Each standard interrupt type (SEI, STI, SSI, or LCOFI) may not be implemented,
in which case the corresponding interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable
bits are read-only zeros.

All bits in sip and sie are WARL fields. The
implemented interrupts may be found by writing one to every bit location
