tags.name: norm:Svpbmt_aliasing_attribute
tags.text:
When Svpbmt is used with non-zero PBMT encodings, it is possible for
multiple virtual aliases of the same physical page to exist
simultaneously with different memory attributes. It is also possible for
a U-mode or S-mode mapping through a PTE with Svpbmt enabled to observe
different memory attributes for a given region of physical memory than a
concurrent access to the same page performed by M-mode or when
MODE=Bare. In such cases,
the behaviors dictated by the attributes
(including coherence, which is otherwise unaffected) may be violated.

tags.name: norm:Svpbmt_noncacheable_aliasing_no_coherence_loss
tags.text:
Accessing the same location using different attributes that are both
non-cacheable (e.g., NC and IO)
does not cause loss of coherence,

tags.name: norm:Svpbmt_noncacheable_aliasing_may_weaken_ordering
tags.text:
might result in weaker memory ordering than the stricter attribute
ordinarily guarantees.

tags.name: norm:Svpbmt_noncacheable_aliasing_fence_prevents_ordering_loss
tags.text:
fence iorw, iorw instruction
between such accesses suffices to prevent loss of memory ordering.

tags.name: norm:Svpbmt_cacheable_aliasing_may_cause_coherence_loss
tags.text:
may
cause loss of coherence.

tags.name: norm:Svpbmt_cacheable_aliasing_fence_flush_fence_required
tags.text: prevents both loss of coherence and loss of memory ordering:

tags.name: norm:Svpbmt_hgatp_stage_override_rule
tags.text:
if hgatp.MODE is not equal to zero, non-zero G-stage PTE PBMT bits
override the attributes in the PMA to produce an intermediate set of
attributes.

tags.name: norm:Svpbmt_vsatp_stage_override_rule
tags.text:
if vsatp.MODE is not equal to zero, non-zero VS-stage PTE PBMT
bits override the intermediate attributes to produce the final set of
attributes used by accesses to the page in question.

tags.name: norm:Svinval_split_fine_grained
tags.text:
that can be more efficiently batched or pipelined on certain classes of
high-performance implementation.

tags.name: norm:Svinval_sinval_vma_invalidates_same_as_sfence_vma
tags.text:
However, unlike SFENCE.VMA, SINVAL.VMA
instructions are only ordered with respect to SFENCE.VMA,
SFENCE.W.INVAL, and SFENCE.INVAL.IR instructions as defined below.

tags.name: norm:Svinval_sfence_w_inval_orders_before_sinval_vma
tags.text:
The SFENCE.INVAL.IR
instruction guarantees that any previous SINVAL.VMA instructions executed
by the current hart are ordered before subsequent implicit references by
that hart to the memory-management data structures.

tags.name: norm:Svinval_sequence_rs1_rs2
tags.text:
the values of rs1 and rs2 for the SFENCE.VMA are the same as those
used in the SINVAL.VMA,

tags.name: norm:Svinval_sequence_reads_writes_before
tags.text:
reads and writes prior to the SFENCE.W.INVAL are considered to be
those prior to the SFENCE.VMA,

tags.name: norm:Svinval_sequence_reads_writes_after
tags.text:
reads and writes following the SFENCE.INVAL.IR are considered to be
those subsequent to the SFENCE.VMA.

tags.name: norm:Svinval_hinval_vvma_gvma
tags.text:
These have the same semantics as SINVAL.VMA, except that they combine with
SFENCE.W.INVAL and SFENCE.INVAL.IR to replace HFENCE.VVMA and
HFENCE.GVMA, respectively, instead of SFENCE.VMA.

tags.name: norm:Svinval_hinval_gvma_uses_vmid
tags.text: HINVAL.GVMA uses VMIDs instead of ASIDs.

tags.name: norm:Svinval_illegal_instruction_u_mode
tags.text:
In particular, an attempt to execute any of
these instructions in U-mode always raises an illegal-instruction
exception.

tags.name: norm:Svinval_illegal_instruction_tvm
tags.text:
An attempt to execute SINVAL.VMA or HINVAL.GVMA in S-mode or HS-mode when
mstatus.TVM=1 also raises an illegal-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:Svinval_virtual_instruction_vu_vs
tags.text:
An attempt to execute HINVAL.VVMA or HINVAL.GVMA in VS-mode or VU-mode, or to
execute SINVAL.VMA in VU-mode, raises a virtual-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:Svinval_virtual_instruction_vtvms
tags.text:
When hstatus.VTVM=1, an attempt to execute SINVAL.VMA in VS-mode also raises
a virtual-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:Svinval_sfence_w_inval_inval_u_mode
tags.text: raises an illegal-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:Svinval_sfence_w_inval_inval_vu_mode
tags.text: Doing so in VU-mode raises a virtual-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:Svinval_sfence_w_inval_inval_s_vs_mode
tags.text:
SFENCE.W.INVAL and SFENCE.INVAL.IR are unaffected by the mstatus.TVM and
hstatus.VTVM fields and hence are always permitted in S-mode and VS-mode.

tags.name: norm:Svadu_hw_update_a_d_bits
tags.text: If the Svadu extension is implemented, the menvcfg.ADUE field is writable.

tags.name: norm:Svadu_hypervisor_adue_writable
tags.text:
If the hypervisor extension is additionally implemented, the henvcfg.ADUE
field is also writable.

tags.name: norm:Svadu_disabled_hw_update_falls_back_to_svade
tags.text:
When hardware updating of A/D bits is disabled, the Svade extension,
which mandates exceptions when A/D bits need be set, instead takes effect.

tags.name: norm:Svvptc_explicit_stores_update_valid_bit
tags.text:
When the Svvptc extension is implemented, explicit stores by a hart that update
the Valid bit of leaf and/or non-leaf PTEs from 0 to 1 and are visible to a hart
will eventually become visible within a bounded timeframe to subsequent implicit
accesses by that hart to such PTEs.

tags.name: norm:Svrsw60t59b_reserved_bits_60_59
tags.text:
If the Svrsw60t59b extension is implemented, then bits 60-59 of the page table
entries (PTEs) are reserved for use by supervisor software and are ignored by
the implementation.

tags.name: norm:Svrsw60t59b_h_g_stage_reserved_bits
tags.text:
If the Hypervisor (H) extension is also implemented, then bits 60-59 of the
G-stage PTEs are reserved for use by supervisor software and are ignored by
the implementation.

tags.name: norm:Svrsw60t59b_depends_on_sv39
tags.text: The Svrsw60t59b extension depends on Sv39.

tags.name: norm:Ssqosid_shared_resource_need_management
tags.text:
When multiple workloads execute concurrently on modern processors—equipped with
large core counts, multiple cache hierarchies, and multiple memory controllers—
the performance of any given workload becomes less deterministic, or even
non-deterministic, due to shared resource contention.

tags.name: norm:Ssqosid_hw_monitoring_required
tags.text:
To manage performance variability, system software needs resource allocation and
monitoring capabilities. These capabilities allow for the reservation of
resources like cache and bandwidth, thus meeting individual performance
targets
while minimizing interference.

tags.name: norm:Ssqosid_srmcfg_introduced
tags.text:
To facilitate this, the QoS Identifiers extension (Ssqosid) introduces the
srmcfg register, which configures a hart with two identifiers: a Resource
Control ID (RCID) and a Monitoring Counter ID (MCID). These identifiers
accompany each request issued by the hart to shared resource controllers.

tags.name: norm:Ssqosid_metadata_used_by_controllers
tags.text:
Additional metadata, like the nature of the memory access and the ID of the
originating supervisor domain, can accompany RCID and MCID. Resource
controllers may use this metadata for differentiated service such as a different
capacity allocation for code storage vs. data storage. Resource controllers can
use this data for security policies such as not exposing statistics of one
security domain to another.

tags.name: norm:Ssqosid_cbqri_spec
tags.text:
These identifiers are crucial for the RISC-V Capacity and Bandwidth Controller
QoS Register Interface (CBQRI) specification, which provides methods for
setting resource usage limits and monitoring resource consumption. The RCID
controls resource allocations, while the MCID is used for tracking resource
usage.

tags.name: norm:Ssqosid_srmcfg_register_size
tags.text:
The srmcfg register is an SXLEN-bit read/write register used to configure a
Resource Control ID (RCID) and a Monitoring Counter ID (MCID). Both RCID
and MCID are WARL fields.

tags.name: norm:Ssqosid_rcid_mcid_accompany_requests
tags.text:
The RCID and MCID accompany each request made by the hart to shared resource
controllers. The RCID is used to determine the resource allocations (e.g.,
cache occupancy limits, memory bandwidth limits, etc.) to enforce. The MCID
is used to identify a counter to monitor resource usage.

tags.name: norm:Ssqosid_srmcfg_csr_applies_to_all_modes
tags.text:
The RCID and MCID configured in the srmcfg CSR apply to all privilege
modes of software execution on that hart by default, but this behavior may be
overridden by future extensions.

tags.name: norm:Ssqosid_smstateen_srmcfg_requires_mstateen0
tags.text:
If extension Smstateen is implemented together with Ssqosid, then Ssqosid also
requires the SRMCFG bit in mstateen0 to be implemented.

tags.name: norm:Ssqosid_srmcfg_access_illegal_instruction
tags.text:
If mstateen0.SRMCFG is 0, attempts to access srmcfg in privilege modes
less privileged than M-mode raise an illegal-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:Ssqosid_srmcfg_access_virtual_instruction
tags.text:
If mstateen0.SRMCFG is 1 or if extension Smstateen is not implemented,
attempts to access srmcfg when V=1 raise a virtual-instruction exception.

normative_rules.name: stimecmp_stimecmph_sz_acc
normative_rules.summary: stimecmp is a 63-bit CSR with 64-bit precision; RV32 accesses split into stimecmp and stimecmph

tags.name: norm:stimecmp_stimecmph_sz_acc
tags.text:
The stimecmp CSR is a 64-bit register and
has 64-bit precision on all RV32 and RV64 systems.
In RV32 only, accesses to the stimecmp CSR access the low 32 bits,
while accesses to the stimecmph CSR access the high 32 bits of stimecmp.

normative_rules.name: mip_stip_op
normative_rules.summary: Supervisor timer interrupt becomes pending in mip/sip STIP when time >= stimecmp

tags.name: norm:mip_sip_stip_op
tags.text: A supervisor timer interrupt becomes pending, as reflected in the STIP bit in the mip and sip registers whenever time contains a value greater than or equal to stimecmp, treating the values as unsigned integers. If the result of this comparison changes, it is guaranteed to be reflected in STIP eventually, but not necessarily immediately. The interrupt remains posted until stimecmp becomes greater than time, typically as a result of writing stimecmp. The interrupt will be taken based on the standard interrupt enable and delegation rules.

tags.name: norm:senvcfg_cbze
tags.text: The Zicboz extension adds the CBZE (Cache Block Zero instruction enable) field to senvcfg. The CBZE field controls execution of the cache block zero instruction (CBO.ZERO) in U-mode. Execution of CBO.ZERO in U-mode is enabled only if execution of the instruction is enabled for use in S-mode and CBZE is set to 1; otherwise, an illegal-instruction exception is raised. When the Zicboz extension is not implemented, CBZE is read-only zero.

tags.name: norm:senvcfg_cbcfe
tags.text: The Zicbom extension adds the CBCFE (Cache Block Clean and Flush instruction Enable) field to senvcfg to control execution of the CBO.CLEAN and CBO.FLUSH instructions in U-mode. Execution of these instructions in U-mode is enabled only if execution of these instructions is enabled for use in S-mode and CBCFE is set to 1; otherwise, an illegal-instruction exception is raised. When the Zicbom extension is not implemented, CBCFE is read-only zero.

tags.name: norm:senvcfg_cbie
tags.text: The Zicbom extension adds the CBIE (Cache Block Invalidate instruction Enable) WARL field to senvcfg to control execution of the CBO.INVAL instruction in U-mode. The encoding 10b is reserved. When the Zicbom extension is not implemented, CBIE is read-only zero. Execution of CBO.INVAL in U-mode is enabled only if execution of the instruction is enabled for use in S-mode and CBIE is set to 01b or 11b; otherwise, an illegal-instruction exception is raised.

tags.name: norm:senvcfg_cbie
tags.text: The Zicbom extension adds the CBIE (Cache Block Invalidate instruction Enable) WARL field to senvcfg to control execution of the CBO.INVAL instruction in U-mode. The encoding 10b is reserved. When the Zicbom extension is not implemented, CBIE is read-only zero. Execution of CBO.INVAL in U-mode is enabled only if execution of the instruction is enabled for use in S-mode and CBIE is set to 01b or 11b; otherwise, an illegal-instruction exception is raised.

tags.name: norm:cbo-inval_s-mode_op0
tags.text:
If CBO.INVAL is enabled in S-mode to perform a flush operation, then when the
instruction is enabled in U-mode it performs a flush operation, even if CBIE
is set to 11b. Otherwise, the instruction behaves as follows, depending on the
CBIE encoding:

tags.name: norm:cfi_call_term
tags.text: The term call is used to refer to a JAL or JALR instruction with a link register as destination, i.e., rd≠x0. Conventionally, the link register is x1 or x5. A call using JAL or C.JAL is termed a direct call. A C.JALR expands to JALR x1, 0(rs1) and is a call. A call using JALR or C.JALR is termed an indirect-call.

tags.name: norm:cfi_return_term
tags.text: The term return is used to refer to a JALR instruction with rd=x0 and with rs1=x1 or rs1=x5. A C.JR instruction expands to JALR x0, 0(rs1) and is a return if rs1=x1 or rs1=x5.

tags.name: norm:cfi_indirect-jump_term
tags.text: The term indirect-jump is used to refer to a JALR instruction with rd=x0 and where the rs1 is not x1 or x5 (i.e., not a return). A C.JR instruction where rs1 is not x1 or x5 (i.e., not a return) is an indirect-jump.

tags.name: norm:zicflip_lpad_enc
tags.text:
The LPAD
instruction (See <<LP_INST>>) is encoded using the AUIPC major opcode with
rd=x0.

tags.name: norm:zicflip_lpad_imm_enc
tags.text:
The LPAD
instruction is encoded with a 20-bit immediate value called the landing-pad-label
(LPL) that is matched to the expected landing pad label. When LPL is encoded
as zero, the LPAD instruction does not perform the label check and in programs
built with this single label mode of operation the indirect call/jump sites do
not need to establish an expected landing pad label value in x7.

tags.name: norm:zicflip_lpad_expected
tags.text: The Zicfilp extension, when enabled, determines if an indirect call or an indirect jump must land on a landing pad, as specified in . If is_lp_expected is 1, then the hart updates the ELP to LP_EXPECTED.

tags.name: norm:zicflip_indirect_branch_lpad
tags.text: An indirect branch using JALR, C.JALR, or C.JR with rs1 as x7 is termed a software guarded branch. Such branches do not need to land on a LPAD instruction and thus do not set ELP to LP_EXPECTED.

tags.name: norm:zicflip_lpad_label
tags.text:
To support labeled landing pads, the
indirect call/jump sites establish an expected landing pad label (e.g., using
the LUI instruction) in the bits 31:12 of the x7 register.

tags.name: norm:zicflip_elp_lpad_expected
tags.text: When ELP is set to LP_EXPECTED, if the next instruction in the instruction stream is not 4-byte aligned, or is not LPAD, or if the landing pad label encoded in LPAD is not zero and does not match the expected landing pad label in bits 31:12 of the x7 register, then a software-check exception (cause=18) with xtval set to "landing pad fault (code=2)" is raised else the ELP is updated to NO_LP_EXPECTED.

tags.name: norm:zicflip_lpad_enabled_instr_allowed
tags.text:
When Zicfilp is enabled, LPAD is the only instruction allowed to execute when
the ELP state is LP_EXPECTED. If Zicfilp is not enabled then the instruction
is a no-op.

tags.name: norm:zicflip_lpad_enabled_exception
tags.text:
If Zicfilp is enabled, the LPAD instruction causes a
software-check exception with xtval set to "landing pad fault (code=2)"

tags.name: norm:zicflip_lpad_alignment_exception
tags.text: The pc is not 4-byte aligned and ELP is LP_EXPECTED.

tags.name: norm:zicflip_lpad_label_exception
tags.text: The ELP is LP_EXPECTED and the LPL is not zero and the LPL does not match the expected landing pad label in bits 31:12 of the x7 register.

tags.name: norm:zicflip_lpad_no_sw_exception
tags.text: If a software-check exception is not caused then the ELP is updated to NO_LP_EXPECTED.

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_link_reg_func_enter
tags.text:
When Zicfiss is enabled, each function that needs to spill the link register,
typically non-leaf functions, store the link register value to the regular stack
and a shadow copy of the link register value to the shadow stack when the
function is entered (the prologue).

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_link_reg_func_return
tags.text:
When such a function returns (the
epilogue), the function loads the link register from the regular stack and
the shadow copy of the link register from the shadow stack.

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_stack_compare
tags.text:
Then, the link
register value from the regular stack and the shadow link register value from
the shadow stack are compared. A mismatch of the two values is indicative of a
subversion of the return address control variable and causes a software-check
exception.

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_enc
tags.text:
The Zicfiss instructions, except SSAMOSWAP.W/D, are encoded using a subset of
May-Be-Operation instructions defined by the Zimop and Zcmop extensions.

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_sspush_enc
tags.text: SSPUSH x1 and SSPUSH x5 - encoded using MOP.RR.7

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_c-sspush_enc
tags.text: C.SSPUSH x1 - encoded using C.MOP.1

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_sspopchk_enc
tags.text: SSPOPCHK x1 and SSPOPCHK x5 - encoded using MOP.R.28

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_c-sspopchk_enc
tags.text: C.SSPOPCHK x5 - encoded using C.MOP.5

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_ssrdp_enc
tags.text: SSRDP - encoded using MOP.R.28

tags.name: norm:zicfiss_atomic_swap
tags.text: Perform an atomic swap from a shadow stack location (See <<SSAMOSWAP>>)

tags.name: norm:ssp_op
tags.text:
The ssp CSR is an unprivileged read-write (URW) CSR that reads and writes
XLEN low order bits of the shadow stack pointer (ssp).

tags.name: norm:ssp_addr
tags.text:
The CSR address is
0x011.

tags.name: norm:ssp_sz
tags.text:
There is no high CSR defined as the ssp is always as wide as the XLEN
of the current privilege mode.

tags.name: norm:ssp-field_1_0
tags.text:
The bits 1:0 of ssp are read-only zero. If the
UXLEN or SXLEN may never be 32, then the bit 2 is also read-only zero.

tags.name: norm:ss_push
tags.text: A shadow stack push operation is defined as decrement of the ssp by XLEN/8 followed by a store of the value in the link register to memory at the new top of the shadow stack.

tags.name: norm:sspush_c-sspush_instr
tags.text: The SSPUSH instruction and its compressed form C.SSPUSH can be used to push a link register on the shadow stack. The SSPUSH and C.SSPUSH instructions perform a store identically to the existing store instructions, with the difference that the base is implicitly ssp and the width is implicitly XLEN.

tags.name: norm:sspush_c-sspush_decrement
tags.text: The ssp is decremented by SSPUSH and C.SSPUSH only if the store to the shadow stack completes successfully.

tags.name: norm:ss_pop
tags.text: A shadow stack pop operation is defined as an XLEN wide read from the current top of the shadow stack followed by an increment of the ssp by XLEN/8.

tags.name: norm:sspopchk_shadow_return_pop
tags.text: The SSPOPCHK instruction, and its compressed form C.SSPOPCHK, can be used to pop the shadow return address value from the shadow stack and check that the value matches the contents of the link register, and if not cause a software-check exception with xtval set to "shadow stack fault (code=3)".

tags.name: norm:sspopchk-c_load_op
tags.text: The C.SSPOPCHK, and SSPOPCHK instructions perform a load identically to the existing load instructions, with the difference that the base is implicitly ssp and the width is implicitly XLEN.

tags.name: norm:sspop_exception
tags.text: If the value loaded from the address in ssp does not match the value in rs1, a software-check exception (cause=18) is raised with xtval set to "shadow stack fault (code=3)". The software-check exception caused by SSPOPCHK/ C.SSPOPCHK is lower in priority than a load/store/AMO access-fault exception.

tags.name: norm:sspop_increment
tags.text: The ssp is incremented by SSPOPCHK and C.SSPOPCHK only if the load from the shadow stack completes successfully and no software-check exception is raised.

tags.name: norm:ssrdp_read
tags.text: The SSRDP instruction is provided to move the contents of ssp to a destination register.

tags.name: norm:rv32_ssamoswap-w_op
tags.text: For RV32, SSAMOSWAP.W atomically loads a 32-bit data value from address of a shadow stack location in rs1, puts the loaded value into register rd, and stores the 32-bit value held in rs2 to the original address in rs1. SSAMOSWAP.D (RV64 only) is similar to SSAMOSWAP.W but operates on 64-bit data values.

tags.name: norm:ssamoswap_address
tags.text: Just as for AMOs in the A extension, SSAMOSWAP.W/D requires that the address held in rs1 be naturally aligned to the size of the operand (i.e., eight-byte aligned for doublewords, and four-byte aligned for words). The same exception options apply if the address is not naturally aligned.

tags.name: norm:rv64_ssamoswap-w_op
tags.text: For RV64, SSAMOSWAP.W atomically loads a 32-bit data value from address of a shadow stack location in rs1, sign-extends the loaded value and puts it in rd, and stores the lower 32 bits of the value held in rs2 to the original address in rs1.

tags.name: norm:ssamoswap_address
tags.text: Just as for AMOs in the A extension, SSAMOSWAP.W/D requires that the address held in rs1 be naturally aligned to the size of the operand (i.e., eight-byte aligned for doublewords, and four-byte aligned for words). The same exception options apply if the address is not naturally aligned.

tags.name: norm:elen
tags.text:
The maximum size in bits of a vector element that any operation can produce or consume, ELEN {ge} 8, which
must be a power of 2.

tags.name: norm:vlen
tags.text: The number of bits in a single vector register, VLEN {ge} ELEN, which must be a power of 2, and must be no greater than 2^16^.

tags.name: norm:vill_implicit_encoding
tags.text:
A small implementation supporting ELEN=32 requires only seven bits of state in vtype: two bits for ma and ta, two bits for vsew[1:0] and three bits for vlmul[2:0].  The illegal value represented by vill can be internally encoded using the illegal 64-bit combination in vsew[1:0] without requiring an additional storage
bit to hold vill.

tags.name: norm:hw_mstatus_vs_dirty_update
tags.text:
Implementations may also change mstatus.VS or vsstatus.VS from Initial or
Clean to Dirty at any time, even when there is no change in vector state.

tags.name: norm:reserved_vill_set
tags.text: Implementations may set vill in either case.

tags.name: norm:vreg_count
tags.text: The vector extension adds 32 architectural vector registers, v0-v31 to the base scalar RISC-V ISA.

tags.name: norm:mstatus_vs_op_initial_clean
tags.text: When mstatus.VS is set to Initial or Clean, executing any instruction that changes vector state, including the vector CSRs, will change mstatus.VS to Dirty. Implementations may also change mstatus.VS from Initial or Clean to Dirty at any time, even when there is no change in vector state.

tags.name: norm:vsstatus_vs_mstatus_vs_op_off
tags.text: When V=1, both vsstatus.VS and mstatus.VS are in effect: attempts to execute any vector instruction, or to access the vector CSRs, raise an illegal-instruction exception when either field is set to Off.

tags.name: norm:vsstatus_vs_sz_acc
tags.text: When the hypervisor extension is present, a vector context status field, VS, is added to vsstatus[10:9]. It is defined analogously to the floating-point context status field, FS.

tags.name: norm:vsstatus_vs_sz_acc
tags.text: When the hypervisor extension is present, a vector context status field, VS, is added to vsstatus[10:9]. It is defined analogously to the floating-point context status field, FS.

tags.name: norm:mstatus_vs_op_off
tags.text: Attempts to execute any vector instruction, or to access the vector CSRs, raise an illegal-instruction exception when mstatus.VS is set to Off.

tags.name: norm:vsstatus_vs_mstatus_vs_op_active
tags.text:
When V=1 and neither vsstatus.VS nor mstatus.VS is set to Off, executing
any instruction that changes vector state, including the vector CSRs, will
change both mstatus.VS and vsstatus.VS to Dirty.

tags.name: norm:mstatus_vs_sstatus_vs_op
tags.text: A vector context status field, VS, is added to mstatus[10:9] and shadowed in sstatus[10:9].  It is defined analogously to the floating-point context status field, FS.

tags.name: norm:vsstatus_vs_op
tags.text: Similarly, when V=1, both vsstatus.VS and the HS-level sstatus.VS are in effect. Attempts to execute a vector instruction when either field is 0 (Off) raise an illegal-instruction exception. Modifying the vector state when V=1 causes both fields to be set to 3 (Dirty).

tags.name: norm:vsstatus_vs_mstatus_vs_op_off
tags.text: When V=1, both vsstatus.VS and mstatus.VS are in effect: attempts to execute any vector instruction, or to access the vector CSRs, raise an illegal-instruction exception when either field is set to Off.

tags.name: norm:vsstatus_vs_mstatus_vs_op_active
tags.text:
When V=1 and neither vsstatus.VS nor mstatus.VS is set to Off, executing
any instruction that changes vector state, including the vector CSRs, will
change both mstatus.VS and vsstatus.VS to Dirty.

tags.name: norm:vsstatus_sd_op_vs
tags.text: If vsstatus.VS is Dirty, vsstatus.SD is 1; otherwise, vsstatus.SD is set in accordance with existing specifications.

tags.name: norm:mstatus_sd_op_vs
tags.text: If mstatus.VS is Dirty, mstatus.SD is 1; otherwise, mstatus.SD is set in accordance with existing specifications.

tags.name: norm:mutable_misa_v
tags.text: Implementations may have a writable misa.V field.

tags.name: norm:mstatus_vs_exists
tags.text:
Analogous to the
way in which the floating-point unit is handled, the mstatus.VS
field may exist even if misa.V is clear.

tags.name: norm:vsstatus_vs_exists
tags.text: For implementations with a writable misa.V field, the vsstatus.VS field may exist even if misa.V is clear.

tags.name: norm:vtype_sz_acc_op
tags.text:
The read-only XLEN-wide vector type CSR, vtype provides the
default type used to interpret the contents of the vector register
file, and can only be updated by vset{i}vl{i} instructions.

tags.name: norm:vtype_sz_acc_op
tags.text:
The read-only XLEN-wide vector type CSR, vtype provides the
default type used to interpret the contents of the vector register
file, and can only be updated by vset{i}vl{i} instructions.

tags.name: norm:vtype_fields_sz
tags.text: The vtype register has five fields, vill, vma, vta, vsew[2:0], and vlmul[2:0].  Bits vtype[XLEN-2:8] should be written with zero, and non-zero values in this field are reserved.

tags.name: norm:vtype_fields_sz
tags.text: The vtype register has five fields, vill, vma, vta, vsew[2:0], and vlmul[2:0].  Bits vtype[XLEN-2:8] should be written with zero, and non-zero values in this field are reserved.

tags.name: norm:vtype_fields_sz
tags.text: The vtype register has five fields, vill, vma, vta, vsew[2:0], and vlmul[2:0].  Bits vtype[XLEN-2:8] should be written with zero, and non-zero values in this field are reserved.

tags.name: norm:vtype_fields_sz
tags.text: The vtype register has five fields, vill, vma, vta, vsew[2:0], and vlmul[2:0].  Bits vtype[XLEN-2:8] should be written with zero, and non-zero values in this field are reserved.

tags.name: norm:vtype_fields_sz
tags.text: The vtype register has five fields, vill, vma, vta, vsew[2:0], and vlmul[2:0].  Bits vtype[XLEN-2:8] should be written with zero, and non-zero values in this field are reserved.

tags.name: norm:vtype_vsew_op
tags.text: The value in vsew sets the dynamic selected element width (SEW).  By default, a vector register is viewed as being divided into VLEN/SEW elements.

tags.name: norm:vtype_vsew_rsv
tags.text:
While it is anticipated the larger vsew[2:0] encodings
(100-111) will be used to encode larger SEW, the encodings are
formally reserved at this point.

tags.name: norm:vtype_lmul_val
tags.text:
Implementations must support LMUL integer values of
1, 2, 4, and 8.

tags.name: norm:vtype_lmul_fval
tags.text: Implementations must provide fractional LMUL settings that allow the narrowest supported type to occupy a fraction of a vector register corresponding to the ratio of the narrowest supported type’s width to that of the largest supported type’s width.  In general, the requirement is to support LMUL ≥ SEWMIN/ELEN, where SEWMIN is the narrowest supported SEW value and ELEN is the widest supported SEW value.  In the standard extensions, SEWMIN=8.  For standard vector extensions with ELEN=32, fractional LMULs of 1/2 and 1/4 must be supported.  For standard vector extensions with ELEN=64, fractional LMULs of 1/2, 1/4, and 1/8 must be supported.

tags.name: norm:vtype_sew_val
tags.text: For a given supported fractional LMUL setting, implementations must support SEW settings between SEWMIN and LMUL * ELEN, inclusive.

tags.name: norm:vtype_lmul_fval_rsv
tags.text: The use of vtype encodings with LMUL < SEWMIN/ELEN is reserved, but implementations can set vill if they do not support these configurations.

tags.name: norm:lmul
tags.text: LMUL is set by the signed vlmul field in vtype (i.e., LMUL = 2vlmul[2:0]).

tags.name: norm:vlmax
tags.text: The derived value VLMAX = LMUL*VLEN/SEW represents the maximum number of elements that can be operated on with a single vector instruction given the current SEW and LMUL settings as shown in the table below.

tags.name: norm:vreg_offgroup_lmul2_rsv
tags.text:
Instructions specifying an LMUL=2 vector register group
with an odd-numbered vector register are reserved.

tags.name: norm:vreg_offgroup_lmul4_rsv
tags.text:
instructions specifying an LMUL=4 vector register group using vector
register numbers that are not multiples of four are reserved.

tags.name: norm:vreg_offgroup_lmul8_rsv
tags.text:
instructions specifying an LMUL=8 vector register group
using register numbers that are not multiples of eight are reserved.

tags.name: norm:vreg_mask_lmul_indp
tags.text: Mask registers are always contained in a single vector register, regardless of LMUL.

tags.name: norm:vtype_vta-vma_op
tags.text: These two bits modify the behavior of destination tail elements and destination inactive masked-off elements respectively during the execution of vector instructions.  The tail and inactive sets contain element positions that are not receiving new results during a vector operation, as defined in .

tags.name: norm:vtype_vta-vma_op
tags.text: These two bits modify the behavior of destination tail elements and destination inactive masked-off elements respectively during the execution of vector instructions.  The tail and inactive sets contain element positions that are not receiving new results during a vector operation, as defined in .

tags.name: norm:vtype_vta-vma_val
tags.text: All systems must support all four options:

tags.name: norm:vtype_vta-vma_val
tags.text: All systems must support all four options:

tags.name: norm:vreg_mask_tail_agn
tags.text: Mask destination tail elements are always treated as tail-agnostic, regardless of the setting of vta.

tags.name: norm:vreg_mask_op
tags.text: When a set is marked undisturbed, the corresponding set of destination elements in a vector register group retain the value they previously held.

tags.name: norm:vreg_agnostic_op
tags.text: When a set is marked agnostic, the corresponding set of destination elements in any vector destination operand can either retain the value they previously held, or are overwritten with 1s.  Within a single vector instruction, each destination element can be either left undisturbed or overwritten with 1s, in any combination, and the pattern of undisturbed or overwritten with 1s is not required to be deterministic when the instruction is executed with the same inputs.

tags.name: norm:vreg_mask_tail_op
tags.text: In addition, except for mask load instructions, any element in the tail of a mask result can also be written with the value the mask-producing operation would have calculated with vl=VLMAX. Furthermore, for mask-logical instructions and vmsbf.m, vmsif.m, vmsof.m mask-manipulation instructions, any element in the tail of the result can be written with the value the mask-producing operation would have calculated with vl=VLEN, SEW=8, and LMUL=8 (i.e., all bits of the mask register can be overwritten).

tags.name: norm:vtype_vill_op
tags.text: If the vill bit is set, then any attempt to execute a vector instruction that depends upon vtype will raise an illegal-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:vl_acc
tags.text: The XLEN-bit-wide read-only vl CSR can only be updated by the vset{i}vl{i} instructions, and the fault-only-first vector load instruction variants.

tags.name: norm:vl_op
tags.text: The vl register holds an unsigned integer specifying the number of elements to be updated with results from a vector instruction, as further detailed in .

tags.name: norm:vlenb_acc_op
tags.text: The XLEN-bit-wide read-only CSR vlenb holds the value VLEN/8, i.e., the vector register length in bytes.

tags.name: norm:vlenb_acc_op
tags.text: The XLEN-bit-wide read-only CSR vlenb holds the value VLEN/8, i.e., the vector register length in bytes.

tags.name: norm:vstart_acc_sz
tags.text: The XLEN-bit-wide read-write vstart CSR specifies the index of the first element to be executed by a vector instruction, as described in .

tags.name: norm:vstart_acc_sz
tags.text: The XLEN-bit-wide read-write vstart CSR specifies the index of the first element to be executed by a vector instruction, as described in .

tags.name: norm:vstart_op
tags.text:
All vector instructions are defined to begin execution with the
element number given in the vstart CSR, leaving earlier elements in
the destination vector undisturbed

tags.name: norm:vstart_update
tags.text:
reset the vstart CSR to
zero at the end of execution.

tags.name: norm:vstart_unmodified
tags.text: vstart is not modified by vector instructions that raise illegal-instruction exceptions.

tags.name: norm:vstart_sz_writable
tags.text: The vstart CSR is defined to have only enough writable bits to hold the largest element index (one less than the maximum VLMAX).

tags.name: norm:vstart_val_rsv
tags.text: The use of vstart values greater than the largest element index for the current vtype setting is reserved.

tags.name: norm:vstart_vtype_dep
tags.text: Implementations are permitted to raise illegal-instruction exceptions when attempting to execute a vector instruction with a value of vstart that the implementation can never produce when executing that same instruction with the same vtype setting.

tags.name: norm:vxrm_val_sz_acc
tags.text: The vector fixed-point rounding-mode register holds a two-bit read-write rounding-mode field in the least-significant bits (vxrm[1:0]).  The upper bits, vxrm[XLEN-1:2], should be written as zeros.

tags.name: norm:vxrm_val_sz_acc
tags.text: The vector fixed-point rounding-mode register holds a two-bit read-write rounding-mode field in the least-significant bits (vxrm[1:0]).  The upper bits, vxrm[XLEN-1:2], should be written as zeros.

tags.name: norm:vxrm_val_sz_acc
tags.text: The vector fixed-point rounding-mode register holds a two-bit read-write rounding-mode field in the least-significant bits (vxrm[1:0]).  The upper bits, vxrm[XLEN-1:2], should be written as zeros.

tags.name: norm:vcsr_vxrm_op
tags.text: The vector fixed-point rounding-mode is given a separate CSR address to allow independent access, but is also reflected as a field in vcsr.

tags.name: norm:vxrm_op
tags.text: The fixed-point rounding algorithm is specified as follows. Suppose the pre-rounding result is v, and d bits of that result are to be rounded off. Then the rounded result is (v >> d) + r, where r depends on the rounding mode as specified in the following table.

tags.name: norm:vxsat_op_acc_sz
tags.text: The vxsat CSR has a single read-write least-significant bit (vxsat[0]) that indicates if a fixed-point instruction has had to saturate an output value to fit into a destination format. Bits vxsat[XLEN-1:1] should be written as zeros.

tags.name: norm:vxsat_op_acc_sz
tags.text: The vxsat CSR has a single read-write least-significant bit (vxsat[0]) that indicates if a fixed-point instruction has had to saturate an output value to fit into a destination format. Bits vxsat[XLEN-1:1] should be written as zeros.

tags.name: norm:vxsat_op_acc_sz
tags.text: The vxsat CSR has a single read-write least-significant bit (vxsat[0]) that indicates if a fixed-point instruction has had to saturate an output value to fit into a destination format. Bits vxsat[XLEN-1:1] should be written as zeros.

tags.name: norm:vcsr_vxsat_op
tags.text: The vxsat bit is mirrored in vcsr.

tags.name: norm:vcsr_vxrm-vxsat_acc
tags.text: The vxrm and vxsat separate CSRs can also be accessed via fields in the XLEN-bit-wide vector control and status CSR, vcsr.

tags.name: norm:vcsr_vxrm-vxsat_acc
tags.text: The vxrm and vxsat separate CSRs can also be accessed via fields in the XLEN-bit-wide vector control and status CSR, vcsr.

tags.name: norm:vreg_lmul1_op
tags.text: When LMUL=1, elements are simply packed in order from the least-significant to most-significant bits of the vector register.

tags.name: norm:vreg_flmul_op
tags.text: When LMUL < 1, only the first LMUL*VLEN/SEW elements in the vector register are used.  The remaining space in the vector register is treated as part of the tail, and hence must obey the vta setting.

tags.name: norm:vreg_lmulge2_op
tags.text: When vector registers are grouped, the elements of the vector register group are packed contiguously in element order beginning with the lowest-numbered vector register and moving to the next-highest-numbered vector register in the group once each vector register is filled.

tags.name: norm:vreg_mask_vtype_indp
tags.text: A vector mask occupies only one vector register regardless of SEW and LMUL.

tags.name: norm:vreg_mask_sz
tags.text: Each element is allocated a single mask bit in a mask vector register. The mask bit for element i is located in bit i of the mask register, independent of SEW or LMUL.

tags.name: norm:vreg_scalar_lmul_indp
tags.text:
Any vector register can be used to hold a scalar regardless
of the current LMUL setting.

tags.name: norm:eew_emul
tags.text: Each vector operand has an effective element width (EEW) and an effective LMUL (EMUL) that is used to determine the size and location of all the elements within a vector register group.  By default, for most operands of most instructions, EEW=SEW and EMUL=LMUL.

tags.name: norm:eew_emul
tags.text: Each vector operand has an effective element width (EEW) and an effective LMUL (EMUL) that is used to determine the size and location of all the elements within a vector register group.  By default, for most operands of most instructions, EEW=SEW and EMUL=LMUL.

tags.name: norm:eew_emul_sew_lmul_dep
tags.text: EEW/EMUL = SEW/LMUL.

tags.name: norm:eew_emul_sew_lmul_dep
tags.text: EEW/EMUL = SEW/LMUL.

tags.name: norm:vnarrowing_eew_emul
tags.text:
Narrowing instructions have a source operand that has
EEW=2*SEW and EMUL=2*LMUL but with a destination where EEW=SEW and EMUL=LMUL.

tags.name: norm:vnarrowing_eew_emul
tags.text:
Narrowing instructions have a source operand that has
EEW=2*SEW and EMUL=2*LMUL but with a destination where EEW=SEW and EMUL=LMUL.

tags.name: norm:emul_offgroup_rsv
tags.text:
Using other than the lowest-numbered
vector register to specify a vector register group is a reserved
encoding.

tags.name: norm:vreg_source_eew_rsv
tags.text: A vector register cannot be used to provide source operands with more than one EEW for a single instruction.  A mask register source is considered to have EEW=1 for this constraint.  An encoding that would result in the same vector register being read with two or more different EEWs, including when the vector register appears at different positions within two or more vector register groups, is reserved.

tags.name: norm:vreg_overlap_legal
tags.text: A destination vector register group can overlap a source vector register group only if one of the following holds:

tags.name: norm:vreg_mask_overlap
tags.text: For the purpose of determining register group overlap constraints, mask elements have EEW=1.

tags.name: norm:vreg_overlap_rsv
tags.text: Any instruction encoding that violates the overlap constraints is reserved.

tags.name: norm:vreg_overlap_agn
tags.text: When source and destination registers overlap and have different EEW, the instruction is mask- and tail-agnostic, regardless of the setting of the vta and vma bits in vtype.

tags.name: norm:emul_rsv
tags.text: The largest vector register group used by an instruction can not be greater than 8 vector registers (i.e., EMUL≤8), and if a vector instruction would require greater than 8 vector registers in a group, the instruction encoding is reserved.  For example, a widening operation that produces a widened vector register group result when LMUL=8 is reserved as this would imply a result EMUL=16.

tags.name: norm:vreg_scalar_emul
tags.text: Widened scalar values, e.g., input and output to a widening reduction operation, are held in the first element of a vector register and have EMUL=1.

tags.name: norm:vmask_inactive_op
tags.text:
Element operations
that are masked off (inactive) never generate exceptions.

tags.name: norm:vmask_agn_op
tags.text:
The
destination vector register elements corresponding to masked-off
elements are handled with either a mask-undisturbed or mask-agnostic
policy depending on the setting of the vma bit in vtype

tags.name: norm:vmask_vm_enc
tags.text: Where available, masking is encoded in a single-bit vm field in the  instruction (inst[25]).

tags.name: norm:vreg_vmask
tags.text: The mask value used to control execution of a masked vector instruction is always supplied by vector register v0.

tags.name: norm:vreg_vmask_rsv
tags.text: The destination vector register group for a masked vector instruction cannot overlap the source mask register (v0), unless the destination vector register is being written with a mask value (e.g., compares) or the scalar result of a reduction.  These instruction encodings are reserved.

tags.name: norm:vstart_vl_dep
tags.text: When vstart ≥ vl, there are no body elements, and no elements are updated in any destination vector register group, including that no tail elements are updated with agnostic values.

tags.name: norm:vstart_vl_scalar_indp
tags.text: Instructions that write an x register or f register do so even when vstart ≥ vl, including when vl=0.

tags.name: norm:vector_prestart_element
tags.text: The prestart elements do not raise exceptions and do not update the destination vector register.

tags.name: norm:vector_inactive_element
tags.text: The inactive elements do not raise exceptions and do not update any destination vector register group unless masked agnostic is specified (vtype.vma=1), in which case inactive elements may be overwritten with 1s.

tags.name: norm:vector_tail_element
tags.text: The tail elements do not raise exceptions, and do not update any destination vector register group unless tail agnostic is specified (vtype.vta=1), in which case tail elements may be overwritten with 1s, or with the result of the instruction in the case of mask-producing instructions except for mask loads.  When LMUL < 1, the tail includes the elements past VLMAX that are held in the same vector register.

tags.name: norm:vset_op
tags.text:
The
vset{i}vl{i} instructions set the vtype and vl CSRs based on
their arguments, and write the new value of vl into rd.

tags.name: norm:vtype_vstart_op
tags.text: the vl register is also set to zero.

tags.name: norm:vtype_acc
tags.text: The new vtype value is encoded in the immediate fields of vsetvli and vsetivli, and in the rs2 register for vsetvl.

tags.name: norm:vtype_sz_acc_op
tags.text:
The read-only XLEN-wide vector type CSR, vtype provides the
default type used to interpret the contents of the vector register
file, and can only be updated by vset{i}vl{i} instructions.

tags.name: norm:vtype_vill_val
tags.text:
If the vtype value is not supported by the implementation, then
the vill bit is set in vtype, the remaining bits in vtype are
set to zero

tags.name: norm:vtype_vill_val_vill
tags.text: A vtype value with vill set is treated as an unsupported configuration.

tags.name: norm:vtype_vill_all_bits
tags.text: Implementations must consider all bits of the vtype value to determine if the configuration is supported.  An unsupported value in any location within the vtype value must result in vill being set.

tags.name: norm:vsetvl_op
tags.text: When rs1 is not x0, the AVL is an unsigned integer held in the x register specified by rs1, and the new vl value is also written to the x register specified by rd.

tags.name: norm:vsetvl_op_rs1_x0_rd_nx0
tags.text: When rs1=x0 but rd≠x0, the maximum unsigned integer value (~0) is used as the AVL, and the resulting VLMAX is written to vl and also to the x register specified by rd.

tags.name: norm:vsetvl_op_rs1_x0_rd_x0
tags.text: When rs1=x0 and rd=x0, the instructions operate as if the current vector length in vl is used as the AVL, and the resulting value is written to vl, but not to a destination register.  This form can only be used when VLMAX and hence vl is not actually changed by the new SEW/LMUL ratio.

tags.name: norm:vtype_vset_rsv
tags.text: Use of the instructions with a new SEW/LMUL ratio that would result in a change of VLMAX is reserved. Use of the instructions is also reserved if vill was 1 beforehand.

tags.name: norm:vsetivli_op
tags.text: For the vsetivli instruction, the AVL is encoded as a 5-bit zero-extended immediate (0—​31) in the rs1 field.

tags.name: norm:vl_val_lead-in
tags.text: The vset{i}vl{i} instructions first set VLMAX according to their vtype argument, then set vl obeying the following constraints:

tags.name: norm:vl_val_list
tags.text:
vl = AVL if AVL ≤ VLMAX
ceil(AVL / 2) ≤ vl ≤ VLMAX if AVL < (2 * VLMAX)
vl = VLMAX if AVL ≥ (2 * VLMAX)
Deterministic on any given implementation for same input AVL and VLMAX values
These specific properties follow from the prior rules:

vl = 0 if  AVL = 0
vl > 0 if AVL > 0
vl ≤ VLMAX
vl ≤ AVL
a value read from vl when used as the AVL argument to vset{i}vl{i} results in the same value in vl, provided the resultant VLMAX equals the value of VLMAX at the time that vl was read

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_vstart
tags.text: All vector loads and stores may generate and accept a non-zero vstart value.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_base_stride_regtype
tags.text:
Vector load/store base
registers and strides are taken from the GPR x registers.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_base
tags.text: The base effective address for all vector accesses is given by the contents of the x register named in rs1.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_unit_stride_op
tags.text: Vector unit-stride operations access elements stored contiguously in memory starting from the base effective address.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_constant_stride_op
tags.text: Vector constant-stride operations access the first memory element at the base effective address, and then access subsequent elements at address increments given by the byte offset contained in the x register specified by rs2.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_strided_eew
tags.text: Vector memory unit-stride and constant-stride operations directly encode EEW of the data to be transferred statically in the instruction to reduce the number of vtype changes when accessing memory in a mixed-width routine.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_unit_stride_vd_vs3
tags.text: For loads, the vd register will hold the first field loaded from the segment.  For stores, the vs3 register is read to provide the first field to be stored to each segment.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_scalar_missaligned_independence
tags.text: Support for misaligned vector memory accesses is independent of an implementation’s support for misaligned scalar memory accesses.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_scalar_missaligned_dependence
tags.text: Vector misaligned memory accesses follow the same rules for atomicity as scalar misaligned memory accesses.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_indexed_unordered
tags.text: However, even for the ordered form, accesses to the fields within an individual segment are not ordered with respect to each other.

tags.name: norm:vector_ff_op
tags.text: If element 0 raises an exception, vl is not modified, and the trap is taken.  If an element > 0 raises an exception, the corresponding trap is not taken, and the vector length vl is reduced to the index of the element that would have raised an exception.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_indexed_op
tags.text: Vector indexed operations add the contents of each element of the vector offset operand specified by vs2 to the base effective address to give the effective address of each element.  The data vector register group has EEW=SEW, EMUL=LMUL, while the offset vector register group has EEW encoded in the instruction and EMUL=(EEW/SEW)*LMUL.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_indexed_eew
tags.text: Indexed operations use the explicit EEW encoding in the instruction to set the size of the indices used, and use SEW/LMUL to specify the data width.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_ins_req
tags.text: Implementations must provide vector loads and stores with EEWs corresponding to all supported SEW settings.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_ins_rsv
tags.text: Vector load/store encodings for unsupported EEW widths are reserved.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_bytewise
tags.text: The vector offset operand is treated as a vector of byte-address offsets.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_xlen_dep
tags.text: If the vector offset elements are narrower than XLEN, they are zero-extended to XLEN before adding to the base effective address.  If the vector offset elements are wider than XLEN, the least-significant XLEN bits are used in the address calculation.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_eew_rsv
tags.text: If the implementation does not support the EEW of the offset elements, the instruction is reserved.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_stride_ordered_op
tags.text: Vector unit-stride and constant-stride memory accesses do not guarantee ordering between individual element accesses.  The vector indexed load and store memory operations have two forms, ordered and unordered.  The indexed-ordered variants preserve element ordering on memory accesses.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_stride_unordered_op
tags.text: For unordered instructions (mop[1:0]!=11) there is no guarantee on element access order.  If the accesses are to a strongly ordered IO region, the element accesses can be initiated in any order.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_stride_unordered_precise
tags.text: For implementations with precise vector traps, exceptions on indexed-unordered stores must also be precise.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_nf_op
tags.text: The nf[2:0] field encodes the number of fields in each segment.  For regular vector loads and stores, nf=0, indicating that a single value is moved between a vector register group and memory at each element position.  Larger values in the nf field are used to access multiple contiguous fields within a segment as described below in .

tags.name: norm:vector_wholels_nf_op
tags.text: The nf[2:0] field also encodes the number of whole vector registers to transfer for the whole vector register load/store instructions.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_eew_emul
tags.text:
Vector loads and stores have an EEW encoded directly in the
instruction.  The corresponding EMUL is calculated as EMUL =
(EEW/SEW)*LMUL.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_eew_emul
tags.text:
Vector loads and stores have an EEW encoded directly in the
instruction.  The corresponding EMUL is calculated as EMUL =
(EEW/SEW)*LMUL.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_emul_rsv
tags.text:
If the EMUL would be out of range (EMUL>8 or
EMUL<1/8), the instruction encoding is reserved.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_emul_offgroup_rsv
tags.text:
The vector register
groups must have legal register specifiers for the selected EMUL,
otherwise the instruction encoding is reserved.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_indexed_eew_emul
tags.text: Vector unit-stride and constant-stride use the EEW/EMUL encoded in the instruction for the data values, while vector indexed loads and stores use the EEW/EMUL encoded in the instruction for the index values and the SEW/LMUL encoded in vtype for the data values.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_mew_rsv
tags.text: The mew bit (inst[28]) when set is expected to be used to encode expanded memory sizes of 128 bits and above, but these encodings are currently reserved.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_unit_stride_mask
tags.text: Additional unit-stride mask load and store instructions are provided to transfer mask values to/from memory.  These operate similarly to unmasked byte loads or stores (EEW=8), except that the effective vector length is evl=ceil(vl/8) (i.e. EMUL=1), and the destination register is always written with a tail-agnostic policy.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_neg_zero_stride
tags.text: Negative and zero strides are supported.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_neg_zero_stride
tags.text: Negative and zero strides are supported.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_constant_stride_unordered
tags.text: Element accesses within a constant-stride instruction are unordered with respect to each other.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_constant_stride_x0
tags.text: When rs2=x0, then an implementation is allowed, but not required, to perform fewer memory operations than the number of active elements, and may perform different numbers of memory operations across different dynamic executions of the same static instruction.

tags.name: norm:vector_ff_trigger
tags.text:
The unit-stride fault-only-first load instructions are used to
vectorize loops with data-dependent exit conditions ("while" loops).
These instructions execute as a regular load except that they will
only take a trap caused by a synchronous exception on element 0.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_overwrite_past_trap
tags.text:
Load instructions may overwrite active destination vector register
group elements past the element index at which the trap is reported.

tags.name: norm:vector_ff_past_trap
tags.text: Similarly, fault-only-first load instructions may update active destination elements past the element that causes trimming of the vector length (but not past the original vector length).  The values of these spurious updates do not have to correspond to the values in memory at the addressed memory locations.  Non-idempotent memory locations can only be accessed when it is known the corresponding element load operation will not be restarted due to a trap or vector-length trimming.

tags.name: norm:vector_ff_no_exception
tags.text: Even when an exception is not raised, implementations are permitted to process fewer than vl elements and reduce vl accordingly, but if vstart=0 and vl>0, then at least one element must be processed.

tags.name: norm:vector_ff_interrupt_behavior
tags.text: When the fault-only-first instruction takes a trap due to an interrupt, implementations should not reduce vl and should instead set a vstart value.

tags.name: norm:nfields
tags.text: The three-bit nf field in the vector instruction encoding is an unsigned integer that contains one less than the number of fields per segment, NFIELDS.

tags.name: norm:nfields_op
tags.text: Each field will be held in successively numbered vector register groups.  When EMUL>1, each field will occupy a vector register group held in multiple successively numbered vector registers, and the vector register group for each field must follow the usual vector register alignment constraints (e.g., when EMUL=2 and NFIELDS=4, each field’s vector register group must start at an even vector register, but does not have to start at a multiple of 8 vector register number).

tags.name: norm:emul_nfields_rsv
tags.text: The EMUL setting must be such that EMUL * NFIELDS ≤ 8, otherwise the instruction encoding is reserved.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_rsv
tags.text: If the vector register numbers accessed by the segment load or store would increment past 31, then the instruction encoding is reserved.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_op
tags.text: The vl register gives the number of segments to move, which is equal to the number of elements transferred to each vector register group.  Masking is also applied at the level of whole segments.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_unordered
tags.text: For segment loads and stores, the individual memory accesses used to access fields within each segment are unordered with respect to each other even for ordered indexed segment loads and stores.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_vstart_dep
tags.text: The vstart value is in units of whole segments.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_partial_access
tags.text:
If a trap occurs during
access to a segment, it is implementation-defined whether a subset
of the faulting segment's accesses are performed before the trap is taken.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_unit_stride_op
tags.text: The vector unit-stride load and store segment instructions move packed contiguous segments into multiple destination vector register groups.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls__seg_ff_unit-stride_op
tags.text: For fault-only-first segment loads, if an exception is detected partway through accessing the zeroth segment, the trap is taken. If an exception is detected partway through accessing a subsequent segment, vl is reduced to the index of that segment.

tags.name: norm:vector_ff_seg_partial_access
tags.text: In both cases, it is implementation-defined whether a subset of the segment is loaded.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_ff_overload
tags.text: These instructions may overwrite destination vector register group elements past the point at which a trap is reported or past the point at which vector length is trimmed.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_constant_stride_op
tags.text: Vector constant-stride segment loads and stores move contiguous segments where each segment is separated by the byte-stride offset given in the rs2 GPR argument.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_constant_stride_unordered
tags.text: Accesses to the fields within each segment can occur in any order, including the case where the byte stride is such that segments overlap in memory.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_indexed_op
tags.text: Vector indexed segment loads and stores move contiguous segments where each segment is located at an address given by adding the scalar base address in the rs1 field to byte offsets in vector register vs2.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_indexed_eew_emul_op
tags.text: The data vector register group has EEW=SEW, EMUL=LMUL, while the index vector register group has EEW encoded in the instruction with EMUL=(EEW/SEW)*LMUL.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_indexed_eew_emul_op
tags.text: The data vector register group has EEW=SEW, EMUL=LMUL, while the index vector register group has EEW encoded in the instruction with EMUL=(EEW/SEW)*LMUL.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_indexed_emul_nfields_val
tags.text: The EMUL * NFIELDS ≤ 8 constraint applies to the data vector register group.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_indexed_vreg_rsv
tags.text: For vector indexed segment loads, the destination vector register groups cannot overlap the source vector register group (specified by vs2), else the instruction encoding is reserved.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_wholereg_eew
tags.text: The load instructions have an EEW encoded in the mew and width fields following the pattern of regular unit-stride loads.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_wholereg_op
tags.text:
NFIELDS
indicates the number of vector registers to transfer, numbered
successively after the base.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_wholereg_nf_rsv
tags.text:
Only NFIELDS values of 1, 2, 4, 8 are
supported, with other values reserved.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_wholereg_op_cont
tags.text:
When multiple registers are
transferred, the lowest-numbered vector register is held in the
lowest-numbered memory addresses and successive vector register
numbers are placed contiguously in memory.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_seg_wholereg_evl
tags.text: The instructions operate with an effective vector length, evl=NFIELDS*VLEN/EEW, regardless of current settings in vtype and vl.  The usual property that no elements are written if vstart ≥ vl does not apply to these instructions. Similarly, the property that the instructions are reserved if vstart exceeds the largest element index for the current vtype setting does not apply. Instead, the instructions are reserved if vstart ≥ evl.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_wholereg_missaligned_exception
tags.text: Implementations are allowed to raise a misaligned address exception on whole register loads and stores if the base address is not naturally aligned to the larger of the size of the encoded EEW in bytes (EEW/8) or the implementation’s smallest supported SEW size in bytes (SEWMIN/8).

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_missaligned_exception
tags.text: If an element accessed by a vector memory instruction is not naturally aligned to the size of the element, either the element is transferred successfully or an address-misaligned exception is raised on that element.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_program_order
tags.text: Vector memory instructions appear to execute in program order on the local hart.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_rvwmo
tags.text: Vector memory instructions follow RVWMO at the instruction level.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_indexed_ordered_ordered
tags.text: Except for vector indexed-ordered loads and stores, element operations are unordered within the instruction.

tags.name: norm:vector_ls_indexed_ordered_rvwmo
tags.text: Vector indexed-ordered loads and stores read and write elements from/to memory in element order respectively, obeying RVWMO at the element level.

tags.name: norm:vector_load_store_semantics
tags.text:
Vector loads and stores move values between vector registers and
memory.

tags.name: norm:vector_masked_memory_access
tags.text:
Vector loads and stores can be masked, and they only access memory or raise
exceptions for active elements.

tags.name: norm:vector_masked_inactive_behavior
tags.text:
Masked vector loads do not update inactive elements in the destination vector
register group, unless masked agnostic is specified (vtype.vma=1).

tags.name: norm:vl_control_dependency
tags.text: Instructions affected by the vector length register vl have a control dependency on vl, rather than a data dependency.

tags.name: norm:vmask_control_dependency
tags.text: Similarly, masked vector instructions have a control dependency on the source mask register, rather than a data dependency.

tags.name: norm:V_fp_frm
tags.text: All vector floating-point operations use the dynamic rounding mode in the frm register.

tags.name: norm:V_inv_frm_rsv
tags.text:
Use of the frm field
when it contains an invalid rounding mode by any vector floating-point
instruction--even those that do not depend on the rounding mode, or
when vl=0, or when vstart {ge} vl--is reserved.

tags.name: norm:vop-vv_vreg_vs2_vs1
tags.text: Vector-vector operations take two vectors of operands from vector register groups specified by vs2 and vs1 respectively.

tags.name: norm:vop-vx_vop-vi_vreg_vs2
tags.text: the vector register group operand is specified by vs2.

tags.name: norm:vop-vx_vop-vi_vreg_vs2
tags.text: the vector register group operand is specified by vs2.

tags.name: norm:vop-vi_imm_5bit
tags.text:
For integer operations, the scalar can be a 5-bit immediate, imm[4:0], encoded
in the rs1 field.  The value is sign-extended to SEW bits, unless otherwise specified.

tags.name: norm:vop-vx_xreg_rs1
tags.text:
For integer operations, the scalar can be taken from the scalar x
register specified by rs1.

tags.name: norm:vop-vx_rs1_trunc_lsb_sewbits
tags.text: If XLEN>SEW, the least-significant SEW bits of the x register are used, unless otherwise specified.

tags.name: norm:vop-vx_rs1_sext_sewbits
tags.text:
If XLEN<SEW, the value from the x register is sign-extended to SEW
bits.

tags.name: norm:vfop_freg
tags.text:
For floating-point operations, the scalar can be taken from a scalar
f register.

tags.name: norm:vfop_freg_NaNbox_lsb_sewbits
tags.text:
If FLEN > SEW, the value in the f registers is
checked for a valid NaN-boxed value, in which case the
least-significant SEW bits of the f register are used, else the
canonical NaN value is used.

tags.name: norm:V_fp_eew_rsv
tags.text:
Vector instructions where any
floating-point vector operand's EEW is not a supported floating-point
type width (which includes when FLEN < SEW) are reserved.

tags.name: norm:V_Zinx_fp_scalar
tags.text:
When adding a vector extension to the Zfinx/Zdinx/Zhinx
extensions, floating-point scalar arguments are taken from the x registers.
NaN-boxing is not supported in these extensions, and so operands narrower
than XLEN bits are not checked for a NaN box; bits XLEN-1:EEW are ignored.
For RV32_Zdinx, EEW=64 scalar arguments are supplied by an x-register pair.

tags.name: norm:V_masked
tags.text:
Vector arithmetic instructions are masked under control of the vm
field.

tags.name: norm:vwop_vd_eew_emul
tags.text:
A few vector arithmetic instructions are defined to be widening
operations where the destination vector register group has EEW=2*SEW
and EMUL=2*LMUL.

tags.name: norm:vnop_vd_vs2_eew_emul
tags.text:
A few instructions are provided to convert double-width source vectors
into single-width destination vectors.  These instructions convert a
vector register group specified by vs2 with EEW/EMUL=2*SEW/2*LMUL to a vector register
group with the current SEW/LMUL setting.

tags.name: norm:vnop_vd_vs2_eew_emul
tags.text:
A few instructions are provided to convert double-width source vectors
into single-width destination vectors.  These instructions convert a
vector register group specified by vs2 with EEW/EMUL=2*SEW/2*LMUL to a vector register
group with the current SEW/LMUL setting.

tags.name: norm:vnop_vs1_eew_emul
tags.text:
Where there is a second
source vector register group (specified by vs1), this has the same
(narrower) width as the result (i.e., EEW=SEW).

instructions are also provided for the vector-scalar forms.

tags.text:

tags.name: norm:vsext_vzext_op
tags.text:

tags.name: norm:vsll_vsrl_vsra_op
tags.text:
A full set of vector shift instructions are provided, including
logical shift left (sll), and logical (zero-extending srl) and
arithmetic (sign-extending sra) shift right.  The data to be shifted
is in the vector register group specified by vs2 and the shift
amount value can come from a vector register group vs1, a scalar
integer register rs1, or a zero-extended 5-bit immediate.

tags.name: norm:vnsrl_vnsra_op

The narrowing right shifts extract a smaller field from a wider
operand and have both zero-extending (srl) and sign-extending
(sra) forms.  The shift amount can come from a vector register
group, or a scalar x register, or a zero-extended 5-bit immediate.

tags.name: norm:vnsrl_vnsra_op

operand and have both zero-extending (srl) and sign-extending
(sra) forms.  The shift amount can come from a vector register

used (e.g., the low 6 bits for a SEW=64-bit to SEW=32-bit narrowing

tags.name: norm:vmul_vmulh_vmulhu_vmulhsu_op
tags.text:
The single-width multiply instructions perform a SEW-bit*SEW-bit
multiply to generate a 2*SEW-bit product, then return one half of the
product in the SEW-bit-wide destination.  The mul versions write
the low word of the product to the destination register, while the
mulh versions write the high word of the product to the
destination register.

tags.name: norm:vmul_vmulh_vmulhu_vmulhsu_op

product in the SEW-bit-wide destination.  The mul versions write
the low word of the product to the destination register, while the
mulh versions write the high word of the product to the
destination register.

tags.name: norm:vmacc_vnmsac_vmadd_vnmsub_op
tags.text:
The integer multiply-add instructions are destructive and are provided
in two forms, one that overwrites the addend or minuend
(vmacc, vnmsac) and one that overwrites the first multiplicand
(vmadd, vnmsub).

tags.name: norm:vmacc_vnmsac_vmadd_vnmsub_op_lowhalf
tags.text: The low half of the product is added or subtracted from the third operand.

tags.name: norm:vmerge_op
tags.text:
The vector integer merge instructions combine two source operands
based on a mask.

tags.name: norm:vmerge_op_mask
tags.text:
The vmerge instructions are encoded as masked instructions (vm=0).
The instructions combine two
sources as follows.  At elements where the mask value is zero, the
first operand is copied to the destination element, otherwise the
second operand is copied to the destination element.  The first
operand is always a vector register group specified by vs2.  The
second operand is a vector register group specified by vs1 or a
scalar x register specified by rs1 or a 5-bit sign-extended
immediate.

tags.name: norm:vmerge_all_elem
tags.text:
Unlike regular arithmetic instructions, the
merge operates on all body elements (i.e., the set of elements from
vstart up to the current vector length in vl).

tags.name: norm:vmv_op
tags.text:
The vector integer move instructions copy a source operand to a vector
register group.
The vmv.v.v variant copies a vector register group, whereas the vmv.v.x
and vmv.v.i variants splat a scalar register or immediate to all active
elements of the destination vector register group.

tags.name: norm:vmv_vs2_nv0_rsv
tags.text:
The first operand specifier (vs2) must contain v0, and any other vector
register number in vs2 is reserved.

tags.name: norm:vsaddu_vsadd_vssubu_vssub_op
tags.text:

signed and unsigned integers.

tags.text:

destination, the result is replaced with the closest representable
value, and the vxsat bit is set.

tags.name: norm:vaaddu_vaadd_vasubu_vasub_op
tags.text:
The averaging add and subtract instructions right shift the result by
one bit and round off the result according to the setting in vxrm.
Computation is performed in infinite precision before rounding and truncating.

tags.name: norm:vasub_vasubu_op_overflow
tags.text: For vasub and vasubu, overflow is ignored and the result wraps around.

tags.name: norm:vaaddu_vaadd_vasubu_vasub_op
tags.text:
The averaging add and subtract instructions right shift the result by
one bit and round off the result according to the setting in vxrm.
Computation is performed in infinite precision before rounding and truncating.

tags.name: norm:vasub_vasubu_op_overflow
tags.text: For vasub and vasubu, overflow is ignored and the result wraps around.

one bit and round off the result according to the setting in vxrm.

tags.name: norm:vasub_vasubu_op_overflow
tags.text: For vasub and vasubu, overflow is ignored and the result wraps around.

tags.name: norm:vaaddu_vaadd_vasubu_vasub_op
tags.text:
The averaging add and subtract instructions right shift the result by
one bit and round off the result according to the setting in vxrm.
Computation is performed in infinite precision before rounding and truncating.

tags.name: norm:vasub_vasubu_op_overflow
tags.text: For vasub and vasubu, overflow is ignored and the result wraps around.

tags.name: norm:vsmul_op
tags.text:
The signed fractional multiply instruction produces a 2*SEW product of
the two SEW inputs, then shifts the result right by SEW-1 bits,
rounding these bits according to vxrm, then saturates the result to
fit into SEW bits.

tags.name: norm:vxsat_op_vsmul
tags.text:
If the result causes saturation, the vxsat bit
is set.

tags.name: norm:vxsat_op_vsmul
tags.text:
If the result causes saturation, the vxsat bit
is set.

tags.name: norm:vssrl_vssra_op
tags.text:
These instructions shift the input value right, and round off the
shifted out bits according to vxrm.  The scaling right shifts have
both zero-extending (vssrl) and sign-extending (vssra) forms.  The
data to be shifted is in the vector register group specified by vs2
and the shift amount value can come from a vector register group
vs1, a scalar integer register rs1, or a zero-extended 5-bit
immediate.

tags.name: norm:vssrl_vssra_op
tags.text:
These instructions shift the input value right, and round off the
shifted out bits according to vxrm.  The scaling right shifts have
both zero-extending (vssrl) and sign-extending (vssra) forms.  The
data to be shifted is in the vector register group specified by vs2
and the shift amount value can come from a vector register group
vs1, a scalar integer register rs1, or a zero-extended 5-bit
immediate.

tags.name: norm:vssrl_vssra_shamt
tags.text: Only the low lg2(SEW) bits of the shift-amount value are used to control the shift amount.

tags.name: norm:vssrl_vssra_shamt
tags.text: Only the low lg2(SEW) bits of the shift-amount value are used to control the shift amount.

tags.name: norm:vnclipu_vnclip_op
tags.text:
The vnclip instructions are used to pack a fixed-point value into a
narrower destination.  The instructions support rounding, scaling, and
saturation into the final destination format.  The source data is in
the vector register group specified by vs2. The scaling shift amount
value can come from a vector register group vs1, a scalar integer
register rs1, or a zero-extended 5-bit immediate.

tags.name: norm:vnclipu_vnclip_op
tags.text:
The vnclip instructions are used to pack a fixed-point value into a
narrower destination.  The instructions support rounding, scaling, and
saturation into the final destination format.  The source data is in
the vector register group specified by vs2. The scaling shift amount
value can come from a vector register group vs1, a scalar integer
register rs1, or a zero-extended 5-bit immediate.

tags.name: norm:vnclipu_vnclip_shamt
tags.text:
The low
lg2(2*SEW) bits of the vector or scalar shift-amount value (e.g., the
low 6 bits for a SEW=64-bit to SEW=32-bit narrowing operation) are
used to control the right shift amount, which provides the scaling.

tags.name: norm:vnclipu_vnclip_shamt
tags.text:
The low
lg2(2*SEW) bits of the vector or scalar shift-amount value (e.g., the
low 6 bits for a SEW=64-bit to SEW=32-bit narrowing operation) are
used to control the right shift amount, which provides the scaling.

tags.name: norm:vnclipu_overflow
tags.text:
For vnclipu, the shifted rounded source value is treated as an
unsigned integer and saturates if the result would overflow the
destination viewed as an unsigned integer.

tags.name: norm:vnclip_overflow
tags.text:
For vnclip, the shifted rounded source value is treated as a signed
integer and saturates if the result would overflow the destination viewed
as a signed integer.

tags.name: norm:vxsat_op_vnclip_u
tags.text:
If any destination element is saturated, the vxsat bit is set in the
vxsat register.

tags.name: norm:V_fp_EEW_IEEE_nsupported_rsv
tags.text:
If the EEW of a vector
floating-point operand does not correspond to a supported IEEE
floating-point type, the instruction encoding is reserved.

tags.name: norm:mstatus_FS_off_V_fp_ill
tags.text:
If the floating-point unit status field mstatus.FS is Off then any
attempt to execute a vector floating-point instruction will raise an
illegal-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:mstatus_FS_dirty_V_fp
tags.text:
Any vector floating-point instruction
that modifies any floating-point extension state (i.e., floating-point
CSRs or f registers) must set mstatus.FS to Dirty.

tags.name: norm:vsstatus_mstatus_FS_off_hypervisor_V_fp_ill
tags.text:
If the hypervisor extension is implemented and V=1, the vsstatus.FS field is
additionally in effect for vector floating-point instructions.  If
vsstatus.FS or mstatus.FS is Off then any
attempt to execute a vector floating-point instruction will raise an
illegal-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:vsstatus_mstatus_FS_off_hypervisor_V_fp_ill
tags.text:
If the hypervisor extension is implemented and V=1, the vsstatus.FS field is
additionally in effect for vector floating-point instructions.  If
vsstatus.FS or mstatus.FS is Off then any
attempt to execute a vector floating-point instruction will raise an
illegal-instruction exception.

tags.name: norm:vsstatus_mstatus_FS_dirty_hypervisor_V_fp
tags.text:
Any vector floating-point instruction
that modifies any floating-point extension state (i.e., floating-point
CSRs or f registers) must set both mstatus.FS and vsstatus.FS to Dirty.

tags.name: norm:vsstatus_mstatus_FS_dirty_hypervisor_V_fp
tags.text:
Any vector floating-point instruction
that modifies any floating-point extension state (i.e., floating-point
CSRs or f registers) must set both mstatus.FS and vsstatus.FS to Dirty.

tags.name: norm:fflags_op_V_fp
tags.text:
A vector floating-point exception at any active floating-point element
sets the standard FP exception flags in the fflags register.  Inactive
elements do not set FP exception flags.
